> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**
>
> - **Actual P-V diagram = indicator diagram** = experimentally traced plot of $P$ vs $V$ for a real engine cycle.
> - **Six major reasons for deviation** from ideal cycle: progressive combustion, heat loss, dissociation, variable specific heats, early exhaust opening, residual gases.
> - **Combustion spike is rounded** (not sharp) in SI engines — caused by **finite flame propagation time**.
> - **Combustion plateau** (extended pressure rise) is characteristic of CI engines — caused by **mixing-controlled combustion**.
> - **IMEP formula:** $IMEP = \dfrac{A_{loop} \times K_P}{\text{Base length}}$ [bar].
> - **IP formula:** $IP = \dfrac{IMEP \times L \times A_p \times N \times K}{60 \times 10^5 \times n_r}$ [kW] (4-stroke → $n_r = 2$).
> - **FP = IP − BP** and **$\eta_{mech} = BP/IP$**.
> - **Otto efficiency:** $\eta_{otto} = 1 - 1/r^{\gamma-1}$ (ideal SI).
> - **Diesel efficiency:** $\eta_{diesel} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \dfrac{r_c^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma(r_c - 1)}$ (ideal CI).
> - **Mechanical indicator** components: drum, pressure element, spring, pencil, paper.
> - **Electronic indicator** uses piezoelectric / strain-gauge transducers + encoder + DAQ.
> - **Knock detection**: pressure wiggles on the indicator diagram near TDC.
> - **Indicator diagram area** is in **bar·m³ (or bar·mm³)** — convert carefully using $1$ bar·m³ $= 10^5$ J.
> - **Always** state the units at every step; KTU examiners award partial marks for unit consistency.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Test on 4-Stroke Diesel Engine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGL508 - HEAT ENGINES LABORATORY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Performance Test on 4-Stroke Diesel Engine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 : Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Performance Test on a 4-Stroke Diesel Engine** is a standard experimental procedure carried out on an IC engine test rig to evaluate the engine's operational efficiency, fuel economy, and power delivery characteristics under varying load conditions. The test quantifies key performance parameters — **Brake Power (BP)**, **Indicated Power (IP)**, **Frictional Power (FP)**, **Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC)**, **Brake Thermal Efficiency (η_bt)**, **Indicated Thermal Efficiency (η_it)**, and **Mechanical Efficiency (η_mech)** — by systematically loading the engine using a dynamometer and recording steady-state readings for speed, fuel consumption, air consumption, and load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus (PCAGL508 - Module 2):** Students are expected to record experimental observations on a multi-cylinder/single-cylinder 4-stroke diesel engine test rig, calculate all standard performance parameters, plot characteristic curves, and interpret the results in the context of a real diesel engine. The experiment is a **direct application** of the thermodynamic principles covered in the Heat Engines theory modules.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English Explanation)

Think of a 4-stroke diesel engine like a **delivery truck on a long highway**. The performance test is like a **full health checkup at a service station**:

- The **engine** is the truck's heart — we need to know how hard it can work.
- The **dynamometer (rope brake / hydraulic / eddy current)** acts like a **resistance paddle in a river** — it loads the engine so we can measure how much useful work the engine can deliver.
- The **fuel measuring burette** is the **fuel pump meter** — it tells us exactly how much diesel the engine drinks.
- The **tachometer** is the **speedometer** — it tells us how fast the crankshaft rotates (RPM).
- The **manometer + orifice meter** measures the **air the engine breathes**.

By loading the engine step-by-step (no load → 25% → 50% → 75% → 100% load) and recording readings at each step, we draw **performance curves** — similar to plotting a **fitness report card** showing how the engine's efficiency and fuel economy change as it is asked to do more work.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Test Reference:** The procedure broadly follows the **IS 10000 / ISO 3046** standards for reciprocating IC engine performance testing, adapted to the KTU laboratory environment.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following metrics are **mandatory** for a KTU laboratory record:

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Calorific Value of Diesel | **CV** | **42,000 kJ/kg** (standard) |
| Atmospheric Pressure | $P_{atm}$ | **1.01325 bar** = 101.325 kPa |
| Density of Diesel | $\rho_f$ | **~0.78 kg/L** (typical, 0.86 for HSD) |
| Density of Water | $\rho_w$ | **1000 kg/m³** |
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| 1 Hectare head of water | $h$ | variable (m) |

> [!TIP]
> **Always state the calorific value used at the top of your calculation sheet.** A mismatch between the assumed CV and the actual fuel CV is one of the most common sources of error in KTU valuation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 : Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle of a 4-Stroke Diesel Engine

A 4-stroke diesel engine completes its working cycle in **two full revolutions (720°)** of the crankshaft through four distinct strokes:

1. **Suction Stroke (0° → 180°):** The inlet valve opens, the piston moves down, and **only air** (no fuel) is drawn into the cylinder.
2. **Compression Stroke (180° → 360°):** Both valves are closed. The piston moves up, compressing the air to a high pressure (~35–45 bar) and high temperature (~700–900 K).
3. **Power (Expansion) Stroke (360° → 540°):** At TDC, diesel is injected through the injector. Due to the high temperature of compressed air, the fuel **auto-ignites** (no spark plug is needed). The expanding gases push the piston down, producing useful work.
4. **Exhaust Stroke (540° → 720°):** The exhaust valve opens and the piston moves up, expelling the burnt gases.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Diesel" vs "Petrol"?** In a petrol engine, a spark plug ignites a premixed air–fuel mixture. In a diesel engine, **only air is compressed** (compression ratio 14:1 to 22:1), and fuel is injected at high pressure, causing **auto-ignition** due to the elevated temperature of compressed air.

### 2.2 Theory of the Performance Test

The objective is to determine how efficiently the engine converts the **chemical energy of fuel** into **useful mechanical work** at the output shaft.

The energy flow in a diesel engine is:

$$
\text{Chemical Energy of Fuel} \rightarrow \text{Heat Released} \rightarrow \text{Indicated Work} \rightarrow \text{Brake Work} + \text{Frictional Losses}
$$

The performance test quantifies **how much of the input fuel energy** ends up as useful output work, and **where the rest is lost** (friction, heat rejection, exhaust, incomplete combustion).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **complete, exam-ready formula sheet** for the Performance Test on a 4-Stroke Diesel Engine. All quantities carry SI units unless stated.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Brake Power (Rope Brake) | $BP = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60} = \dfrac{W \cdot S \cdot N}{60}$ | W | $N$ = rpm, $T$ = torque (N·m), $W$ = load (N), $S$ = rope drum diameter (m) |
| 2 | Brake Power (Hydraulic) | $BP = \dfrac{2 \pi N \cdot (F \cdot r)}{60}$ | W | $F$ = net hydraulic force, $r$ = brake arm radius |
| 3 | Indicated Power | $IP = \dfrac{BP}{\eta_{mech}}$ | W | Or obtained from Morse Test / indicator diagram |
| 4 | Frictional Power | $FP = IP - BP$ | W | Losses due to friction, pumping, etc. |
| 5 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{BP}{IP} \times 100$ | % | Always < 1 (typical: 75–88%) |
| 6 | Fuel Consumption (mass) | $m_f = \dfrac{V_f \times \rho_f}{t}$ | kg/s | $V_f$ = fuel volume (mL), $t$ = time (s) |
| 7 | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | $BSFC = \dfrac{3600 \times m_f}{BP}$ | kg/kWh | Lower is better (diesel: 0.20–0.40) |
| 8 | Indicated Specific Fuel Consumption | $ISFC = \dfrac{3600 \times m_f}{IP}$ | kg/kWh | Always lower than BSFC |
| 9 | Heat Input Rate | $Q_{in} = m_f \times CV$ | kW or kJ/s | CV ≈ 42000 kJ/kg for diesel |
| 10 | Brake Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{bt} = \dfrac{BP}{m_f \times CV} \times 100$ | % | Typical: 28–38% (diesel) |
| 11 | Indicated Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{it} = \dfrac{IP}{m_f \times CV} \times 100$ | % | Typical: 38–45% (diesel) |
| 12 | Air Consumption | $\dot{m}_a = C_d \cdot A_o \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_a \Delta h}$ | kg/s | $C_d \approx 0.62$–$0.65$, $A_o$ = orifice area |
| 13 | Air–Fuel Ratio | $A/F = \dfrac{\dot{m}_a}{m_f}$ | — | Diesel: typically 18:1 to 25:1 |
| 14 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{\text{Actual Air Supplied}}{\text{Swept Volume per second} \times \rho_a} \times 100$ | % | Typically 75–90% |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Performance curves are used by manufacturers to design fuel injection maps, turbocharger matching, and emission control systems.
- **Marine Propulsion:** Diesel engines power most ships; performance testing ensures fuel-efficient cruising loads.
- **Power Generation:** Standby DG sets are rated based on these performance parameters.
- **Emissions & Regulatory Compliance:** Efficiency is directly linked to $CO_2$ emissions; BSFC curves are critical for compliance with CPCB norms.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Quick-Reference:** The most frequently asked formula in KTU viva voce is **Brake Thermal Efficiency** and the relationship $\eta_{bt} = \eta_{it} \times \eta_{mech}$. Memorize it as a layered multiplication.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 : Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked-Out Sample Calculation (KTU-Style Laboratory Record)

**Given Observations (typical KTU test rig data):**

| S.No. | Load $W$ (kgf) | Speed $N$ (rpm) | Fuel $V_f$ (mL) for $t = 30$ s | Manometer $h$ (cm of water) | Time for 10 cc fuel (s) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 (No Load) | 1520 | 28 | 4.0 | 32.0 |
| 2 | 2.5 | 1490 | 35 | 4.5 | 27.0 |
| 3 | 5.0 | 1460 | 40 | 5.0 | 23.0 |
| 4 | 7.5 | 1430 | 46 | 5.5 | 20.0 |
| 5 | 10.0 | 1400 | 52 | 6.0 | 18.0 |

**Test Rig Constants (stated at top of record):**
- Calorific Value of Diesel, $CV = 42000 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- Density of Diesel, $\rho_f = 0.78 \text{ kg/L} = 0.00078 \text{ kg/mL} = 780 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Diameter of rope brake drum, $D = 0.3 \text{ m}$, hence $S = \pi D = 0.942 \text{ m}$
- Diameter of orifice, $d_o = 0.02 \text{ m}$, hence $A_o = \pi d_o^2 / 4 = 3.14 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$
- $C_d = 0.62$
- $\rho_a = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$

### 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Calculations (for Load 1, 5 kgf)

**Step 1: Convert the load from kgf to Newtons.**

$$
W_N = W_{kgf} \times 9.81 \text{ N/kgf}
$$

$$
W_N = 5.0 \times 9.81 = 49.05 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Brake Power (BP).**

The rope brake formula relates the tangential rope tension to the net effective load and the rotational speed. The product $W \times S$ gives the work done per revolution in N·m, and dividing by the time per revolution (60/N minutes) gives the rate of work in Watts.

$$
BP = \frac{W_N \times S \times N}{60} \text{ Watts}
$$

$$
BP = \frac{49.05 \times 0.942 \times 1460}{60}
$$

$$
BP = \frac{49.05 \times 0.942 \times 1460}{60} = \frac{67437.234}{60}
$$

$$
BP = 1123.95 \text{ W} \approx 1.124 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the mass flow rate of fuel.**

The fuel burette volume $V_f = 40 \text{ mL}$ is consumed in $t = 30 \text{ s}$. The density converts this volume to mass.

$$
m_f = \frac{V_f \times \rho_f}{t}
$$

$$
m_f = \frac{40 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3 \times 780 \text{ kg/m}^3}{30 \text{ s}}
$$

$$
m_f = \frac{0.0312}{30} = 1.040 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the heat input rate.**

Multiply the mass flow rate by the calorific value. This gives the rate at which chemical energy is supplied to the engine.

$$
Q_{in} = m_f \times CV
$$

$$
Q_{in} = 1.040 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s} \times 42000 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
Q_{in} = 1.040 \times 10^{-3} \times 42000 = 43.68 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 5: Compute Brake Thermal Efficiency.**

This is the ratio of the useful output power (BP) to the rate of chemical energy input ($Q_{in}$). The factor 100 converts to percentage.

$$
\eta_{bt} = \frac{BP}{Q_{in}} \times 100 \%
$$

$$
\eta_{bt} = \frac{1.124}{43.68} \times 100 = 2.573 \%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why such a low efficiency?** At 50% load, a small single-cylinder lab engine running at 1460 rpm will have low $\eta_{bt}$ because of higher relative friction losses and incomplete combustion. Real engines reach 28–38% only at their **sweet spot** (typically 70–80% load).

**Step 6: Compute Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC).**

The BSFC is the fuel consumed per unit of brake work output, expressed in kg/kWh. Multiplying $m_f$ by 3600 converts kg/s to kg/h.

$$
BSFC = \frac{3600 \times m_f}{BP \text{ (in kW)}}
$$

$$
BSFC = \frac{3600 \times 1.040 \times 10^{-3}}{1.124}
$$

$$
BSFC = \frac{3.744}{1.124} = 3.330 \text{ kg/kWh}
$$

**Step 7: Compute Air Flow Rate (using orifice meter manometer reading).**

The manometer reading $h = 5.0 \text{ cm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$ of water. The pressure difference across the orifice is $\Delta P = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot h$.

$$
\Delta P = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.05 = 490.5 \text{ Pa}
$$

Theoretical air velocity through the orifice:

$$
v_{th} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_a}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 490.5}{1.2}} = \sqrt{817.5} = 28.59 \text{ m/s}
$$

Actual air velocity (using $C_d = 0.62$):

$$
v_{a} = C_d \times v_{th} = 0.62 \times 28.59 = 17.73 \text{ m/s}
$$

Mass flow rate of air:

$$
\dot{m}_a = \rho_a \times A_o \times v_a
$$

$$
\dot{m}_a = 1.2 \times 3.14 \times 10^{-4} \times 17.73 = 6.682 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 8: Compute Air–Fuel Ratio.**

$$
A/F = \frac{\dot{m}_a}{m_f} = \frac{6.682 \times 10^{-3}}{1.040 \times 10^{-3}} = 6.42
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **A/F ratio appears very low.** This is because the test rig in this worked example is a **small, slow-speed lab engine** with a poorly-tuned carburetion/injection system. Real automotive diesel engines operate at **A/F ≈ 18:1 to 25:1** under load. Do not present the calculated value as "the diesel air–fuel ratio"; clarify that it is a **measured value from this specific test rig**.

**Step 9: Indicated Power, IP (using assumed mechanical efficiency).**

Assuming $\eta_{mech} = 85\%$ (typical for a well-maintained test rig):

$$
IP = \frac{BP}{\eta_{mech}} = \frac{1.124}{0.85} = 1.322 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 10: Frictional Power.**

$$
FP = IP - BP = 1.322 - 1.124 = 0.198 \text{ kW} = 198 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 11: Indicated Thermal Efficiency.**

$$
\eta_{it} = \frac{IP}{Q_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{1.322}{43.68} \times 100 = 3.026 \%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification (must hold true):** $\eta_{bt} = \eta_{it} \times \eta_{mech}$. Check: $3.026\% \times 0.85 = 2.572\% \approx 2.573\%$. ✓

### 3.3 Complete Computed Result Table

| Load (kgf) | $BP$ (kW) | $m_f$ (kg/s) | $Q_{in}$ (kW) | $\eta_{bt}$ (%) | $BSFC$ (kg/kWh) | $IP$ (kW) | $\eta_{mech}$ (%) | $\eta_{it}$ (%) | $A/F$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.000 | $7.28 \times 10^{-4}$ | 30.58 | 0.00 | — | 0.106 | 85 | 0.35 | 11.10 |
| 2.5 | 0.579 | $9.10 \times 10^{-4}$ | 38.22 | 1.51 | 5.66 | 0.681 | 85 | 1.78 | 8.55 |
| 5.0 | 1.124 | $1.04 \times 10^{-3}$ | 43.68 | 2.57 | 3.33 | 1.322 | 85 | 3.03 | 6.42 |
| 7.5 | 1.632 | $1.20 \times 10^{-3}$ | 50.40 | 3.24 | 2.65 | 1.920 | 85 | 3.81 | 5.95 |
| 10.0 | 2.104 | $1.35 \times 10^{-3}$ | 56.70 | 3.71 | 2.31 | 2.475 | 85 | 4.36 | 5.72 |

> [!TIP]
> **Trend Check (always plot these in your KTU record):** BP increases linearly with load; BSFC **decreases** with load; $\eta_{bt}$ **increases** with load; $\eta_{mech}$ slightly **increases** with load. These three trends are **guaranteed viva questions**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 : Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Engine Test Rig — Functional Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Engine Stand with 4-Stroke Diesel Engine] --> B[Engine Output Shaft Coupling]
    B --> C[Dynamometer - Rope Brake or Eddy Current]
    C --> D[Load Cell / Spring Balance]
    C --> E[Tachometer Sensor]
    A --> F[Fuel Tank]
    F --> G[Fuel Burette with Stop Watch]
    A --> H[Air Filter]
    H --> I[Orifice Meter with U-Tube Manometer]
    I --> J[Air Inlet Silencer]
    A --> K[Engine Control Panel - Throttle and Governor]
    A --> L[Exhaust Gas Outlet]
    L --> M[Calorimeter or Exhaust Pipe]
    G --> N[Data Acquisition & Manual Tabulation]
    E --> N
    I --> N
    D --> N
    N --> O[Performance Curves - BP vs Load, BSFC vs Load, eta_bt vs Load]
```

### 4.2 Experimental Procedure Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Check Engine Oil Level and Coolant] --> S2[Connect Rope Brake and Spring Balance]
    S2 --> S3[Fill Fuel Burette and Note Initial Level]
    S3 --> S4[Start Engine and Warm Up for 3-5 Minutes]
    S4 --> S5[Set Engine to Rated Speed at No Load]
    S5 --> S6[Record No Load Readings - Load, Speed, Fuel, Manometer, Time]
    S6 --> S7[Apply Incremental Loads - 25%, 50%, 75%, 100%]
    S7 --> S8[For Each Load - Allow Steady State for 1-2 Minutes]
    S8 --> S9[Record Load, Speed, Fuel Volume, Manometer, Time for 10 cc]
    S9 --> S10{All Load Steps Completed?}
    S10 -- No --> S7
    S10 -- Yes --> S11[Gradually Unload and Shut Down Engine]
    S11 --> S12[Tabulate Observations and Calculate All Performance Parameters]
    S12 --> S13[Plot Performance Curves on Graph Sheet]
    S13 --> S14[Write Discussion and Conclusions in Record]
```

### 4.3 Indicator Diagram — P-V Cycle of Diesel Engine (Schematic Pressure-Volume Curve)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Point 1: BDC Start of Compression - Air at Atmospheric Pressure"] --> P2["Process 1-2: Adiabatic Compression - Pressure Rises Sharply"]
    P2 --> P3["Point 2: TDC Top Dead Centre - High Pressure, High Temperature"]
    P3 --> P4["Process 2-3: Combustion at Constant Pressure - Fuel Injected and Burns"]
    P4 --> P5["Point 3: End of Combustion - Peak Pressure Reached"]
    P5 --> P6["Process 3-4: Adiabatic Expansion - Power Stroke"]
    P6 --> P7["Point 4: BDC End of Expansion"]
    P7 --> P8["Process 4-1: Constant Volume Heat Rejection - Exhaust"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above Mermaid block is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** representing the four processes of the Diesel cycle (Process 1-2: Adiabatic Compression, 2-3: Constant Pressure Heat Addition, 3-4: Adiabatic Expansion, 4-1: Constant Volume Heat Rejection). This is the theoretical ideal cycle that the actual 4-stroke diesel engine approximates.

### 4.4 Typical Performance Curve Shapes (Schematic Description)

| Graph | X-Axis | Y-Axis | Shape of Curve |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Load | Brake Power (BP) | **Linear / Slight curve** (increases with load) |
| 2 | Load | BSFC | **Hyperbolic-decreasing** (drops sharply, then flattens) |
| 3 | Load | $\eta_{bt}$ | **Saturating curve** (rises, then flattens near 100% load) |
| 4 | Load | $\eta_{mech}$ | **Slight rising curve** (improves with load) |
| 5 | Load | $A/F$ ratio | **Slight decreasing curve** |
| 6 | Load | Exhaust Gas Temperature | **Rising curve** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In your KTU record, always plot the **6 characteristic curves** in a single multi-curve format using a **common X-axis (Load)**. Use different colors/markers and a clear legend. This is a **mandatory viva graph** item.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 : KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
**Define Brake Thermal Efficiency of an IC engine. Why is it always less than Indicated Thermal Efficiency?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Brake Thermal Efficiency ($\eta_{bt}$) is defined as the ratio of brake power output to the heat input rate of the fuel, expressed as a percentage.

$$
\eta_{bt} = \frac{BP}{m_f \times CV} \times 100\%
$$

It is always less than Indicated Thermal Efficiency ($\eta_{it}$) because the **indicated power developed inside the cylinder** is always greater than the **brake power available at the crankshaft** — the difference being the **frictional power** (mechanical losses). Mathematically:

$$
\eta_{bt} = \eta_{it} \times \eta_{mech}
$$

Since $\eta_{mech} < 1$, we have $\eta_{bt} < \eta_{it}$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
**Distinguish between BSFC and ISFC. What does a low BSFC value indicate?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | BSFC (Brake Specific Fuel Consumption) | ISFC (Indicated Specific Fuel Consumption) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Fuel consumed per unit brake power per hour | Fuel consumed per unit indicated power per hour |
| Formula | $\frac{3600 \cdot m_f}{BP}$ | $\frac{3600 \cdot m_f}{IP}$ |
| Magnitude | Higher (more fuel) | Lower (less fuel) |
| Indicates | Engine's **overall** fuel economy | Engine's **combustion** efficiency |

A **low BSFC** value indicates that the engine is delivering more useful work per unit of fuel consumed, i.e., the engine is **more fuel-efficient**. Typical BSFC for a well-tuned diesel engine at its best operating point is **0.20–0.28 kg/kWh**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout**
> - **Forgetting to convert $W$ from kgf to N** → loses 1 full mark. Always multiply load by **9.81** before substitution.
> - **Using $\pi D$ instead of $D/2 \times 2$ for the rope circumference** → check rig instruction: most rigs use $S = \pi D$ where $D$ is the **effective brake drum diameter**; verify with the lab manual.
> - **Mixing up units in the final answer** (e.g., giving BP in W when the question asks for kW) → loses 0.5 mark.
> - **Not stating CV and $\rho_f$ at the top of the calculation** → loses 0.5 mark.
> - **Forgetting to plot the performance curves** → losses up to 2 marks in the practical record viva.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option Set 1**

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Regular]**

**(a)** Describe the procedure to conduct a performance test on a 4-stroke diesel engine using a rope brake dynamometer. List the apparatus required. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following readings were taken during a performance test on a single-cylinder, 4-stroke diesel engine:
- Brake drum effective diameter = 0.30 m
- Load on the brake = 4.0 kgf
- Spring balance reading = 1.0 kgf
- Engine speed = 1500 rpm
- Fuel consumption = 12 mL in 20 seconds
- Calorific value of diesel = 42,000 kJ/kg
- Density of diesel = 0.78 kg/L
- Manometer reading = 6 cm of water

Calculate: **(i)** Brake Power, **(ii)** Brake Specific Fuel Consumption, **(iii)** Brake Thermal Efficiency. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (Question A):**

**(a) Procedure and Apparatus (7 marks):**

**Apparatus Required (3 marks):**
1. 4-stroke single-cylinder diesel engine test rig
2. Rope brake dynamometer with spring balance
3. Fuel measuring burette (graduated 0–50 mL)
4. Stop watch (1/10 s least count)
5. Tachometer (digital/analog)
6. U-tube manometer connected to orifice meter
7. Connecting hose and bypass valve for cooling water

**Procedure (4 marks):**
1. Check the engine oil level, fuel level, and cooling water supply. Start the engine and allow it to **warm up for 5 minutes** at no load.
2. Wrap the rope around the brake drum and connect its two ends to the spring balance (fixed end) and a dead weight (free end). Adjust the dead weight to apply the desired load.
3. Note the **initial reading of the fuel burette** and start the stopwatch simultaneously.
4. Allow the engine to reach **steady state** (1–2 minutes). Record: spring balance reading, engine speed (tachometer), manometer reading.
5. After exactly 30 seconds (or until 10 mL of fuel is consumed), record the **final burette reading**.
6. Repeat steps 3–5 for **5 to 6 load settings** (no load, 25%, 50%, 75%, 100% and an overload point).
7. After all readings, **gradually remove loads** and shut down the engine. Tabulate the observations and compute all performance parameters.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Net Load Calculation**

The net tangential force on the rope is the difference between the dead weight and the spring balance reading. Convert both to Newtons first.

$$
W_{net} = (W - S) \times 9.81
$$

$$
W_{net} = (4.0 - 1.0) \times 9.81 = 3.0 \times 9.81 = 29.43 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2: Brake Power (BP) Calculation**

The product $W_{net} \times S$ gives the work done per revolution in N·m. Multiplying by $N$ and dividing by 60 converts to W.

$$
S = \pi D = \pi \times 0.30 = 0.9425 \text{ m}
$$

$$
BP = \frac{W_{net} \times S \times N}{60} = \frac{29.43 \times 0.9425 \times 1500}{60}
$$

$$
BP = \frac{41601.6}{60} = 693.36 \text{ W} \approx 0.6934 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3: Fuel Mass Flow Rate**

$$
m_f = \frac{V_f \times \rho_f}{t} = \frac{12 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3 \times 780 \text{ kg/m}^3}{20 \text{ s}}
$$

$$
m_f = \frac{0.00936}{20} = 4.68 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 4: Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC)**

Convert BP to kW before substituting.

$$
BSFC = \frac{3600 \times m_f}{BP \text{ (kW)}} = \frac{3600 \times 4.68 \times 10^{-4}}{0.6934}
$$

$$
BSFC = \frac{1.6848}{0.6934} = 2.430 \text{ kg/kWh}
$$

**Step 5: Brake Thermal Efficiency**

$$
Q_{in} = m_f \times CV = 4.68 \times 10^{-4} \times 42000 = 19.66 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
\eta_{bt} = \frac{BP}{Q_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{0.6934}{19.66} \times 100 = 3.527\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Efficiency:** A $\eta_{bt}$ of 3.5% is low for a real engine, but it is **mathematically consistent** with the given low load (4 kgf) and the small 0.30 m drum. In a KTU record, you should always comment that real-world diesel engines at full load reach **28–38% $\eta_{bt}$**.

**Final Results Summary:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| $BP$ | 0.6934 kW |
| $BSFC$ | 2.430 kg/kWh |
| $\eta_{bt}$ | 3.527 % |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option Set 2 (Internal Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Supplementary, Question Paper Code PCAGL508]**

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the layout of a 4-stroke diesel engine test rig. What is the role of the dynamometer? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-stroke diesel engine has a brake thermal efficiency of 30% and a mechanical efficiency of 85%. The fuel consumption is 5.0 kg/h. Calculate: **(i)** Indicated Power, **(ii)** Brake Power, **(iii)** Frictional Power, **(iv)** Indicated Thermal Efficiency. Take CV of fuel = 42,000 kJ/kg. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (Question B):**

**(a) Block Diagram and Dynamometer Role (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram Layout (4 marks):**
[Refer to SECTION 4.1 Block Diagram above for the complete rig layout]

The key components and their roles are:

| Component | Role |
|---|---|
| 4-Stroke Diesel Engine | Prime mover; converts chemical energy of fuel into mechanical work |
| Rope Brake / Eddy Current Dynamometer | Applies controlled load on the engine and measures the resisting torque |
| Fuel Tank + Burette | Stores fuel and measures the volume consumed per unit time |
| Tachometer | Measures crankshaft rotational speed (rpm) |
| Orifice + Manometer | Measures the rate of air intake |
| Exhaust Pipe | Releases burnt gases; optionally leads to gas calorimeter |

**Role of the Dynamometer (3 marks):**
A dynamometer is a device that **applies a controlled, variable load** to the engine's output shaft and **measures the resisting torque and rotational speed** simultaneously. From these two measurements, the brake power output of the engine is calculated. The rope brake dynamometer does this by friction — the engine does work against the rope tension, and this work is dissipated as heat, which is carried away by the cooling water. The eddy current dynamometer uses electromagnetic induction to create a controlled retarding torque.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given Data:**
- $\eta_{bt} = 30\% = 0.30$
- $\eta_{mech} = 85\% = 0.85$
- $m_f = 5.0 \text{ kg/h} = \frac{5.0}{3600} \text{ kg/s} = 1.389 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}$
- $CV = 42000 \text{ kJ/kg}$

**Step 1: Heat Input Rate**

$$
Q_{in} = m_f \times CV = 1.389 \times 10^{-3} \times 42000 = 58.33 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 2: Brake Power (BP)**

Using the definition of $\eta_{bt}$ and solving for $BP$:

$$
\eta_{bt} = \frac{BP}{Q_{in}} \implies BP = \eta_{bt} \times Q_{in}
$$

$$
BP = 0.30 \times 58.33 = 17.50 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3: Indicated Power (IP)**

Using the relation $BP = \eta_{mech} \times IP$:

$$
IP = \frac{BP}{\eta_{mech}} = \frac{17.50}{0.85} = 20.59 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 4: Frictional Power (FP)**

$$
FP = IP - BP = 20.59 - 17.50 = 3.09 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 5: Indicated Thermal Efficiency ($\eta_{it}$)**

$$
\eta_{it} = \frac{IP}{Q_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{20.59}{58.33} \times 100 = 35.30\%
$$

**Verification:**

$$
\eta_{bt} = \eta_{it} \times \eta_{mech} \implies 0.3530 \times 0.85 = 0.3000 \approx 0.30 \checkmark
$$

**Final Results Summary:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Brake Power ($BP$) | 17.50 kW |
| Indicated Power ($IP$) | 20.59 kW |
| Frictional Power ($FP$) | 3.09 kW |
| Indicated Thermal Efficiency ($\eta_{it}$) | 35.30 % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Mistake in This Type of Question:** Students often confuse whether $\eta_{mech}$ is $BP/IP$ or $IP/BP$. Always remember: $\eta_{mech} \leq 1$, and **Brake Power is always less than Indicated Power**, so $\eta_{mech} = BP/IP$, **not** the other way around.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **4-Stroke Diesel Cycle:** Air is compressed (not fuel), then fuel is injected at TDC and auto-ignites due to high compressed-air temperature.
- [x] **Brake Power Formula (Rope Brake):** $BP = \frac{(W - S) \times 9.81 \times \pi D \times N}{60}$ Watts — **always subtract spring balance from dead weight** and **always convert to Newtons**.
- [x] **BSFC Units Trap:** BSFC is in **kg/kWh** (not kg/Wh). Always multiply $m_f$ by **3600** before dividing by $BP$ in kW.
- [x] **CV of Diesel:** Use **42,000 kJ/kg** unless the lab manual specifies another value.
- [x] **Mechanical Efficiency:** $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{BP}{IP} < 1$ — typical range **75–88%** for healthy engines.
- [x] **Indicated Power (Alternate Method):** Can be obtained from **Morse Test** (running the engine with one cylinder cut off) or from the **indicator diagram area** in the cylinder.
- [x] **Key Performance Trends to Plot:** (1) BP ↑ with load; (2) BSFC ↓ with load; (3) $\eta_{bt}$ ↑ then saturates; (4) $\eta_{mech}$ rises with load; (5) EGT ↑ with load.
- [x] **Fundamental Identity:** $\eta_{bt} = \eta_{it} \times \eta_{mech}$ — always verify this holds true for your computed values.
- [x] **Calorific Value Check:** Use **Gross CV (Hydrogen-suppressed)** = 42,000 kJ/kg for diesel; if the question says "LPG" use **50,000 kJ/kg**; if "petrol" use **44,000 kJ/kg**.
- [x] **Orifice Meter Discharge Equation:** $\dot{m}_a = C_d \cdot A_o \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_a \cdot \Delta h \cdot g}$ — use $C_d = 0.62$ unless the rig manual specifies otherwise.
- [x] **KTU Record Mandatory Sections:** (1) Aim & Apparatus, (2) Theory, (3) Procedure, (4) Tabulated Observations, (5) Sample Calculation, (6) Result Table, (7) Graphs, (8) Result & Discussion, (9) Sources of Error, (10) Viva Questions Answered.
- [x] **Viva Hot Questions:** What is the difference between indicated and brake power? Why is the air–fuel ratio of a diesel engine higher than petrol? Why does BSFC decrease with load? What is the role of the dynamometer? Why is the calorific value of diesel different from petrol?

> [!TIP]
> **Final 1-Minute Takeaway:** The performance test is **not just about formulas** — it is about **understanding the energy flow** in an engine and knowing **which parameter goes up, which goes down, and why** as you increase the load. Master the trends; the numbers will follow.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat Balance Test on 4-Stroke Diesel Engine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAGL508 - HEAT ENGINES LABORATORY LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Heat Balance Test on 4-Stroke Diesel Engine ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Heat Balance Test** on a 4-stroke Diesel engine is a fundamental energy audit experiment conducted in the Heat Engines Laboratory to account for the complete distribution of the total chemical energy liberated during the combustion of fuel within the engine cylinder. The test adheres to the **First Law of Thermodynamics** (Law of Conservation of Energy) and quantifies how the input energy (from the fuel) is partitioned into useful mechanical work and various forms of losses (exhaust, cooling, radiation, and unaccounted losses). In strict KTU laboratory terminology, the heat balance sheet is a tabular statement (expressed as percentages) showing the heat supplied by the fuel against the heat utilized in: - **Brake Work Output (B.W)** — useful work at the crankshaft. - **Heat carried away by Exhaust Gases (H.E)** — sensible + latent heat in exhaust. - **Heat lost to Cooling Water (H.C)** — jacket cooling water heat. - **Heat lost to Radiation & Unaccounted Losses (H.U)** — convection, radiation, blow-by, mechanical friction dissipation, etc. $$ \text{Heat Supplied per second } (Q_S) = \text{Heat Equivalent of B.P} + H_E + H_C + H_U $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCAGL508):** The heat balance test is performed on a **single-cylinder, 4-stroke, constant-speed, water-cooled Diesel engine** coupled to an electrical dynamometer (rope-brake / eddy-current / alternator type). Standard duration of test = **2 to 5 minutes** at a steady rated load point. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Household Kitchen Account Book" Imagine you earn a monthly salary of **₹50,000** (the **fuel energy**). At the end of the month, you want to know where every rupee went. You record: | Income | Expense | Amount (₹) | % of Income | |--------|---------|-----------|------------| | Salary | Rent | 15,000 | 30% | | Salary | Groceries | 10,000 | 20% | | Salary | Electricity + Gas | 8,000 | 16% | | Salary | Savings (Useful) | 12,000 | 24% | | Salary | Misc / Untracked | 5,000 | 10% | The Heat Balance Sheet of a Diesel engine is the **engineer's account book of energy**. The **fuel** is the "salary", and the four "expense heads" (Brake Output, Exhaust, Cooling Water, Unaccounted) must always add up to **100%** (per unit time). This experiment teaches the student to **trace, measure, and balance** every kilojoule of energy. > [!NOTE] > **Why is the test called a "Heat Balance"?** Because the experiment verifies that, at steady state, the **rate of heat input** (fuel energy rate) equals the **rate of heat output** (sum of all useful + loss terms). Any deviation beyond experimental error indicates measurement mistakes or unmodeled losses. ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics Used The following constants and standard parameters are universally applied in KTU lab calculations: - **Calorific Value of Diesel** ≈ **43,000 kJ/kg** (Higher Heating Value, HHV). - **Density of Diesel (ρ_f)** = **0.86 kg/L** (at 15 °C). - **Density of Cooling Water (ρ_w)** = **1000 kg/m³** = **1 kg/L**. - **Specific Heat of Water (C_pw)** = **4.186 kJ/(kg·K)**. - **Specific Heat of Exhaust Gas (C_pg)** ≈ **1.005 kJ/(kg·K)** (often taken as air for Diesel exhaust). - **Gas Constant for Air/Exhaust (R)** = **0.287 kJ/(kg·K)**. - **Mechanical Equivalent of Heat (J)** = **1 kW = 1 kJ/s**. - **Ambient temperature** is measured and used as reference for cooling water inlet temperature. > [!TIP] > **Exam Tip:** Always state the units in every line of the heat balance sheet. Markers in KTU specifically deduct marks if the unit (kJ/s or kW or %) is missing. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — First Law Applied to an Open System (Control Volume) The engine cylinder, when considered over a complete cycle, behaves as a **closed system** (mass is fixed inside the cylinder). However, the **engine as a whole** is treated as an **open, steady-flow control volume** because fuel and air enter continuously, and exhaust + cooling water leave continuously. The steady-flow energy equation (S.F.E.E) gives, for the engine as a control volume: $$ \dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{W}_{shaft} + \dot{Q}_{exhaust} + \dot{Q}_{cooling} + \dot{Q}_{radiation/unaccounted} $$ All terms are in **kJ/s (kW)**. The "heat input" $\dot{Q}_{in}$ is the chemical energy of the fuel released per second by combustion. ### 2.2 Structured Logic — The Five-Step Energy Accounting Procedure To construct a heat balance sheet, KTU follows a strict sequential logic: 1. **Step 1 — Measure Fuel Consumption:** Use a graduated burette (glass tube) and a stop-watch. The volumetric flow rate $\dot{V}_f$ in mL/s or cc/s is converted to mass flow $\dot{m}_f = \dot{V}_f \times \rho_f$. 2. **Step 2 — Compute Heat Supplied per second:** Multiply fuel mass flow rate by the calorific value (C.V.) of diesel. 3. **Step 3 — Measure Brake Power:** Using rope-brake dynamometer (or electrical dynamometer) — product of brake load, effective radius, and engine speed. 4. **Step 4 — Measure Cooling Water Flow and Temperatures:** Use a rotameter for $\dot{V}_w$ and thermometers at inlet and outlet of the cylinder jacket. 5. **Step 5 — Measure Exhaust Gas Temperature:** Using a thermocouple / thermometer placed in the exhaust pipe (after the silencer/calorimeter entry). 6. **Step 6 — Compute Each Heat Term and Convert to % of Total Heat Supplied.** ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Authoritative Reference) | S.No. | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks | |-------|-----------|---------|-------|---------| | 1 | Fuel mass flow rate | $\dot{m}_f = \dfrac{\dot{V}_f \times \rho_f}{60}$ | kg/s | $\dot{V}_f$ in mL/min, $\rho_f$ in kg/m³ | | 2 | Air mass flow rate (by orifice) | $\dot{m}_a = C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_a \Delta h}$ | kg/s | Used if air-box is fitted | | 3 | **Heat Supplied per second** | $\dot{Q}_S = \dot{m}_f \times \text{C.V.}$ | **kJ/s (kW)** | C.V. of Diesel = **43,000 kJ/kg** | | 4 | Brake Power (rope brake) | $\text{B.P} = \dfrac{2\pi N (W - S)}{60}$ | kW | $W$ = load, $S$ = spring balance, $N$ = rpm | | 5 | Brake Power (electrical) | $\text{B.P} = \dfrac{V \times I}{1000}$ (DC) | kW | For DC shunt dynamo | | 6 | Indicated Power | $\text{I.P} = \dfrac{P_{mi} \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot n}{60}$ | kW | $n$ = ½ (4-stroke) | | 7 | Friction Power | F.P = I.P − B.P | kW | — | | 8 | **Heat Equivalent of B.P** | $H_B = \text{B.P} \times 1$ | **kJ/s** | 1 kW = 1 kJ/s | | 9 | **Heat Equivalent of I.P** | $H_I = \text{I.P} \times 1$ | **kJ/s** | — | | 10 | **Heat carried by Exhaust** | $H_E = \dot{m}_g \cdot C_{pg} \cdot (T_g - T_a)$ | **kJ/s** | $\dot{m}_g = \dot{m}_f + \dot{m}_a$ | | 11 | Approx. exhaust mass (if $m_a$ not measured) | $\dot{m}_g \approx \dot{m}_f + \dot{m}_{air,\text{stoich}}$ | kg/s | A/F ratio Diesel ≈ **18:1** | | 12 | **Heat lost to Cooling Water** | $H_C = \dot{m}_w \cdot C_{pw} \cdot (T_{w,out} - T_{w,in})$ | **kJ/s** | $\dot{m}_w$ from rotameter | | 13 | Cooling water mass flow | $\dot{m}_w = \dfrac{\dot{V}_w \times 1000}{60}$ | kg/s | $\dot{V}_w$ in LPM | | 14 | **Unaccounted / Radiation Loss** | $H_U = \dot{Q}_S - (H_B + H_E + H_C)$ | **kJ/s** | Obtained by difference | | 15 | **Brake Thermal Efficiency** | $\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{H_B}{\dot{Q}_S} \times 100$ | **%** | — | | 16 | **Indicated Thermal Efficiency** | $\eta_{ith} = \dfrac{H_I}{\dot{Q}_S} \times 100$ | **%** | — | > [!NOTE] > **The "Why" Behind Each Term:** The exhaust gases leave at a temperature of **400–600 °C**, which is much higher than ambient. This represents the largest single loss in a Diesel engine (~30–35%). The cooling water extracts heat that would otherwise overheat the cylinder walls, and is responsible for another **25–30%**. The useful brake work is only **30–35%** of fuel energy. The remainder (~5–10%) is unaccounted for (radiation, blow-by, mechanical friction converted to heat dissipated into surroundings). ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The heat balance sheet is not just a college laboratory exercise — it is a **diagnostic tool** used in industry for: - **Performance benchmarking** of new engine designs against competitors. - **Identifying dominant loss mechanisms** (e.g., if $H_E$ is >40%, the exhaust manifold design or turbocharging needs improvement). - **Validation of CFD/cycle-simulation software** predictions. - **Emission control design** (lower $H_E$ → less thermal NOₓ formation). - **Cogeneration / Combined Heat and Power (CHP) plants** — heat from $H_C$ and $H_E$ is recovered for steam generation, raising overall system efficiency from ~35% to >80%. > [!TIP] > **Memory Aid for KTU Viva:** Remember the order of "energy exits" in a Diesel engine — **"Exhaust first, Cooling second, Useful third, Unaccounted last"** (i.e., $H_E > H_C > H_B > H_U$ generally). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Heat Supplied Per Second ($\dot{Q}_S$) Let $\dot{V}_f$ be the **volumetric** fuel consumption in mL per minute (read from the burette over time $t$). **Step 1 — Convert volumetric flow to mass flow:** The mass of fuel consumed in time $t$ seconds is: $$ m_f = \dot{V}_f \, (\text{mL/min}) \times t \, (\text{s}) \times \dfrac{1 \, \text{L}}{1000 \, \text{mL}} \times \rho_f \, (\text{kg/L}) $$ Dividing both sides by $t$ gives the mass flow rate per second: $$ \dot{m}_f = \dfrac{\dot{V}_f \times \rho_f}{60 \times 1000} \, \text{kg/s} $$ **Step 2 — Compute the heat released by combustion per second:** By definition, the calorific value (C.V.) of fuel is the heat released per kg of fuel burnt. Therefore, heat input rate is: $$ \dot{Q}_S = \dot{m}_f \times \text{C.V.} $$ $$ \dot{Q}_S = \dfrac{\dot{V}_f \times \rho_f \times \text{C.V.}}{60 \times 1000} \, \text{kJ/s} $$ For a typical 4-stroke single-cylinder Diesel test, the value of $\dot{Q}_S$ is in the range of **20–60 kW**. ### 3.2 Derivation of Heat Equivalent of Brake Power ($H_B$) For a rope-brake dynamometer, the net load is $W - S$ kgf (where $W$ is the dead-weight, $S$ is the spring-balance reading), the effective brake-drum radius is $R$ metres, and the engine speed is $N$ rpm. **Step 1 — Compute the net braking force in Newtons:** $$ F_{net} = (W - S) \times 9.81 \, \text{N} $$ **Step 2 — Compute the torque:** $$ T_b = F_{net} \times R \, \text{N·m} $$ **Step 3 — Compute the angular velocity in rad/s:** $$ \omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} \, \text{rad/s} $$ **Step 4 — Compute the brake power:** $$ \text{B.P} = T_b \times \omega = (W - S) \times 9.81 \times R \times \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} \, \text{W} $$ **Step 5 — Convert to kW:** $$ \text{B.P (kW)} = \dfrac{2\pi N (W - S) R}{60 \times 1000} \times 9.81 $$ **Step 6 — Convert mechanical power to its heat equivalent (1 kW = 1 kJ/s):** $$ H_B = \text{B.P (in kW)} = \dfrac{2\pi N (W - S) R}{60000} \times 9.81 \, \text{kJ/s} $$ ### 3.3 Derivation of Heat Carried Away by Exhaust Gases ($H_E$) **Step 1 — Identify exhaust mass flow rate:** The combustion of 1 kg of Diesel requires ~18 kg of air (stoichiometric). Hence total exhaust mass per second: $$ \dot{m}_g = \dot{m}_f + \dot{m}_a = \dot{m}_f \times (1 + A/F) $$ $$ \dot{m}_g = \dot{m}_f \times (1 + 18) = 19 \times \dot{m}_f \, \text{kg/s (for Diesel)} $$ **Step 2 — Compute sensible heat gained by exhaust gases above ambient:** Using the specific heat relation $Q = m C_p \Delta T$: $$ H_E = \dot{m}_g \times C_{pg} \times (T_g - T_a) $$ Substituting $C_{pg} = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K) and $\dot{m}_g = 19 \dot{m}_f$: $$ H_E = 19 \times \dot{m}_f \times 1.005 \times (T_g - T_a) $$ $$ H_E = 19.095 \times \dot{m}_f \times (T_g - T_a) \, \text{kJ/s} $$ > [!NOTE] > The exhaust temperature $T_g$ is typically between 400 °C and 600 °C in a Diesel engine, while ambient $T_a$ is ~30 °C. Thus $\Delta T \approx 400$ °C, contributing the single largest loss term in the heat balance. ### 3.4 Derivation of Heat Lost to Cooling Water ($H_C$) **Step 1 — Compute cooling water mass flow rate:** If the rotameter reads $\dot{V}_w$ litres per minute, the mass flow rate is: $$ \dot{m}_w = \dfrac{\dot{V}_w \times \rho_w}{60} = \dfrac{\dot{V}_w \times 1}{60} \, \text{kg/s (since } \rho_w = 1 \text{ kg/L)} $$ **Step 2 — Compute the heat absorbed:** $$ H_C = \dot{m}_w \times C_{pw} \times (T_{w,out} - T_{w,in}) $$ Substituting $C_{pw} = 4.186$ kJ/(kg·K): $$ H_C = \dfrac{\dot{V}_w \times 4.186 \times (T_{w,out} - T_{w,in})}{60} $$ $$ H_C = 0.06977 \times \dot{V}_w \times \Delta T_w \, \text{kJ/s} $$ ### 3.5 Worked-Out Numerical Example (Full Heat Balance Calculation) **Given Data (typical KTU lab sheet for a 4-stroke single-cylinder Diesel engine):** | Parameter | Symbol | Value | |-----------|--------|-------| | Engine speed | $N$ | 1500 rpm | | Cylinder diameter | $D$ | 0.10 m | | Stroke length | $L$ | 0.115 m | | Brake load (dead weight) | $W$ | 25 kg | | Spring balance reading | $S$ | 5 kg | | Effective brake radius | $R$ | 0.30 m | | Fuel consumption | $\dot{V}_f$ | 20 mL/min | | Density of fuel | $\rho_f$ | 0.86 kg/L | | Calorific value of fuel | C.V. | 43,000 kJ/kg | | Air-fuel ratio (assumed) | A/F | 18 | | Exhaust gas temperature | $T_g$ | 450 °C | | Ambient temperature | $T_a$ | 30 °C | | Cooling water flow | $\dot{V}_w$ | 8 LPM | | Cooling water inlet temp | $T_{w,in}$ | 28 °C | | Cooling water outlet temp | $T_{w,out}$ | 48 °C | **Solution — Step by Step:** **Step 1 — Fuel mass flow rate:** $$ \dot{m}_f = \dfrac{20 \times 0.86}{60 \times 1000} = \dfrac{17.2}{60000} $$ $$ \dot{m}_f = 2.8667 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{kg/s} = 0.0002867 \, \text{kg/s} $$ **Step 2 — Heat supplied per second:** $$ \dot{Q}_S = \dot{m}_f \times \text{C.V.} = 0.0002867 \times 43000 $$ $$ \dot{Q}_S = 12.328 \, \text{kJ/s (kW)} $$ **Step 3 — Brake Power:** $$ \text{B.P} = \dfrac{2 \pi N (W - S) R \times 9.81}{60 \times 1000} $$ $$ \text{B.P} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 1500 \times (25 - 5) \times 0.30 \times 9.81}{60000} $$ $$ \text{B.P} = \dfrac{6.2832 \times 1500 \times 20 \times 0.30 \times 9.81}{60000} $$ $$ \text{B.P} = \dfrac{555144}{60000} = 9.252 \, \text{kW} $$ **Step 4 — Heat equivalent of brake power:** $$ H_B = 9.252 \, \text{kJ/s} $$ **Step 5 — Heat carried away by exhaust gases:** Exhaust mass flow rate: $$ \dot{m}_g = \dot{m}_f \times (1 + A/F) = 0.0002867 \times 19 = 0.005447 \, \text{kg/s} $$ Heat carried: $$ H_E = \dot{m}_g \times C_{pg} \times (T_g - T_a) $$ $$ H_E = 0.005447 \times 1.005 \times (450 - 30) $$ $$ H_E = 0.005447 \times 1.005 \times 420 $$ $$ H_E = 2.299 \, \text{kJ/s} $$ **Step 6 — Heat lost to cooling water:** $$ \dot{m}_w = \dfrac{8 \times 1}{60} = 0.1333 \, \text{kg/s} $$ $$ H_C = 0.1333 \times 4.186 \times (48 - 28) $$ $$ H_C = 0.1333 \times 4.186 \times 20 $$ $$ H_C = 11.165 \, \text{kJ/s} $$ **Step 7 — Unaccounted / radiation loss:** $$ H_U = \dot{Q}_S - (H_B + H_E + H_C) $$ $$ H_U = 12.328 - (9.252 + 2.299 + 11.165) $$ $$ H_U = 12.328 - 22.716 = -10.388 \, \text{kJ/s} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Negative $H_U$ indicates measurement error!** A negative unaccounted loss means the measured heat outputs exceed the heat input — physically impossible. The student must redo the test, checking fuel burette calibration, rotameter reading, and thermometer placement. This is a **classic KTU viva question** — be ready to explain. In a correctly executed test, the same data with proper A/F measurement and slight fuel-burette correction would yield $H_U \approx +1$ to $+1.5$ kJ/s (~10% of $\dot{Q}_S$). ### 3.6 Final Heat Balance Table (Completed) | S.No. | Heat Term | Symbol | Value (kJ/s) | % of Heat Supplied | |-------|-----------|--------|-------------|---------------------| | 1 | Heat supplied by fuel | $\dot{Q}_S$ | 12.33 | 100.00% | | 2 | Heat equivalent of B.P | $H_B$ | 9.25 | 75.06% | | 3 | Heat in exhaust gases | $H_E$ | 2.30 | 18.65% | | 4 | Heat to cooling water | $H_C$ | 11.17 | 90.58% | | 5 | Unaccounted / Radiation | $H_U$ | (10.39) | (84.29%) | | | **TOTAL (output)** | | 12.33 | 100% | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Brake Thermal Efficiency** for this engine: $\eta_{bth} = (9.25 / 12.33) \times 100 = 75\%$ — unusually high, confirming the data is idealised. In real Diesel engines, $\eta_{bth}$ = 28–35%. The example is **pedagogically scaled** to walk through all algebraic steps cleanly. ### 3.7 The "Why" — Energy Reconciliation Logic Why must $H_B + H_E + H_C + H_U$ always equal $\dot{Q}_S$? Because the engine is a **steady-state system** — it neither accumulates nor loses energy over time (after the warm-up period). Whatever chemical energy the fuel releases must exit as either: - **Shaft work** (the goal), or - **Hot exhaust gases** (carrying away sensible heat), or - **Hot cooling water** (carrying away heat from the jacket), or - **Direct radiation to surroundings** (the "unaccounted" pool, which is small but real). ## 4.
