# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]`
**Define retardation test. What is its significance in IC engine testing?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Definition (1 mark):** The retardation test, also known as the motoring test or running-down test, is a method of determining the frictional power of an internal combustion engine by allowing the engine to decelerate freely from its running speed after the fuel supply has been cut off.
>
> **Significance (2 marks):**
> 1. It directly yields the **frictional power (FP)** without requiring the engine to be loaded.
> 2. From FP and known BP, the **indicated power (IP)** and **mechanical efficiency (η_mech)** can be computed.
> 3. It is especially useful for **large diesel engines** where dynamometer loading is impractical.
> 4. It separates **mechanical (friction + pumping) losses** from thermodynamic (combustion) losses — a key input for design improvements.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Sample Paper 2]`
**Write the formula for frictional power obtained from a retardation test and explain each term.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> $$\boxed{\,P_f \;=\; \frac{4\pi^2 \cdot I \cdot N \cdot dN}{3600 \cdot 1000 \cdot dt}\;\;\text{kW}\,}$$
>
> Where:
> - `P_f` = Frictional power (kW) — power dissipated in engine friction
> - `I` = Moment of inertia of flywheel and all rotating parts (kg·m²)
> - `N` = Instantaneous engine speed at which dN/dt is measured (rpm)
> - `dN/dt` = Rate of change of speed at that instant (rpm/s) — measured from slope of N-t curve
> - The factor `4π²/3600` converts rpm to rad/s in the angular velocity and deceleration.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Choice 1 — Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) Explain with a neat sketch the principle and procedure of conducting a retardation test on a diesel engine. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle (3 marks):**
The retardation test is based on Newton's second law of rotational motion. When the engine is motored at its rated speed and the fuel supply is cut off, the only torque acting on the rotating system is the **frictional torque** (T_f) of the engine. Therefore, by Newton's law:

$$T_f \;=\; I \cdot \alpha$$

Since `P_f = T_f × ω`, the frictional power at any speed can be computed if we know:
- The moment of inertia `I` of the flywheel and rotating masses.
- The angular velocity `ω` at that instant.
- The angular deceleration `α = dω/dt` (obtained from the slope of the N-t curve).

**Apparatus Required (1 mark):**
1. Diesel engine under test
2. Cranking motor (DC shunt) with starter and variac
3. Flywheel with known mass and diameter
4. Digital tachometer (inductive pickup type)
5. Stopwatch (0.01 s least count)
6. Cooling water system with flow meter
7. Fuel system with shut-off cock

**Procedure (3 marks):**
1. **Step 1:** Ensure the engine is in good mechanical condition. Fill all lubricants and cooling water. Check fuel line and bleed.
2. **Step 2:** Start the cranking motor and bring the diesel engine up to its **rated speed** (say 1500 rpm) under no load.
3. **Step 3:** Allow the engine to **stabilise thermally** for ~10 minutes until jacket water and lube oil reach steady temperatures.
4. **Step 4:** **Cut off the fuel** completely (move fuel rack to zero). Disengage the cranking motor and decouple it.
5. **Step 5:** Engine now **coasts freely** from rated speed downward under its own friction.
6. **Step 6:** Observe and record the time (t) at which the engine speed falls by every **50 rpm** — using the digital tachometer and stopwatch.
7. **Step 7:** Continue until engine speed falls to about 25–30 % of rated speed.
8. **Step 8:** Plot a graph of **N (rpm) vs. t (s)** on graph paper.
9. **Step 9:** Draw a **tangent** to the curve at the rated-speed point. The slope of the tangent gives `dN/dt`.
10. **Step 10:** Calculate `T_f = I × (2π/60) × (dN/dt)` and `P_f = T_f × ω`.

---

**(b) The flywheel of a single-cylinder 4-stroke diesel engine has a mass of 75 kg and a radius of gyration of 0.30 m. During a retardation test, the speed dropped from 1500 rpm to 1490 rpm in 2.4 seconds. Calculate the frictional power at 1500 rpm. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data (1 mark):**
- Mass of flywheel, `m = 75 kg`
- Radius of gyration, `k = 0.30 m`
- Initial speed, `N₁ = 1500 rpm`
- Final speed, `N₂ = 1490 rpm`
- Time interval, `dt = 2.4 s`

**Step 1 — Compute Moment of Inertia (1 mark)**

For a rim-approximation flywheel, or generally using radius of gyration:

$$I \;=\; m \cdot k^2 \;=\; 75 \times (0.30)^2 \;=\; 75 \times 0.09 \;=\; 6.75 \;\; \text{kg·m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute Rate of Deceleration dN/dt (1 mark)**

$$\frac{dN}{dt} \;=\; \frac{N_1 - N_2}{dt} \;=\; \frac{1500 - 1490}{2.4} \;=\; \frac{10}{2.4} \;=\; 4.1667 \;\; \text{rpm/s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Angular Velocity ω at 1500 rpm (1 mark)**

$$\omega \;=\; \frac{2\pi N}{60} \;=\; \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} \;=\; 50\pi \;=\; 157.08 \;\; \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Angular Deceleration α (1 mark)**

$$\alpha \;=\; \frac{2\pi}{60} \cdot \frac{dN}{dt} \;=\; \frac{2\pi}{60} \times 4.1667 \;=\; 0.4363 \;\; \text{rad/s}^2$$

**Step 5 — Compute Frictional Torque T_f (1 mark)**

$$T_f \;=\; I \cdot \alpha \;=\; 6.75 \times 0.4363 \;=\; 2.945 \;\; \text{N·m}$$

**Step 6 — Compute Frictional Power P_f (1 mark)**

$$P_f \;=\; T_f \cdot \omega \;=\; 2.945 \times 157.08 \;=\; 462.65 \;\; \text{W}$$

$$\boxed{\,P_f \;=\; 0.4627 \;\; \text{kW} \,}$$

---

### **Choice 2 — Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Sample Paper 3]`

**(a) What are the assumptions and limitations of the retardation test? How are they accounted for in practical engine analysis? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions (3 marks):**
1. **Friction torque is the only resisting torque** — i.e., during coasting, the only retarding torque on the rotating system is the engine's internal friction.
2. **Moment of inertia is constant** — assumed constant with speed (true for rigid rotating parts).
3. **Engine temperature is constant** during the test (lube oil viscosity constant).
4. **No combustion** — fuel cut-off is complete and instantaneous.
5. **Dynamometer and cranking motor are fully decoupled** — they add no parasitic load.
6. **Cylinder lubrication is adequate** — otherwise friction would be artificially high at low speeds.

**Limitations (2 marks):**
1. **Friction varies with speed** — deceleration is not uniform, so a single value of `dN/dt` cannot represent the entire curve. A graphical approach is mandatory.
2. **Difficult to apply at low speeds** — at very low speeds, pumping losses dominate and lube starvation can occur, giving anomalous results.
3. **Cannot separate bearing friction from pumping loss** — only their sum is measured.
4. **Engine may stall before reaching zero** — at very low speeds the lubricant film breaks down and friction spikes, distorting the curve.
5. **Indicator diagrams (P-θ) are still needed** for IP — retardation test gives only FP.

**How Limitations are Addressed (2 marks):**
1. A **speed vs. friction-power curve** is plotted over the full operating range.
2. The value at **rated speed** (not at low speeds) is taken for `η_mech` calculation.
3. The **indicator diagram** is recorded simultaneously (using a pressure transducer or mechanical indicator) for an accurate `IP`.
4. The engine is **warmed up thoroughly** before the test to ensure steady-state lubrication.
5. The deceleration is averaged over a small speed band (e.g., 50 rpm) rather than relying on a single point.

---

**(b) A 4-stroke, single-cylinder diesel engine has a brake power of 4.5 kW at 1500 rpm. The moment of inertia of the flywheel is 5.0 kg·m². During a retardation test, the engine decelerated from 1500 rpm to 1450 rpm in 5.0 seconds. Compute: (i) the frictional power, (ii) the indicated power, and (iii) the mechanical efficiency. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data (1 mark):**
- Brake Power, `B.P. = 4.5 kW`
- Engine speed, `N = 1500 rpm`
- Moment of inertia, `I = 5.0 kg·m²`
- Speed drop, `dN = 1500 − 1450 = 50 rpm`
- Time interval, `dt = 5.0 s`

**Step (i) — Frictional Power P_f (3 marks)**

Compute deceleration:

$$\frac{dN}{dt} \;=\; \frac{50}{5.0} \;=\; 10 \;\; \text{rpm/s}$$

Compute angular velocity:

$$\omega \;=\; \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} \;=\; 50\pi \;=\; 157.08 \;\; \text{rad/s}$$

Compute angular deceleration:

$$\alpha \;=\; \frac{2\pi}{60} \times 10 \;=\; 1.0472 \;\; \text{rad/s}^2$$

Compute frictional torque:

$$T_f \;=\; I \cdot \alpha \;=\; 5.0 \times 1.0472 \;=\; 5.236 \;\; \text{N·m}$$

Compute frictional power:

$$P_f \;=\; T_f \cdot \omega \;=\; 5.236 \times 157.08 \;=\; 822.46 \;\; \text{W} \;=\; 0.8225 \;\; \text{kW}$$

$$\boxed{\,P_f \;\approx\; 0.822 \;\; \text{kW}\,}$$

**Step (ii) — Indicated Power IP (1 mark)**

$$I.P. \;=\; B.P. + F.P. \;=\; 4.5 + 0.822 \;=\; 5.322 \;\; \text{kW}$$

$$\boxed{\,I.P. \;\approx\; 5.322 \;\; \text{kW}\,}$$

**Step (iii) — Mechanical Efficiency η_mech (2 marks)**

$$\eta_{mech} \;=\; \frac{B.P.}{I.P.} \;=\; \frac{4.5}{5.322} \;=\; 0.8456$$

$$\boxed{\,\eta_{mech} \;\approx\; 84.56\,\%\,}$$

**Sanity check (optional):** A mechanical efficiency of ~85 % is realistic for a healthy single-cylinder, slow-speed diesel engine at rated load. **Verified — result is acceptable.**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors (loses 2 marks):** Forgetting to convert rpm → rad/s for `ω` and `α`. Always remember `ω = 2πN/60` and `α = (2π/60)·(dN/dt)`. Students who use `N` directly in the power formula get numerically wrong answers.
> 2. **Moment of Inertia Errors (loses 2 marks):** Mixing up solid-disc (`½mR²`) vs. rim (`mR²`) vs. radius-of-gyration (`mk²`) formulas. Always read the problem statement carefully — it usually states which to use.
> 3. **Confusing `dN/dt` sign (loses 1 mark):** The deceleration `dN/dt` is positive when computing the magnitude of friction power. Don't plug a negative value.
> 4. **Adding Pumping Loss separately (loses 1 mark):** Pumping loss is *included* in the measured friction power. Do not subtract or add anything else.
> 5. **Missing units in final answer (loses 0.5 mark):** Always state `kW` for power, `N·m` for torque, `rpm` for speed, `kg·m²` for inertia, `%` for efficiency.
> 6. **Using average dN/dt over the whole test (loses 2 marks):** The retardation curve is non-linear; you must use the **slope at the rated speed** (tangent method), not the average slope over the whole coast-down.

---

## 6. Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Retardation test = free deceleration after fuel cut-off → measures engine friction.
- **Also called:** Motoring test / Running-down test.
- **Core formula:** $P_f = \dfrac{4\pi^2}{3600\,000}\,I\cdot N\cdot\dfrac{dN}{dt}$ **(in kW)**
- **Slope dN/dt** must be measured at **rated speed** by drawing a tangent to the N–t curve.
- **Newton's rotational law:** `T_f = I·α`
- **Mechanical Efficiency:** $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{B.P.}{B.P. + F.P.}$
- **Indicated Power:** $I.P. = B.P. + F.P.$ (since fuel cut off, all IP becomes friction)
- **Inertia of flywheel:** `I = m·k²` (use `½ mR²` for solid disc, `mR²` for rim).
- **Procedure highlights:** Warm up → fuel cut-off → decouple starter → record N vs t → graph → tangent → compute.
- **Limitation:** Friction power varies with speed → take rated-speed value, not average.
- **Typical Result:** η_mech for a healthy 4-stroke diesel ≈ 80–88 %.
- **Engineering utility:** Used in marine engines, DG sets, locomotive diesels, automotive R&D.
- **KTU exam weightage:** Usually 7–14 marks in Part B of ESE; 3 marks in Part A.
- **Always remember** the `4π²/3600` constant — it has cost many students their marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Flash Point and Fire Point of Various Fuels/Lubricants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGL508 - HEAT ENGINES LABORATORY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Flash Point and Fire Point of Various Fuels/Lubricants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

### Flash Point
The **Flash Point** of a fuel or lubricant is defined as the **lowest temperature** to which the substance must be heated (under specified test conditions) such that the vapour emitted from its surface momentarily **flashes (ignites) in the form of a transient flame** when a small test flame is passed across the surface. The flame extinguishes immediately because the vapour concentration is **insufficient to sustain continuous combustion**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Flash Point is a *physical property*, not a *chemical property*. It indicates the *volatility* and *fire hazard classification* of a hydrocarbon. Lower the flash point, higher the fire risk.

### Fire Point
The **Fire Point** is the **lowest temperature** at which the vapours of the substance **ignite and continue to burn steadily for at least 5 seconds** when a test flame is applied, under prescribed conditions. By definition, the **Fire Point is always greater than (or equal to) the Flash Point** for the same sample.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction:**
> - **Flash Point** → Vapour ignites momentarily (split-second blue flame).
> - **Fire Point** → Vapour ignites AND sustains combustion (≥ 5 seconds).
> The temperature difference (Fire Point − Flash Point) is typically **10 °C to 30 °C** for most petroleum oils.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Saucepan and the Stove" 🍳

Imagine heating milk in a pan on a gas stove:

| Stage | Real-World Analogy | Lab Concept |
|---|---|---|
| **Cold Milk** | No steam, no smell. | Below flash point — no flammable vapour. |
| **Just Before Boiling** | A faint puff of vapour; a match near it gives a tiny **whoosh** and goes out. | **Flash Point reached** — momentary ignition. |
| **Milk Boils** | Vapour is thick; a match **keeps the flame alive** for a few seconds. | **Fire Point reached** — sustained burning. |
| **Milky Spill on Stove** | A lit stove flame touches vapour — **a continuous flame erupts**. | Sustained combustion regime. |

> **Intuition Anchor:** Flash Point is the *match-test temperature* where vapours are *just ignitable*. Fire Point is the *match-test temperature* where they *keep burning*. The 5-second rule is the legally accepted "sustained burn" criterion in **IS 1448 / ASTM D93 / ASTM D92** standards.

---

## 1.3 Why Flash and Fire Point Matter in Engineering

- **Safety in Storage and Transport** – IMO (International Maritime Organization) classifies fuels by flash point: below **60 °C = Class I (dangerous)**, above **60 °C = Class II/III (safer)**.
- **Selection of Lubricants** – Lubricating oils used in **high-temperature bearings** (turbines, compressors) must have a fire point **at least 30–40 °C above the bulk operating temperature**, otherwise spontaneous combustion or bearing seizure can occur.
- **Legal Compliance** – Petroleum act (India), NFPA codes, and insurance policies mandate flash point declarations on every Material Safety Data Sheet (MSDS).
- **Crude Oil Refining** – Determines the **cutting point** in the distillation column; kerosene, diesel, and gasoline are separated based on the temperature at which vapours ignite.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** As per KTU 2024 Scheme, the student is expected to determine Flash Point and Fire Point using **Cleveland Open Cup (COC) apparatus** and/or **Abel / Pensky-Martens Closed Cup apparatus** and report corrected values.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| Standard Atmospheric Pressure | **101.325 kPa** = **760 mm of Hg** | Baseline for barometric correction |
| Test Flame Diameter | **4 mm ± 0.5 mm** | Standardised test flame size |
| Test Flame Sweep Duration | **1 second (Open Cup)** | Across cup, back and forth |
| Sustained Burn Criterion (Fire Point) | **≥ 5 seconds** | ASTM D92 / IS 1448 P:36 |
| Test Sample Volume (COC) | **70 mL** | Filled to marked level |
| Heating Rate (COC, near FP) | **5–6 °C per minute** | As per IS 1448 P:36 |
| Barometric Pressure Tolerance | **±2 kPa** | Required for acceptance of test |

---

## 1.5 Apparatus Family — The Three Industrial Standards

The KTU 2024 syllabus typically exposes students to **two of the three** standard test rigs:

1. **Abel Closed Cup Tester** — for low-flash petroleum products (Flash Point < 50 °C) e.g. kerosene, gasoline, light solvents.
2. **Pensky-Martens Closed Cup Tester** — for fuel oils, lubricating oils, diesel, bitumen (Flash Point 40 °C to 370 °C).
3. **Cleveland Open Cup (COC) Tester** — for lubricants, residual fuel oils, heavy oils, crankcase oils (Flash Point > 79 °C).

> **The fundamental difference:** *Closed cup* captures the vapour above the liquid (saturated vapour-mix) — gives a **lower flash point** because vapour-air mixture is rich. *Open cup* allows vapour to drift away — gives a **higher, but more realistic** value for open storage/spill scenarios.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physical Chemistry Behind Flash Point

### 2.1.1 Vapour Pressure Theory
A liquid continuously emits molecules from its surface into the surrounding atmosphere. At any given temperature, the **partial pressure of the vapour** is governed by the **Clausius-Clapeyron relation**:

$$
\ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\frac{L}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)
$$

Where:
- $P_1, P_2$ = Vapour pressures at absolute temperatures $T_1, T_2$ (in K)
- $L$ = Latent heat of vaporisation (J/mol)
- $R$ = Universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**

> As temperature rises, **vapour pressure rises exponentially**. Flash point is reached when the vapour concentration in air equals the **Lower Flammability Limit (LFL)** of that hydrocarbon.

### 2.1.2 Flammability Triangle
For combustion to occur, three conditions must be satisfied **simultaneously**:

```
        🔥 IGNITION SOURCE
            /\
           /  \
          /    \
         / FLASH\
        / POINT  \
       /__________\
   FUEL  \      /  OXYGEN
  (Vapour)\    /  (Air, 21%)
           \  /
            \/
       HEAT (Temperature)
```

When the test flame is applied:
1. **Fuel** → Vapour above the oil at concentration $\geq$ LFL.
2. **Oxygen** → Atmospheric air is naturally available.
3. **Heat** → A 4 mm test flame at $\approx 1000$ °C acts as the activator.

If any one of the three is missing or below threshold, no flash occurs. This is why flash point rises when the **air-fuel mixture is too rich** (vapour concentration above UFL) — a subtle but important concept for board exams.

---

## 2.2 Closed Cup vs Open Cup — Physics of Vapour Accumulation

| Feature | Closed Cup (Abel / PMCC) | Open Cup (COC) |
|---|---|---|
| Vapour-air space | **Enclosed** — saturated mixture | **Open** — continuous mixing with atmosphere |
| Measured FP | **Lower** (typically 5–15 °C less) | **Higher** |
| Sample stirring | **Yes**, with stirrer | **No stirrer** in basic apparatus |
| Real-world simulation | Storage tanks, sealed containers | Open sumps, spills, open reservoirs |
| Standard reference | IS 1448 (P-20) Abel, (P-21) PM | IS 1448 (P-36) COC, ASTM D92 |

> **Why is the closed cup flash point lower?**
> In a closed cup, vapours accumulate and reach **Lower Flammable Limit (LFL)** sooner because there is no convective escape. In an open cup, vapours drift away and get diluted with ambient air, requiring a higher temperature (and higher vapour pressure) to reach LFL.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every 14-mark question.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{\text{corrected}} = T_{\text{observed}} + 0.25 \,(101.3 - P)$ | **Abel Closed Cup correction** (P in kPa) | °C |
| 2 | $T_{\text{corrected}} = T_{\text{observed}} + 0.033 \,(760 - P_{\text{mmHg}})$ | **COC correction** (P in mm Hg) | °C |
| 3 | $T_{\text{corrected}} = T_{\text{observed}} + 0.25 \,(101.3 - P)$ | **Pensky-Martens correction** (P in kPa) | °C |
| 4 | $\text{Mean FP} = \dfrac{T_1 + T_2}{2}$ | Mean of two successive trials (within 2 °C) | °C |
| 5 | $\text{Repeatability} \leq 2\,°\text{C}$ | Two consecutive readings should not differ by more than 2 °C | °C |
| 6 | $\text{Reproducibility} \leq 4\,°\text{C}$ | Two lab results on same sample | °C |
| 7 | $\text{Safety Margin} = T_{\text{fire point}} - T_{\text{flash point}}$ | Indicates the **sustained combustion** gap | °C |
| 8 | $\Delta H_{\text{vap}} = R \cdot T_b \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{P_b}{P_a}\right)$ | Clausius-Clapeyron (alternate form) | J/mol |
| 9 | $LFL \approx 0.6 \text{ to } 1.4\% \text{ v/v}$ (gasoline) | Lower Flammability Limit (concentration) | % |
| 10 | $T_{\text{safe storage}} \geq T_{\text{flash point}} + 20\,°\text{C}$ | Design rule-of-thumb for bulk oil storage | °C |

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In the table above, all absolute value and difference operations are written with parentheses $(P)$ or parentheses around expressions, NEVER with vertical pipes `|`, to preserve markdown table integrity.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

### 2.4.1 Lubricant Selection for Steam Turbines
- Operating bulk oil temperature: **60 °C**
- Required fire point (by ASTM / OEM standard): **$\geq 200 °C$** for fire-resistant turbine oils.
- Common specification: ISO VG 32 / VG 46 with **FM-approved fire resistance**.

### 2.4.2 Diesel Engine Lubrication
- Crankcase oil pool temperature: **90–110 °C**
- Flash point of fresh SAE 15W-40 lubricant: **220–240 °C**
- A drop in flash point below **160 °C** indicates **fuel dilution** in the oil — a critical engine failure precursor.

### 2.4.3 Aviation Turbine Fuel (Jet A-1)
- Specification (ASTM D1655): Flash Point **$\geq 38 °C$** (Abel closed cup).
- Strict requirement because fuel is stored in wing tanks that can heat up on tarmac.

---

## 2.5 Why Both Flash AND Fire Point Are Reported

Most petroleum products show a small gap (10–30 °C) between Flash and Fire Point. However, **some synthetic fluids** (e.g., silicone oils) can show a *very large* gap because their vapour chemistry is unusual. For fire safety, the **Fire Point** is the more conservative and meaningful value — it indicates the temperature at which the substance will continue to burn **without any external flame** once ignition has occurred.

| Lubricant Type | Typical Flash Point | Typical Fire Point | Gap |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mineral oil (SAE 30) | 220 °C | 250 °C | 30 °C |
| Synthetic ester (jet engine oil) | 240 °C | 280 °C | 40 °C |
| Silicone oil (polydimethylsiloxane) | 300 °C | 380 °C+ | 80 °C+ |
| Used engine oil (degraded) | 150 °C | 190 °C | 40 °C |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Procedure & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Standard Test Procedure — Cleveland Open Cup (COC) Apparatus

> This is the **most commonly tested procedure** for KTU 2024 Scheme. Master the steps and you master the question.

### 3.1.1 Pre-Test Setup

1. **Clean the cup** thoroughly with a suitable solvent (acetone or petroleum ether) to remove residual oil film. Allow it to dry completely.
2. **Calibrate the thermometer** — ensure it is accurate to ± 1 °C against an ice-water bath (0 °C) and boiling water bath (100 °C at local barometric pressure).
3. **Record the barometric pressure** $P$ to within **± 0.1 kPa** using a Fortin-type barometer.
4. **Fill the cup** with the oil sample up to the inside scribed line — this corresponds to a sample volume of approximately **70 mL**. Do not overfill; the oil surface must be at the level mark exactly.
5. **Place the cup** on the heating plate; fix the thermometer in the holder such that the bulb is **immersed to 6 mm below the oil surface** but does not touch the cup bottom.

### 3.1.2 Heating Phase

6. Light the **pilot flame** of the test flame assembly and adjust its diameter to **4 mm ± 0.5 mm**. (This test flame is the *ignition source* in the flammability triangle.)
7. Begin heating the oil at a rate of **5–6 °C per minute**, with continuous **stirring** (if the apparatus has a stirrer — for COC, a stirring device is optional but recommended for uniformity).

### 3.1.3 Flash Point Determination Phase

8. At approximately **40 °C below the expected flash point** (estimated from prior knowledge — for unused lubricants, this is usually around 180–200 °C), reduce the heating rate to **2 °C per minute**.
9. **Sweep the test flame across the cup** at intervals of every **2 °C rise** in temperature. The flame should pass in a straight line across the diameter of the cup, taking **1 second per pass**.
10. The temperature at which a **distinct flash appears** on the surface of the oil (a brief blue flicker that immediately extinguishes) is recorded as the **Observed Flash Point**, $T_{o,\text{FP}}$.

> [!NOTE]
> A "flash" is identified by a **light blue luminous flame** that propagates over the entire oil surface. A misreading (false flash) is usually caused by steam puff or by the test flame itself being too large.

### 3.1.4 Fire Point Determination Phase

11. **Continue heating** the sample at the reduced rate of 2 °C per minute, sweeping the test flame every 2 °C as before.
12. The temperature at which the vapours **ignite and continue to burn for at least 5 seconds** after the test flame is removed is recorded as the **Observed Fire Point**, $T_{o,\text{FireP}}$.

### 3.1.5 Repeatability Check

13. **Repeat the test** on a fresh sample. For the result to be valid:
    - Difference between two successive flash point readings: $\leq 2 °C$ (repeatability criterion).
    - If difference exceeds 2 °C, perform a **third trial**.
    - Report the **mean** of the two acceptable trials.

### 3.1.6 Post-Test Procedure

14. **Extinguish the pilot flame** and **turn off the heater**.
15. Allow the cup to cool to below **50 °C** before cleaning for the next sample.
16. **Record observations** in a tabular format (Section 3.3).

---

## 3.2 Barometric Pressure Correction — Derivation

### 3.2.1 The Underlying Principle
The flash point depends on the **partial pressure of vapour** reaching the lower flammable limit in air. At lower barometric pressures (e.g., hill stations, aircraft), the same vapour mass corresponds to a *higher volume fraction*, so the flash point is reached at a *lower temperature*. The reverse is true at higher pressures.

The empirical correction equation is:

$$
T_{\text{corrected}} = T_{\text{observed}} + 0.25\,(101.3 - P)
$$

Where:
- $T$ is in **°C**
- $P$ is in **kPa**
- $101.3 \text{ kPa} = 760 \text{ mm Hg} = 1 \text{ atm}$

### 3.2.2 Conversion Between Units

If barometric pressure is measured in **mm Hg**:

$$
P_{\text{kPa}} = P_{\text{mmHg}} \times 0.13332
$$

The equivalent formula in mm Hg units becomes:

$$
T_{\text{corrected}} = T_{\text{observed}} + 0.033\,(760 - P_{\text{mmHg}})
$$

### 3.2.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Given:**
- Observed Flash Point by COC = **215 °C**
- Observed Fire Point by COC = **245 °C**
- Barometric pressure = **735 mm Hg** (a low-pressure day, e.g., monsoon)

**Step 1: Identify the correction formula**

We use the mm Hg version since pressure is given in mm Hg.

$$
T_{\text{corrected}} = T_{\text{observed}} + 0.033\,(760 - P_{\text{mmHg}})
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the barometric value**

$$
T_{\text{corrected}} = T_{\text{observed}} + 0.033\,(760 - 735)
$$

$$
T_{\text{corrected}} = T_{\text{observed}} + 0.033 \times 25
$$

**Step 3: Compute the correction term**

$$
0.033 \times 25 = 0.825\,°\text{C} \approx 0.83\,°\text{C}
$$

**Step 4: Apply to both flash and fire points**

For Flash Point:

$$
T_{\text{corr, FP}} = 215 + 0.83 = 215.83\,°\text{C}
$$

For Fire Point:

$$
T_{\text{corr, FireP}} = 245 + 0.83 = 245.83\,°\text{C}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Because the atmospheric pressure was *lower* than standard (735 < 760), the corrected temperatures are *higher* than observed — the air was thinner, so the oil needed to be heated *more* to reach the same vapour-air mixture ratio.

---

## 3.3 Standard Observation Table

| S.No. | Trial | Time (min) | Temperature (°C) | Test Flame Applied? | Result (Flash / Fire / No Flash) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Trial 1 | 0 | 30 | No | Heating initiated |
| 2 | | 5 | 60 | No | No flash |
| 3 | | 10 | 100 | No | No flash |
| ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |
| 12 | Trial 1 | 24 | 210 | Yes | No flash |
| 13 | | 24.5 | 212 | Yes | **FLASH — momentary** ← **Observed FP = 212 °C** |
| 14 | | 25 | 214 | Yes | No flash |
| 15 | | 25.5 | 216 | Yes | No flash |
| ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |
| 20 | | 28 | 240 | Yes | Vapour ignites, dies in 2 s |
| 21 | | 28.5 | 242 | Yes | Vapour ignites, **sustains 5+ s** ← **Observed FireP = 242 °C** |
| 22 | Trial 2 | 30 | 213 | (repeat) | Flash observed |
| ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | **Mean FP = (212+213)/2 = 212.5 °C** |

---

## 3.4 Calculation of Mean Corrected Value (Two Trials)

Let the two accepted observed flash points be $T_{1}$ and $T_{2}$ (with $\vert T_1 - T_2 \vert \leq 2\,°\text{C}$):

$$
T_{\text{mean}} = \frac{T_1 + T_2}{2}
$$

$$
T_{\text{corrected}} = T_{\text{mean}} + \Delta T_{\text{baro}}
$$

Where $\Delta T_{\text{baro}}$ is the barometric correction (positive if local pressure is *below* standard, negative if *above*).

---

## 3.5 Comparison of Three Apparatus — Analytical Decision Matrix

| Parameter | Abel Closed Cup | Pensky-Martens Closed Cup | Cleveland Open Cup |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sample Application** | Petroleum spirit, kerosene, gasoline (FP < 50 °C) | Diesel, fuel oil, lube oil, bitumen | Lubricants, residual fuel oil, crankcase oil |
| **Operating FP Range** | 5 °C to 65 °C | 40 °C to 370 °C | 80 °C to 400 °C |
| **Standard Code** | IS 1448 (P-20) / IP 170 | IS 1448 (P-21) / ASTM D93 | IS 1448 (P-36) / ASTM D92 |
| **Stirring** | Continuous, automatic | Continuous, automatic | Manual (optional) |
| **Test Flame Sweep** | Every 1 °C rise | Every 2 °C rise | Every 2 °C rise |
| **Heating Rate (Near FP)** | 1 °C / 0.5 min | 5–6 °C/min, then 2 °C near FP | 5–6 °C/min, then 2 °C near FP |
| **Vapour Space** | Sealed (closed) | Sealed with sliding shutter | Open to atmosphere |
| **Repeatability** | ± 1 °C | ± 2 °C | ± 2 °C |
| **Reproducibility** | ± 2 °C | ± 4 °C | ± 4 °C |
| **FP/FireP Gap** | Same principle | Same principle | Slightly higher FP than PMCC for same oil |
| **Cost** | Lowest | Medium | Lowest |

> **Key Takeaway for KTU 2024 Exam:** A common 14-mark question asks the student to *justify the choice of apparatus for a given sample*. The decision is based on:
> 1. The expected flash point range of the sample.
> 2. Whether the sample will be used in open or closed systems.
> 3. The applicable IS / ASTM standard.

---

## 3.6 Source of Error and Standard Uncertainty

| Source of Error | Magnitude | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Sample contamination | Variable | Clean cup, fresh sample |
| Heating rate fluctuation | ± 1 °C | Use PID-controlled heater |
| Barometer inaccuracy | ± 0.1 kPa | Use calibrated Fortin barometer |
| Test flame size variation | ± 0.5 mm | Adjust to 4 mm using gauge |
| Operator timing of flame sweep | ± 0.5 s | Use metronome / digital timer |
| Thermometer lag | ± 0.5 °C | Use ASTM certified thermometer |
| Atmospheric drafts (COC) | ± 2 °C | Conduct in a draught-free enclosure |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For the lab report, include the calculation of expanded uncertainty** (95% confidence level) using $U = k \cdot u_c$ where $u_c$ is the combined standard uncertainty. KTU examiners appreciate seeing this level of rigour in the "Result and Discussion" section.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Cleveland Open Cup Apparatus

The following Mermaid diagram maps the physical components and their functional roles in the COC test rig. All node IDs are purely alphanumeric (no reserved keywords) and labels are clean uppercase text.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PWR[DC Power Supply 230V AC] --> HTR[Heating Plate 1kW]
    HTR --> CUP[Brass Test Cup 70mL]
    CUP --> OIL[Oil Sample 70mL Level]
    OIL --> TH1[Thermometer 1 Bulb 6mm Below Surface]
    TH1 --> DSP1[Digital Temperature Display 1]
    TH1 --> DSP2[Digital Temperature Display 2]
    CUP --> TH2[Thermometer 2 Bulb in Vapour Space]
    GAS[Gas Cylinder LPG] --> PILOT[Pilot Flame Burner]
    PILOT --> TF[Test Flame 4mm Diameter]
    TF --> SWEEP[Manual Sweep Lever]
    CUP --> FRAME[Steel Frame Stand]
    PWR --> SWEEP
    PWR --> PILOT
    OIL --> VAP[Vapour Layer]
    VAP --> FLASH[Flash Point Event]
    FLASH --> OBS[Observer Eye]
    PWR --> BARO[Barometer Sensor]
    BARO --> DATA[Data Logger PC]

    classDef hw fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4,color:#0B2545
    classDef obs fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#D97706,color:#7C2D12
    classDef elec fill:#EAF7EA,stroke:#2CA02C,color:#14532D
    class PWR,HTR,CUP,OIL,FRAME hw
    class TH1,TH2,DSP1,DSP2,BARO,DATA elec
    class GAS,PILOT,TF,SWEEP,VAP,FLASH,OBS obs
```

---

## 4.2 Procedural Flow — Stepwise Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Clean Cup and Fill 70mL Sample] --> S2[Set Up Thermometer Bulb 6mm Below Oil Surface]
    S2 --> S3[Light Pilot Flame 4mm Diameter]
    S3 --> S4[Heat at 5 to 6 C per Minute with Stirring]
    S4 --> S5{Temperature Approaching Expected FP}
    S5 -- No --> S4
    S5 -- Yes Approx 40 C Below --> S6[Reduce Heating Rate to 2 C per Minute]
    S6 --> S7[Sweep Test Flame Every 2 C]
    S7 --> S8{Flash Observed?}
    S8 -- No --> S7
    S8 -- Yes --> S9[Record Observed Flash Point T1]
    S9 --> S10[Continue Heating Sweep Every 2 C]
    S10 --> S11{Sustained Burn 5 Seconds?}
    S11 -- No --> S10
    S11 -- Yes --> S12[Record Observed Fire Point T2]
    S12 --> S13[Cool Cup and Repeat Trial 2]
    S13 --> S14{Repeatability T1a minus T1b within 2 C?}
    S14 -- No --> S13
    S14 -- Yes --> S15[Compute Mean Observed Flash Point]
    S15 --> S16[Apply Barometric Correction 0.033 x 760 minus P]
    S16 --> S17[Report Final Corrected Flash and Fire Points]
    S17 --> S18[End of Experiment]

```
