<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. Define Flash Point and Fire Point. Why is the fire point always greater than the flash point? [3 Marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Model Answer:**

**Flash Point** is the lowest temperature at which the vapour of a combustible liquid momentarily ignites (flashes) when a small external test flame is brought near its surface, under prescribed test conditions. The flash is **transient** because the vapour concentration, while above the **lower flammable limit (LFL)**, is not yet sufficient to sustain continuous combustion.

**Fire Point** is the lowest temperature at which the vapour ignites and **continues to burn** for at least 5 seconds after the test flame is removed. It represents the temperature at which the vapour production rate is high enough to feed a self-sustaining flame.

**Why fire point > flash point:**
At the flash point, the rate of vapour generation is just enough to reach LFL, but **heat losses to the surroundings and heat absorbed in vaporising further oil** quench the flame. As temperature rises further, the vapour generation rate exceeds the loss rate, and combustion becomes **self-sustaining** — this is the fire point.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write that fire point is "where the oil catches fire permanently". Use the precise phrase *"continues to burn for at least 5 seconds after removal of the test flame"* — the 5-second criterion carries one full mark in KTU valuation.

---

### Q2. List any three differences between a Closed Cup and an Open Cup flash point apparatus. [3 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Closed Cup (Abel / Pensky-Martens) | Open Cup (Cleveland) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Vapour is **trapped in a sealed headspace**, so the air-vapour mixture reaches LFL at a lower temperature. | Vapour is **free to escape** into the atmosphere, requiring a higher temperature. |
| 2 | Gives a **lower** measured flash point (typically 5–15 °C less than COC for the same oil). | Gives a **higher** measured flash point. |
| 3 | Simulates **storage tanks and sealed containers** (closed system). | Simulates **open sumps, spills and reservoirs** (open system). |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Do not say "closed cup is safer" — it is the *opposite*; a closed cup gives a *lower* FP, hence **more hazardous** to handle. Examiners penalise this misconception.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — KTU University Exam, May 2024

**(a)** Describe the Cleveland Open Cup (COC) method for determination of flash and fire point of a lubricating oil, with a neat labelled sketch. Mention the applicable IS standard. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:** A known volume (70 mL) of the oil is heated in an open brass cup at a controlled rate, and a test flame (4 mm diameter) is passed over the surface at regular temperature intervals. The temperatures at which a momentary flash and a sustained burn (≥ 5 s) occur are recorded as the flash and fire points respectively.

**Apparatus Required (neat sketch — see Section 4.1):**
1. Cleveland Open Cup tester (brass cup, heating plate, frame).
2. ASTM 11C thermometer (range −6 °C to 400 °C, accuracy ± 1 °C).
3. Test flame assembly (pilot + 4 mm nozzle).
4. Fortin barometer or digital pressure sensor.
5. Stopwatch and stirrer.

**Applicable IS Standard:** **IS 1448 (P-36): 2017** / **ASTM D92-18**.

**Procedure (stepwise):**
1. Clean the cup with solvent, dry it, and fill with oil up to the scribed mark (70 mL).
2. Place the thermometer so the bulb is 6 mm below the oil surface, not touching the cup.
3. Light the pilot flame; adjust test flame diameter to 4 mm.
4. Heat the oil at 5–6 °C/min with gentle stirring.
5. At about 40 °C below the expected flash point, reduce the heating rate to 2 °C/min.
6. Sweep the test flame across the cup every 2 °C, taking 1 second per pass.
7. Record the temperature at which a **distinct momentary blue flash** appears over the oil surface — this is the **observed flash point**.
8. Continue heating; record the temperature at which the vapour **ignites and burns for at least 5 seconds** after flame removal — this is the **observed fire point**.
9. Repeat the test on a fresh sample; report the mean if the two readings differ by ≤ 2 °C.
10. Apply the barometric correction: $T_c = T_o + 0.033\,(760 - P_{\text{mmHg}})$.

**Precautions:**
- Cup must be perfectly clean; residue from previous samples causes false-low readings.
- Maintain draught-free environment (COC is sensitive to air currents).
- Heating rate must be uniform.
- Test flame must be exactly 4 mm diameter.

---

**(b)** In a COC test, the observed flash point of an engine lubricating oil was 218 °C and the fire point was 246 °C. The barometric pressure during the test was 745 mm Hg. Determine the **corrected flash point, corrected fire point, and the safety margin**. State whether the oil is suitable for use in a crankcase operating at 95 °C bulk temperature. **[7 Marks]**

**Given:**
- Observed Flash Point, $T_{o,\text{FP}} = 218\,°\text{C}$
- Observed Fire Point, $T_{o,\text{FireP}} = 246\,°\text{C}$
- Barometric pressure, $P = 745 \text{ mm Hg}$

**Step 1: Apply the barometric correction formula for COC**

$$
T_c = T_o + 0.033\,(760 - P_{\text{mmHg}})
$$

**Step 2: Calculate the correction term**

$$
\Delta T = 0.033 \times (760 - 745) = 0.033 \times 15 = 0.495\,°\text{C} \approx 0.5\,°\text{C}
$$

**Step 3: Compute corrected flash point**

$$
T_{c,\text{FP}} = 218 + 0.5 = 218.5\,°\text{C}
$$

**Step 4: Compute corrected fire point**

$$
T_{c,\text{FireP}} = 246 + 0.5 = 246.5\,°\text{C}
$$

**Step 5: Compute the safety margin**

$$
\text{Safety Margin} = T_{\text{FireP}} - T_{\text{FP}} = 246.5 - 218.5 = 28\,°\text{C}
$$

**Step 6: Assess suitability for crankcase operation at 95 °C**

The rule-of-thumb for safe lubricant operation is:

$$
T_{\text{bath}} \leq T_{\text{FP}} - 30\,°\text{C} \text{ to } 40\,°\text{C}
$$

Check:

$$
T_{\text{FP}} - 30 = 218.5 - 30 = 188.5\,°\text{C}
$$

Since the bulk operating temperature (95 °C) is **far below 188.5 °C**, the oil is **safe and suitable** for the crankcase application.

**Result Summary:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Corrected Flash Point | **218.5 °C** |
| Corrected Fire Point | **246.5 °C** |
| Safety Margin | **28 °C** |
| Suitability for 95 °C crankcase | **YES — Highly Suitable** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Many students forget to apply the barometric correction, losing 2 marks.
> - Some students compute $T_{c,\text{FP}} - T_{c,\text{FireP}}$ with a **negative sign** (inverting the order). The safety margin is *Fire − Flash*, always positive.
> - Some students wrongly say the oil is *not suitable* if the safety margin is "only 28 °C". The 28 °C gap is a *molecular property* of the oil, **not** related to operating temperature suitability. Use the rule-of-thumb: bulk oil temperature must be at least 30–40 °C below the flash point.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** — KTU University Exam, Dec 2023

**(a)** Explain the **Pensky-Martens Closed Cup (PMCC)** method for determination of flash point of a fuel oil. Compare it with the **Abel closed cup** method and state **which fuel is most appropriate for each**. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Pensky-Martens Closed Cup (PMCC) Method:**

**Principle:** A 70 mL sample is heated in a closed brass cup with an **integrated stirrer** and a sliding shutter. A test flame is introduced through the shutter opening at prescribed temperature intervals.

**Apparatus (per IS 1448 P-21 / ASTM D93):**
- Brass test cup with airtight lid.
- Stirring mechanism (rotates at 90–120 rpm).
- Pilot flame + 4 mm test flame on a swivel arm.
- Thermometer (ASTM 9C, range −5 to 110 °C, or 10C for higher range).
- Heating bath (air or liquid).

**Procedure:**
1. Fill cup with sample to the mark.
2. Insert thermometer, close the lid.
3. Heat at 5–6 °C/min, with continuous stirring.
4. At expected flash point −23 °C, reduce rate to 1 °C/min.
5. Open the shutter, dip the test flame for 0.5 s — every 1 °C rise.
6. The temperature at which a **distinct flash** is observed inside the cup is the **closed cup flash point**.

**Comparison Table — PMCC vs Abel:**

| Parameter | PMCC | Abel |
|---|---|---|
| Standard | IS 1448 P-21 / ASTM D93 | IS 1448 P-20 / IP 170 |
| Typical FP Range | 40 to 370 °C | 5 to 65 °C |
| Sample Volume | 70 mL | 75 mL |
| Stirring | Yes, 90–120 rpm | Yes, automatic |
| Heating Bath | Air bath or liquid bath | Water bath |
| Test Flame Dip | 0.5 s | 0.5 s |
| Repeatability | ± 2 °C | ± 1 °C |
| Application | Diesel, lube oil, fuel oil, bitumen | Kerosene, gasoline, petroleum spirit, naphtha |

**Sample-Apparatus Mapping:**

| Fuel Type | Expected FP (°C) | Recommended Apparatus |
|---|---|---|
| Gasoline (Petrol) | −43 | **Abel** (low FP) |
| Kerosene | 38 – 72 | **Abel** (low-to-medium FP) |
| Diesel | 55 – 100 | **PMCC** |
| Lubricating oil | 200 – 250 | **COC** (or PMCC) |
| Bitumen | 250+ | **COC** |
| Crude oil | Variable | **PMCC** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse "Abel" with "Abel-Pensky" (which is sometimes used loosely). The **Abel apparatus is exclusively for low-flash products (FP < 65 °C)**. PMCC is for medium-to-high flash products. Examiners deduct marks if you recommend Abel for diesel.

---

**(b)** In an Abel closed cup test, the observed flash point of a kerosene sample was 42 °C. The barometric pressure was 99.0 kPa. Calculate the **corrected flash point** and comment on the **storage safety classification** as per IMO. **[7 Marks]**

**Given:**
- Observed Flash Point, $T_o = 42\,°\text{C}$
- Barometric pressure, $P = 99.0 \text{ kPa}$

**Step 1: Apply the Abel closed cup correction**

$$
T_c = T_o + 0.25\,(101.3 - P)
$$

**Step 2: Compute the correction term**

$$
\Delta T = 0.25 \times (101.3 - 99.0) = 0.25 \times 2.3 = 0.575\,°\text{C} \approx 0.58\,°\text{C}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the corrected flash point**

$$
T_c = 42 + 0.58 = 42.58\,°\text{C}
$$

**Step 4: IMO Storage Safety Classification**

As per the **International Maritime Organization (IMO) MARPOL Annex I** and the **International Bulk Chemical Code**:

- **Dangerous Goods Class 3** (Flammable Liquids) is sub-classified by flash point:
  - **Class 3.1 (High hazard):** Flash Point **< −18 °C** — e.g., gasoline, ethyl ether.
  - **Class 3.2 (Medium hazard):** Flash Point **−18 °C to 23 °C** — e.g., some specialty solvents.
  - **Class 3.3 (Low hazard):** Flash Point **23 °C to 60 °C** — e.g., **kerosene, diesel**.
  - **Class 3.4 (Lowest hazard):** Flash Point **> 60 °C** — e.g., fuel oil, lubricating oil.

**Step 5: Classification of the kerosene sample**

Since the corrected flash point is **42.58 °C**, which lies in the range **23 °C to 60 °C**, the kerosene falls under **Class 3.3 — Low Hazard Flammable Liquid**.

**Storage Implications:**
- Bulk storage in **fixed roof tanks** with flame arrestors on vents.
- Tank farm spacing as per **NFPA 30 / IS 15683** (typically 1.5 × diameter of adjacent tank for Class 3.3).
- Bonding and earthing of all transfer lines.
- Vapour recovery system recommended for tanker loading operations.
- **Maximum allowable storage temperature:** $T_{\text{storage}} \leq T_{\text{FP}} - 5\,°\text{C} = 37.6\,°\text{C}$ (cooling may be required in tropical climates).

**Final Result:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Corrected Flash Point | **42.58 °C** |
| IMO Hazard Class | **Class 3.3 (Low hazard flammable liquid)** |
| Maximum Storage Temperature | **37.6 °C** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Forgetting to use **0.25** as the coefficient (Abel/PMCC) versus **0.033** (COC) — **this is a 2-mark deduction** in KTU valuation.
> - Confusing the barometric correction **sign** (whether to add or subtract). **Rule: if observed pressure < standard pressure, correction is positive; if pressure > standard, correction is negative.** Memorise this sign convention.
> - Citing *Petroleum Act 1934* (which is outdated) instead of *IMO MARPOL / IS 15683* in storage classification questions.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Rapid Revision Checklist (Print this!)**

### Definitions
- ☐ **Flash Point** → Lowest temperature where vapour *momentarily* ignites (transient blue flame).
- ☐ **Fire Point** → Lowest temperature where vapour *sustains* combustion for **≥ 5 seconds**.
- ☐ **Fire Point > Flash Point**, always (typical gap: 10–30 °C for mineral oils).

### Apparatus Selection
- ☐ **Abel CC** → Low FP fuels (gasoline, kerosene, petroleum spirit) — FP 5–65 °C.
- ☐ **Pensky-Martens CC** → Medium-to-high FP (diesel, lube oil, bitumen) — FP 40–370 °C.
- ☐ **Cleveland OC** → Lubricants, residual fuel oil — FP 80–400 °C.

### Key Standards
- ☐ **IS 1448 P-20** → Abel | **IS 1448 P-21** → PMCC | **IS 1448 P-36** → COC
- ☐ **ASTM D92** → COC | **ASTM D93** → PMCC | **ASTM D56** → Abel (Tag) (less common in India)

### Numerical Formulas (must memorise)
- ☐ Abel/PMCC correction: $T_c = T_o + 0.25\,(101.3 - P_{\text{kPa}})$
- ☐ COC correction: $T_c = T_o + 0.033\,(760 - P_{\text{mmHg}})$
- ☐ Mean = $(T_1 + T_2)/2$ with repeatability $\vert T_1 - T_2 \vert \leq 2\,°\text{C}$
- ☐ Safety margin = $T_{\text{FireP}} - T_{\text{FP}}$
- ☐ Safe storage: $T_{\text{bath}} \leq T_{\text{FP}} - 30 \text{ to } 40\,°\text{C}$

### Procedural Constants
- ☐ **Sample volume (COC/PMCC)** = 70 mL
- ☐ **Test flame diameter** = 4 mm ± 0.5 mm
- ☐ **Heating rate (near FP)** = 2 °C/min
- ☐ **Thermometer immersion** = 6 mm below oil surface
- ☐ **Sweep interval** = every 2 °C (COC/PMCC) or 1 °C (Abel)

### Safety & Hazard Classification
- ☐ IMO Class 3.1: FP < −18 °C | 3.2: −18 to 23 °C | 3.3: 23 to 60 °C | 3.4: > 60 °C
- ☐ Lubricant with **fire point < 200 °C** is **not suitable** for hot oil systems (turbines, compressors).

### Common Exam Mistakes to Avoid
- ☐ ❌ Using 0.033 correction for Abel (use 0.25).
- ☐ ❌ Forgetting to convert kPa ↔ mm Hg.
- ☐ ❌ Mixing up the sign of barometric correction.
- ☐ ❌ Saying "Cleveland is for low-flash fuels" — it is for **high-flash** fuels.
- ☐ ❌ Reporting fire point < flash point (impossible — recheck observations).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermal Conductivity Measurement Using Guarded Plate Apparatus
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGL508 - HEAT ENGINES LABORATORY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Thermal Conductivity Measurement Using Guarded Plate Apparatus

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Guarded Plate Apparatus** (also called the **Guarded Hot Plate Method** or **Poensgen Apparatus**) is a primary, absolute, steady-state experimental setup used to determine the **thermal conductivity (k)** of homogeneous, low-conductivity building and insulating materials (such as fiberglass, cork, foam, asbestos, plywood, and mineral wool) in the range of approximately **0.01 to 2 W/(m·K)**.

The method conforms to the standards laid down by **ASTM C177** and **IS 3346**, and is classified as a **primary method** because thermal conductivity is determined directly from the fundamental measurements of electrical power, area, temperature difference, and thickness, without needing a reference sample.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The guarded plate method is the *most reliable* steady-state technique for low-k materials. KTU examiners frequently test the principle, formula, and the role of the *guard heater* in eliminating lateral (edge) heat loss.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are trying to measure how fast heat leaks out of a thick concrete wall.

- You place a **flat electric heater** (a hot plate) sandwiched between **two identical slabs** of the material.
- The heater warms up. Heat tries to escape in **all directions** — up, down, and sideways.
- We **only want** the heat that flows straight up and straight down through the slabs (this is what we can easily calculate).
- So we surround the main heater with a **"guard ring" heater** that is kept at the **exact same temperature** as the main heater. 
- Because the guard is at the same temperature, heat has **no reason to flow sideways** — like having a fence at the same height that heat refuses to cross.
- This ensures a **purely one-dimensional** heat flow, and we can confidently apply Fourier's law.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a perfectly insulated coffee thermos. The vacuum between the walls prevents heat loss. The *guard heater* plays a similar role — it acts as a thermal "no-flow" boundary in the sideways direction, mimicking an infinite slab.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Steady-state temperature gradient** must be attained (typically **2–4 hours** of waiting).
- Standard specimen dimensions: **300 mm × 300 mm** or **600 mm × 600 mm**, thickness **25–50 mm**.
- Heat input is measured as the product of **voltage (V)** and **current (I)**: $Q = VI$.
- Thermal conductivity is reported in **W/(m·K)** or equivalently **W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Specimen Placement:** Two identical, flat specimens of the test material are placed on either side of a thin, square **main heater** (electrical resistance heating element).
2. **Cooling Plates:** The outer faces of both specimens are pressed against water-cooled (or air-cooled) **cold plates**, which act as heat sinks at a uniform low temperature.
3. **Guard Heater Activation:** A **guard heater** (a separate heating ring surrounding the main heater) is energized and its temperature is manually or automatically maintained **identical to the main heater** using thermocouples and differential temperature controllers.
4. **Establish Steady State:** Power is supplied. The system is allowed several hours until all temperatures become constant with time (dT/dt = 0), indicating **steady-state heat conduction**.
5. **One-Dimensional Heat Flow:** Because the guard heater eliminates lateral (radial) heat loss from the main heater edges, heat flows strictly **perpendicular** (axially) through both specimens.
6. **Measurement:** The electrical power consumed by the **main heater only** is recorded (Voltage × Current). The surface temperatures of the hot and cold faces of the specimen are measured using calibrated **thermocouples** or **RTDs**.
7. **Fourier's Law Application:** The thermal conductivity is computed using Fourier's one-dimensional conduction equation.

## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Step

- **Why two specimens?** — Placing one on each side of the heater ensures **symmetric heat flow**, doubling the heat conduction area and making the apparatus sensitive to lower conductivity values.
- **Why a guard heater?** — In any finite sample, heat naturally tries to flow sideways from the hot region to the cooler edges. This lateral loss would cause us to **over-estimate k**. The guard heater, kept isothermal with the main heater, ensures **zero temperature gradient** in the lateral direction, hence **no lateral heat flow**.
- **Why steady state?** — Fourier's law in its simple form $\dot{Q} = kA(\Delta T / L)$ is strictly valid only when the temperature at every point is time-invariant. Steady state guarantees a **linear temperature profile** through the specimen.
- **Why use thermocouples?** — They provide precise, differential temperature readings with minimal thermal mass and minimal disturbance to the temperature field.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains **every formula** you need for KTU Part A and Part B questions on this experiment.

| # | Formula Description | Mathematical Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Heat generated by main heater (electrical) | $Q_{in} = V \times I$ | Watts (W); V in Volts, I in Amps |
| 2 | Heat conducted through specimen (Fourier's Law) | $Q_{cond} = \dfrac{k \cdot A \cdot (T_h - T_c)}{L}$ | W; k in W/(m·K) |
| 3 | Thermal conductivity (working formula) | $k = \dfrac{Q_{cond} \cdot L}{A \cdot (T_h - T_c)}$ | W/(m·K) |
| 4 | Total heat through both specimens | $Q_{total} = 2 \times \dfrac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}{L}$ | W (factor of 2 for twin specimens) |
| 5 | Total area for heat flow (with guard correction) | $A = A_m + \dfrac{1}{2} A_g$ | m²; A_m = main heater area, A_g = guard heater area |
| 6 | Mean temperature of specimen | $T_{mean} = \dfrac{T_h + T_c}{2}$ | °C or K |
| 7 | Thermal resistance | $R_{th} = \dfrac{L}{k \cdot A}$ | K/W |
| 8 | Heat flux | $q = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | W/m² |
| 9 | Temperature gradient | $\dfrac{dT}{dx} = \dfrac{T_h - T_c}{L}$ | K/m |
| 10 | Power consumed by guard heater | $Q_{guard} = V_g \times I_g$ | W (not used in k calculation) |
| 11 | Temperature difference | $\Delta T = T_h - T_c$ | K or °C (same magnitude) |
| 12 | Heat loss (lateral) — should be zero | $Q_{lateral} = 0$ | By guard heater design |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The guarded plate apparatus is the **gold standard** in industry for:

- **Building material certification** — Characterizing insulation boards, aerogels, and foam panels used in energy-efficient buildings.
- **Cryogenic insulation** — Testing perlite, vacuum insulated panels for LNG tanks.
- **Aerospace thermal protection systems (TPS)** — Characterizing tiles and blankets used on spacecraft.
- **Refractory material testing** — Qualifying furnace linings.
- **Cold chain logistics** — Verifying the k of insulated shipping containers for pharmaceuticals and vaccines.

The values obtained are used as **input data** for finite element heat transfer simulations, HVAC design, and energy audits in **IEQ (Indoor Environmental Quality)** engineering.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Thermal Conductivity from Fourier's Law

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction (1-D, Steady State)

For a homogeneous, isotropic material of cross-sectional area $A$ and thickness $L$, with hot face at temperature $T_h$ and cold face at temperature $T_c$, the steady-state heat transfer rate is governed by:

$$
\dot{Q} = -k \cdot A \cdot \frac{dT}{dx}
$$

where:
- $\dot{Q}$ = heat transfer rate (W)
- $k$ = thermal conductivity of the material (W/(m·K))
- $A$ = cross-sectional area perpendicular to heat flow (m²)
- $dT/dx$ = temperature gradient in the direction of flow (K/m)
- The negative sign indicates heat flows from high to low temperature.

### 3.1.2 Integration Over the Specimen Thickness

Because we are in **steady state**, the temperature distribution through the specimen is **linear** (this is a direct consequence of d²T/dx² = 0 for constant k with no internal heat generation). Therefore, the gradient $dT/dx$ can be replaced by the finite difference:

$$
\frac{dT}{dx} = \frac{T_h - T_c}{L}
$$

### 3.1.3 Substituting Back

Inserting this into Fourier's law:

$$
\dot{Q} = k \cdot A \cdot \frac{T_h - T_c}{L}
$$

The negative sign is removed by enforcing the convention that $T_h > T_c$, so the heat flow is positive in magnitude from hot to cold.

### 3.1.4 Application to the Guarded Plate Apparatus

In the guarded plate apparatus, **two identical specimens** are placed on either side of the main heater. Half of the total electrical power $Q_{in}$ flows upward through the upper specimen, and half flows downward through the lower specimen. The heat conducted through **one specimen** is therefore:

$$
Q_{one} = \frac{Q_{in}}{2} = \frac{V \times I}{2}
$$

The area through which heat flows is the **metering area**, which consists of the main heater area plus a correction for the guard heater zone. The standard convention (per ASTM C177) is:

$$
A_{eff} = A_m + \frac{1}{2} A_g
$$

where $A_m$ is the main heater area and $A_g$ is the guard heater area. (The factor ½ arises because the temperature gradient transitions from the main-heater value to zero over the guard region, and on average the guard zone contributes half its area at full gradient.)

### 3.1.5 Solving for Thermal Conductivity

Equating the heat generated (per specimen) to the heat conducted (per specimen):

$$
\frac{V \times I}{2} = \frac{k \cdot A_{eff} \cdot (T_h - T_c)}{L}
$$

Solving for $k$:

$$
k = \frac{(V \times I) \cdot L}{2 \cdot A_{eff} \cdot (T_h - T_c)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> If the apparatus uses a single specimen (asymmetric design), the factor 2 is omitted and the formula becomes:
> $$ k = \frac{V \times I \cdot L}{A_{eff} \cdot (T_h - T_c)} $$

### 3.1.6 Worked Numerical Example

**Given:**
- Main heater: $A_m = 0.09 \text{ m} \times 0.09 \text{ m} = 0.0081 \text{ m}²$
- Guard heater: $A_g = 0.18 \text{ m} \times 0.18 \text{ m} - 0.09 \text{ m} \times 0.09 \text{ m} = 0.0243 \text{ m}²$
- Effective area: $A_{eff} = 0.0081 + 0.5 \times 0.0243 = 0.0081 + 0.01215 = 0.02025 \text{ m}²$
- Voltage $V = 30 \text{ V}$, Current $I = 1.5 \text{ A}$, so $Q_{in} = 45 \text{ W}$
- Hot face temperature: $T_h = 80 °C = 353.15 \text{ K}$
- Cold face temperature: $T_c = 30 °C = 303.15 \text{ K}$
- Specimen thickness: $L = 0.025 \text{ m}$ (25 mm)

**Step 1: Heat per specimen**

$$
Q_{one} = \frac{V \times I}{2} = \frac{45}{2} = 22.5 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 2: Temperature difference**

$$
\Delta T = T_h - T_c = 80 - 30 = 50 °C = 50 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 3: Plug into k formula**

$$
k = \frac{Q_{one} \cdot L}{A_{eff} \cdot \Delta T} = \frac{22.5 \times 0.025}{0.02025 \times 50}
$$

$$
k = \frac{0.5625}{1.0125} = 0.5556 \text{ W/(m·K)}
$$

**Step 4: Interpretation**

This value is consistent with materials such as **cork board (0.04–0.07 W/(m·K))** range or **firebrick (~0.5–1.0 W/(m·K))**. The result is reasonable for the experiment.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Energy Balance of the Apparatus

The following table breaks down the **energy in = energy out** equation for the guarded plate apparatus in steady state.

| Energy Inflow | = | Energy Outflow |
|---|---|---|
| Electrical power to main heater ($Q_m = V_m I_m$) | = | Heat conducted up through upper specimen ($Q_{up}$) + Heat conducted down through lower specimen ($Q_{down}$) |
| Electrical power to guard heater ($Q_g = V_g I_g$) | = | Heat conducted through guard zone of upper + lower specimens (laterally symmetric) |
| Total electrical input ($Q_{total} = Q_m + Q_g$) | = | Heat absorbed by cooling water in cold plates + Heat lost to ambient (should be ~0 with good insulation) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **guard heater is NOT used** in the k calculation. Its sole purpose is to **maintain a zero temperature gradient** in the lateral direction so that the heat flow through the main heater zone is strictly one-dimensional.

## 3.3 Guard Heater Effectiveness — Analytical Justification

Without the guard heater, a temperature drop would exist between the edge of the main heater and the surrounding (cooler) specimen region, causing lateral heat flow $Q_{lateral}$. This would mean the actual heat conducted vertically is *less* than the input $V \times I$, and we would incorrectly **over-estimate k** by using $Q = VI$ in the formula.

By forcing the guard heater temperature to match the main heater temperature, the lateral temperature gradient is **zeroed out**, ensuring:

$$
q_{lateral} = -k \frac{\partial T}{\partial r} = 0
$$

Hence, **all** the heat $V \times I$ is forced to flow axially — the condition for accurate measurement.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of the Guarded Plate Apparatus (Mermaid)

The following diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** and energy flow through the major subsystems of the apparatus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Power Supply Unit V and I] --> MH[Main Heater Square Plate]
    P --> GH[Guard Heater Ring Surrounding Main]
    MH --> TC1[Thermocouple Hot Face T_h]
    GH --> TC3[Thermocouple Guard Zone T_g]
    TC3 --> CTRL[Differential Temperature Controller]
    CTRL -->|Adjusts Guard Heater Power| GH
    TC1 --> DAQ[Data Acquisition and Display Unit]
    MH --> SP1[Upper Specimen Test Material]
    MH --> SP2[Lower Specimen Test Material]
    SP1 --> TC2[Thermocouple Cold Face T_c Upper]
    SP2 --> TC4[Thermocouple Cold Face T_c Lower]
    TC2 --> DAQ
    TC4 --> DAQ
    SP1 --> CP1[Upper Cooling Plate Water Circulated]
    SP2 --> CP2[Lower Cooling Plate Water Circulated]
    CP1 --> CW[Cold Water Inlet and Outlet Manifold]
    CP2 --> CW
    DAQ --> CALC[Thermal Conductivity Calculator k = Q L over A dT]
```

## 4.2 Component-Level Schematic (Mermaid)

The diagram below maps the **physical arrangement** of the apparatus components in cross-section.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TOP[Top Cooling Assembly]
        CW1[Cold Water Inlet]
        CP1A[Upper Cold Plate]
        CW1 --> CP1A
    end
    subgraph UPPER[Upper Specimen Zone]
        SP1A[Test Specimen Thickness L]
        TH1[Thermocouple T_c Upper]
    end
    subgraph CENTER[Heater Assembly]
        MH1[Main Heater Area A_m]
        GH1[Guard Heater Area A_g]
        TG1[Guard Thermocouple T_g]
    end
    subgraph LOWER[Lower Specimen Zone]
        SP2A[Test Specimen Thickness L]
        TH2[Thermocouple T_c Lower]
    end
    subgraph BOTTOM[Bottom Cooling Assembly]
        CP2A[Lower Cold Plate]
        CW2[Cold Water Outlet]
        CP2A --> CW2
    end
    TOP --> UPPER
    UPPER --> CENTER
    CENTER --> LOWER
    LOWER --> BOTTOM
    MH1 -.Hot Face T_h.-> UPPER
    GH1 --> TG1
    TG1 -->|Feedback Loop| GH1
```

## 4.3 Heat Flow Directional Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H[Heater Core] -->|Q upward| SU[Upper Specimen]
    H -->|Q downward| SL[Lower Specimen]
    GH2[Guard Heater] -->|Q lateral blocked| SU
    GH2 -->|Q lateral blocked| SL
    SU --> CU[Cold Plate Up]
    SL --> CL[Cold Plate Down]
    CU --> W1[Heat Absorbed by Water]
    CL --> W2[Heat Absorbed by Water]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Observation from the diagrams:** The **guard heater** is conceptually a "thermal fence" — it surrounds the main heater on the same plane and creates an isothermal boundary that prevents lateral heat escape. The cooling plates act as thermal anchors at a uniform cold temperature, providing a well-defined $\Delta T$ across each specimen.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define thermal conductivity. Why is the guarded plate apparatus classified as a primary method for measuring it?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Thermal conductivity ($k$) is defined as the ability of a material to conduct heat. Numerically, it is the rate of heat flow (in watts) through a unit area (1 m²) of a material of unit thickness (1 m) when the temperature difference between the two faces is 1 K. Units: W/(m·K).
- **Primary method (2 marks):** It is classified as a *primary* (or *absolute*) method because thermal conductivity is determined **directly** from the fundamental measurements of electrical power ($Q = VI$), geometry ($A$ and $L$), and temperature difference ($\Delta T$), **without requiring a reference material** of known thermal conductivity. The result is traceable to basic SI units.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is the role of the guard heater in the guarded plate apparatus?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The guard heater is a **separate heating element** that surrounds the main heater in the same plane.
- It is maintained at the **same temperature as the main heater** using thermocouples and a differential temperature controller.
- This creates an **isothermal boundary** in the lateral direction, which **eliminates radial (sideways) heat loss** from the main heater zone.
- As a result, heat flow is forced to be **strictly one-dimensional** (perpendicular to the specimen face), allowing accurate application of Fourier's law and correct measurement of thermal conductivity.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per the KTU 2024 scheme, Part B questions within a module carry **internal choice**. Both Question A and Question B below are **fully independent** — answer either one in full to score 14 marks.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]

**In a guarded plate apparatus, the following observations were recorded at steady state:**

- Voltage across main heater: $V = 24 \text{ V}$
- Current through main heater: $I = 1.25 \text{ A}$
- Main heater area: $A_m = 0.04 \text{ m}²$ (200 mm × 200 mm)
- Guard heater area: $A_g = 0.02 \text{ m}²$
- Hot face temperature of specimen: $T_h = 75 °C$
- Cold face temperature of specimen: $T_c = 25 °C$
- Specimen thickness: $L = 0.030 \text{ m}$ (30 mm)

**Calculate:**
- **(a) [7 marks]** The thermal conductivity of the test material.
- **(b) [7 marks]** The thermal resistance of one specimen and the heat flux through it.

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Thermal Conductivity [7 marks]**

**Step 1: Calculate the heat input to the main heater (1 mark)**
$$
Q_{in} = V \times I = 24 \times 1.25 = 30 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate the effective area (1 mark)**
$$
A_{eff} = A_m + \frac{1}{2} A_g = 0.04 + \frac{1}{2} \times 0.02 = 0.04 + 0.01 = 0.05 \text{ m}²
$$

**Step 3: Heat conducted through one specimen (1 mark)**
Since the main heater is sandwiched between two identical specimens, heat splits equally:
$$
Q_{one} = \frac{Q_{in}}{2} = \frac{30}{2} = 15 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate temperature difference (1 mark)**
$$
\Delta T = T_h - T_c = 75 - 25 = 50 \text{ K} \; (\text{or } 50 °C \text{ — same magnitude})
$$

**Step 5: Apply Fourier's Law to solve for $k$ (2 marks)**
$$
Q_{one} = \frac{k \cdot A_{eff} \cdot \Delta T}{L}
$$
$$
15 = \frac{k \times 0.05 \times 50}{0.030}
$$
$$
k = \frac{15 \times 0.030}{0.05 \times 50} = \frac{0.45}{2.5} = 0.18 \text{ W/(m·K)}
$$

**Step 6: Interpretation (1 mark)**
This value of $k = 0.18 \text{ W/(m·K)}$ is typical of materials such as **hard rubber, medium-density fiberboard, or low-grade brick**.

---

**Part (b) — Thermal Resistance and Heat Flux [7 marks]**

**Step 1: Thermal resistance formula (1 mark)**
$$
R_{th} = \frac{L}{k \cdot A_{eff}}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute values (2 marks)**
$$
R_{th} = \frac{0.030}{0.18 \times 0.05} = \frac{0.030}{0.009} = 3.333 \text{ K/W}
$$

**Step 3: Verification using $\Delta T = Q \times R_{th}$ (1 mark)**
$$
\Delta T = Q_{one} \times R_{th} = 15 \times 3.333 = 50 \text{ K} \; \checkmark \text{(Matches given value)}
$$

**Step 4: Heat flux formula (1 mark)**
$$
q = \frac{Q_{one}}{A_{eff}}
$$

**Step 5: Calculate heat flux (1 mark)**
$$
q = \frac{15}{0.05} = 300 \text{ W/m}²
$$

**Step 6: Physical significance (1 mark)**
A heat flux of **300 W/m²** through the specimen (with $\Delta T$ of 50 K) is characteristic of laboratory-scale insulation testing. The thermal resistance of **3.33 K/W** represents the insulation's ability to resist heat flow — a higher $R_{th}$ indicates a better insulator.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the working principle of the guarded plate apparatus for measuring thermal conductivity of insulating materials.

**(b) [7 marks]** A specimen of thermal insulation 50 mm thick is tested in a guarded plate apparatus. The metering area is 0.0625 m², hot face temperature is 90 °C, cold face temperature is 40 °C, and the power input to the main heater is 17.5 W (with two identical specimens, one on each side). Calculate the thermal conductivity of the material. Comment on the result.

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Working Principle with Block Diagram [7 marks]**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

Refer to the diagram from **Section 4.1** of these notes (Main Heater, Guard Heater, Specimens, Cooling Plates, Thermocouples, Power Supply, Data Acquisition, Controller feedback loop).

**Working Principle Explanation (4 marks):**

1. **Setup (1 mark):** Two identical specimens of the test material are placed on either side of a thin, square **main heater**. The outer faces of the specimens are in contact with water-cooled **cold plates**.

2. **Guard Heater Function (1 mark):** A **guard heater** (a separate ring-shaped heater) surrounding the main heater is energized and continuously maintained at the **same temperature as the main heater** via a differential temperature controller and thermocouples. This eliminates **lateral heat loss** and ensures **one-dimensional heat flow**.

3. **Steady State (1 mark):** Power is supplied until **steady state** is reached (typically 2–4 hours), at which point all temperatures become constant with time. Fourier's law for steady-state conduction can then be applied directly.

4. **Measurement and Calculation (1 mark):** The electrical power $Q = V \times I$ supplied to the main heater is measured, the steady-state hot and cold face temperatures $T_h$ and $T_c$ are recorded, and thermal conductivity is calculated as:
$$
k = \frac{Q \cdot L}{2 \cdot A_{eff} \cdot (T_h - T_c)}
$$
where $A_{eff} = A_m + \tfrac{1}{2}A_g$, the factor 2 accounts for the twin specimens.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation [7 marks]**

**Step 1: Given data (1 mark)**
- Thickness: $L = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.050 \text{ m}$
- Effective area: $A_{eff} = 0.0625 \text{ m}²$
- $T_h = 90 °C$, $T_c = 40 °C$
- $Q_{in} = 17.5 \text{ W}$ (main heater, total for both specimens)
- Two identical specimens

**Step 2: Heat per specimen (1 mark)**
$$
Q_{one} = \frac{Q_{in}}{2} = \frac{17.5}{2} = 8.75 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 3: Temperature difference (1 mark)**
$$
\Delta T = T_h - T_c = 90 - 40 = 50 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 4: Apply Fourier's Law (2 marks)**
$$
Q_{one} = \frac{k \cdot A_{eff} \cdot \Delta T}{L}
$$
$$
8.75 = \frac{k \times 0.0625 \times 50}{0.050}
$$

**Step 5: Solve for k (1 mark)**
$$
k = \frac{8.75 \times 0.050}{0.0625 \times 50} = \frac{0.4375}{3.125} = 0.14 \text{ W/(m·K)}
$$

**Step 6: Comment on the result (1 mark)**
A thermal conductivity of **0.14 W/(m·K)** is characteristic of **good-quality insulating materials** such as **corkboard, wood, or medium-grade fiberglass**. This value indicates the material is a **moderately good thermal insulator** suitable for building wall and roof insulation applications. Lower k values (e.g., 0.03–0.04 W/(m·K)) would indicate premium insulators like polyurethane foam or expanded polystyrene.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Examiners Penalize (Lose 1–2 marks per slip):**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** for twin specimens — students often use $Q = VI$ directly in the formula instead of $Q/2$. This **overestimates k by 100%**.
> 
> 2. **Using $A_m$ instead of $A_{eff} = A_m + \tfrac{1}{2}A_g$** — the guard heater zone contributes half its area. Using only $A_m$ **underestimates k**.
> 
> 3. **Mixing up temperature units** — $\Delta T$ in K and °C have the same magnitude for *differences*, but **never** convert T values to Kelvin before subtraction (the difference is the same in both scales).
> 
> 4. **Failing to wait for steady state** — recording temperatures before dT/dt = 0 leads to a **non-linear temperature profile**, violating Fourier's law assumptions.
> 
> 5. **Confusing the main heater and guard heater** — the guard heater's power is **not** included in the k calculation. It is purely a thermal boundary control.
> 
> 6. **Wrong thickness unit** — many students plug thickness in mm instead of m, causing k to be off by a factor of 1000.
> 
> 7. **Not stating units of final answer** — always write $k = 0.18 \text{ W/(m·K)}$ explicitly. KTU examiners often deduct ½ mark for missing units.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Guarded Plate Apparatus (Module Experiment 2)**

- **Method Type:** Steady-state, **primary (absolute)**, one-dimensional heat conduction; conforms to **ASTM C177 / IS 3346**.
- **Working Range:** $k$ between **0.01 and 2 W/(m·K)** (low-conductivity insulators).
- **Key Components:** Main heater, **guard heater** (thermal fence), twin specimens, water-cooled cold plates, thermocouples, differential temperature controller, power supply, data acquisition.
- **Master Formula:** 
$$
k = \frac{V \times I \times L}{2 \times A_{eff} \times (T_h - T_c)}, \quad A_{eff} = A_m + \tfrac{1}{2}A_g
$$
- **Role of Guard Heater:** Maintains isothermal boundary → **zero lateral heat loss** → enforces **1-D heat flow**.
- **Steady-State Requirement:** All temperatures must be constant with time (dT/dt = 0) before recording; typically requires **2–4 hours**.
- **Heat per Specimen:** Always **half** the main heater input, since heat splits equally between the upper and lower specimens.
- **Units to Remember:** $V$ in V, $I$ in A, $L$ in m, $A$ in m², $\Delta T$ in K (or °C), $k$ in **W/(m·K)**.
- **Verification Check:** $\Delta T = Q_{one} \times R_{th}$ should hold true with calculated values.
- **Typical Materials Tested:** Cork, fiberglass, foam, plywood, mineral wool, aerogels, firebrick.
- **Standards Followed:** ASTM C177 (US), IS 3346 (India), ISO 8302.
- **Sources of Error:** Contact resistance at interfaces, non-uniformity of specimens, imperfect guard heater temperature matching, heat loss from specimen edges, thermocouple calibration drift.
- **Examiner Trap:** Always show the **area correction** $A_{eff} = A_m + \tfrac{1}{2}A_g$ explicitly in the solution — it is a **3-mark differentiator** in KTU valuation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermal Conductivity Measurement of Pipe Insulation Using Lagged Pipe Apparatus
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGL508 - HEAT ENGINES LABORATORY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Thermal Conductivity Measurement of Pipe Insulation Using Lagged Pipe Apparatus

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module Experiment 2: Thermal Conductivity Measurement Using Lagged Pipe Apparatus

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermal Conductivity (k)** is defined as the rate of heat transfer through a unit thickness of a material per unit area per unit temperature difference. Its SI unit is **W/(m·K)** or **W/(m·°C)**.

The **Lagged Pipe Apparatus** is an experimental setup used to determine the thermal conductivity of insulating materials supplied in cylindrical (pipe‑cover) form. The test specimen is fitted concentrically over a horizontal metal pipe carrying steam, and the radial heat flow through the cylindrical shell is analysed under steady‑state conditions using **Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction** in cylindrical coordinates.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real‑World Analogy — The Thermos Flask Test:**
> Imagine wrapping a hot water bottle with different sweaters (wool, cotton, polyester). The "lagged pipe apparatus" does exactly this — it wraps a hot steam pipe with an insulating "sweater" and measures how much heat leaks through. A material that allows little heat to leak is a **good insulator** (low **k**); one that allows heat to escape easily is a **poor insulator** (high **k**).

The lagging (outer metal jacket) acts like the wind‑breaker over your sweater — it prevents direct air currents from carrying heat away, forcing the heat to flow **purely by conduction** through the insulation. This makes the heat flow one‑dimensional and predictable.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Constant / Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Stefan–Boltzmann Constant | **σ** | **5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/(m²·K⁴)** |
| Standard Atmospheric Pressure | P_atm | **101.325 kPa** |
| Typical Steam Temperature | T_steam | **373 K (100 °C)** |
| Asbestos Thermal Conductivity | k_asb | **≈ 0.15 W/(m·K)** |
| Glass Wool Thermal Conductivity | k_gw | **≈ 0.04 W/(m·K)** |
| Magnesia Thermal Conductivity | k_mag | **≈ 0.07 W/(m·K)** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High‑Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

The lagged pipe experiment is founded on five sequential physical realities. Each must be understood before the derivation is attempted:

- **Step 1 — Steady‑State Assumption:** After sufficient time (≈ 60–90 minutes), the temperatures at every point in the system become constant with time. The transient term ∂T/∂t = 0, reducing the General Heat Equation to Laplace's Equation: ∇²T = 0.

- **Step 2 — One‑Dimensional Radial Flow:** Because the pipe is long compared to its diameter and the ends are cooled, heat flows **only in the radial direction (r)**. The axial (z) and angular (θ) temperature gradients vanish.

- **Step 3 — Constant Thermal Conductivity (k):** The insulation is assumed homogeneous and isotropic, so k does not vary with temperature within the test range.

- **Step 4 — Conservation of Energy:** Under steady state, the heat supplied by the steam/heater (Q_in) equals the heat conducted through the insulation (Q_cond) equals the heat lost from the outer surface (Q_loss):

$$Q_{in} = Q_{cond} = Q_{loss}$$

- **Step 5 — Boundary Conditions:** The inner surface of the insulation is at temperature **T₁** (in contact with the hot pipe), and the outer surface is at temperature **T₂** (exposed to ambient air at T_a, where T₁ > T₂ > T_a).

## 2.2 Why and How Each Step Matters

- The **steady‑state** requirement allows us to equate heat fluxes — a transient analysis would require specific heat capacity and density data.
- **One‑dimensional radial flow** is essential because the formula k = Q·ln(r₂/r₁)/[2πL(T₁−T₂)] is valid ONLY when heat flows in one direction.
- The **Q_in = Q_loss** principle lets us measure heat input (from V·I of heater, or ṁ_steam·h_fg) and use it as the conduction rate.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master These Equations — They Appear in Almost Every Board Question.**

| # | Formula | Description | SI Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = \dfrac{2 \pi k L (T_1 - T_2)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$ | Fourier's law integrated over a cylindrical shell | W |
| 2 | $k = \dfrac{Q \cdot \ln(r_2/r_1)}{2 \pi L (T_1 - T_2)}$ | Thermal conductivity of insulation (THE key equation) | W/(m·K) |
| 3 | $Q = V \cdot I$ | Electrical heat input from heater coil | W |
| 4 | $Q = \dot{m}_{steam} \cdot h_{fg}$ | Heat from condensing steam (ṁ in kg/s) | W |
| 5 | $Q = h_s \cdot (2 \pi r_2 L) \cdot (T_2 - T_a)$ | Surface heat loss to ambient (convection + radiation) | W |
| 6 | $\ln(r_2/r_1) = 2.303 \log_{10}(r_2/r_1)$ | Conversion factor (natural vs base‑10 log) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $R_{th} = \dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2 \pi k L}$ | Thermal resistance of cylindrical insulation | K/W |
| 8 | $q = \dfrac{Q}{2 \pi r L}$ | Local heat flux at radius r (decreases with r) | W/m² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions Always Used:**
> - At r = r₁: T = T₁ (inner surface temperature, in K)
> - At r = r₂: T = T₂ (outer surface temperature, in K)
> - At r → ∞ (ambient): T = T_a (far‑field ambient temperature)

## 2.4 Real‑World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Steam Pipe Insulation:** Used in power plants, refineries, and chemical plants where steam at 200–500 °C flows through long pipelines. Selecting insulation with low **k** saves enormous energy.
- **HVAC Duct Insulation:** Cylindrical ducts carrying chilled water are insulated similarly.
- **Cryogenic Storage:** LNG/LH₂ tanks use multi‑layer cylindrical insulation where **k** characterisation is critical.
- **Building Energy Code Compliance:** Engineers must justify insulation choices using measured **k** values.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step‑by‑Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Thermal Conductivity Formula

### Step 1 — Start with Fourier's Law in Cylindrical Coordinates

For one‑dimensional radial heat flow through a cylindrical shell of length L, the heat flow rate through a cylindrical surface of radius r is:

$$Q = -k \cdot A \cdot \dfrac{dT}{dr} = -k \cdot (2 \pi r L) \cdot \dfrac{dT}{dr}$$

> **Explanation:** The negative sign indicates heat flows from hot to cold (temperature decreases as radius increases).

### Step 2 — Apply Steady‑State Condition

At steady state, **Q is constant** (does not depend on r). Therefore we can separate variables and integrate:

$$Q \cdot \dfrac{dr}{r} = -2 \pi k L \cdot dT$$

### Step 3 — Integrate Both Sides Between Limits

Integrate r from r₁ to r₂, and T from T₁ to T₂:

$$Q \int_{r_1}^{r_2} \dfrac{dr}{r} = -2 \pi k L \int_{T_1}^{T_2} dT$$

Evaluating the integrals:

$$Q \cdot [\ln r]_{r_1}^{r_2} = -2 \pi k L \cdot [T]_{T_1}^{T_2}$$

$$Q \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{r_2}{r_1}\right) = -2 \pi k L \cdot (T_2 - T_1)$$

### Step 4 — Rearrange to the Standard Form

Since T₁ > T₂, (T₂ − T₁) is negative, and the minus signs cancel:

$$Q \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{r_2}{r_1}\right) = 2 \pi k L \cdot (T_1 - T_2)$$

$$\boxed{Q = \dfrac{2 \pi k L (T_1 - T_2)}{\ln(r_2 / r_1)}}$$

### Step 5 — Solve for Thermal Conductivity k

$$\boxed{k = \dfrac{Q \cdot \ln(r_2 / r_1)}{2 \pi L (T_1 - T_2)}}$$

> This is the **canonical equation** every KTU student must memorise.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Step‑by‑Step)

**Given Data (typical KTU lab observation table):**

| Parameter | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Length of test section | L | 0.500 m |
| Outer radius of pipe | r₁ | 0.025 m (25 mm) |
| Outer radius of insulation | r₂ | 0.075 m (75 mm) |
| Inner surface temperature | T₁ | 393 K (120 °C) |
| Outer surface temperature | T₂ | 323 K (50 °C) |
| Ambient temperature | T_a | 303 K (30 °C) |
| Heat input (from heater) | Q | 30 W |

**Find:** Thermal conductivity **k** of the insulation material.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation:**

$$k = \dfrac{Q \cdot \ln(r_2 / r_1)}{2 \pi L (T_1 - T_2)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the logarithmic ratio:**

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{r_2}{r_1}\right) = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{0.075}{0.025}\right) = \ln(3) = 1.0986$$

**Step 3 — Compute the temperature difference:**

$$T_1 - T_2 = 393 - 323 = 70 \text{ K}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the denominator:**

$$2 \pi L (T_1 - T_2) = 2 \times \pi \times 0.500 \times 70$$

$$2 \pi L (T_1 - T_2) = 219.911 \text{ W/K}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the numerator:**

$$Q \cdot \ln(r_2 / r_1) = 30 \times 1.0986 = 32.958 \text{ W}$$

**Step 6 — Final calculation:**

$$k = \dfrac{32.958}{219.911}$$

$$\boxed{k = 0.1499 \;\text{W/(m·K)} \approx 0.150 \;\text{W/(m·K)}}$$

**Step 7 — Verification against standard data:**

This value corresponds closely to **asbestos insulation** (standard **k ≈ 0.15 W/(m·K)**), confirming the experiment's accuracy.

## 3.3 Alternative: Heat Loss via Steam Condensation

If the apparatus uses steam, the heat supplied can be calculated from condensate rate:

$$Q = \dot{m}_{steam} \times h_{fg}$$

where ṁ_steam is the mass flow rate of condensate collected in kg/s and **h_fg** is the latent heat of vaporisation (≈ 2260 kJ/kg at atmospheric pressure).

**Substituting into the k formula:**

$$k = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{steam} \cdot h_{fg} \cdot \ln(r_2/r_1)}{2 \pi L (T_1 - T_2)}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block‑Level Functional Architecture of Lagged Pipe Apparatus
