## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: Short Answer [3 Marks]**
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q: Define thermal conductivity. State Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction and explain its significance in the composite wall experiment.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Thermal conductivity ($k$) is defined as the ability of a material to conduct heat. Numerically, it is the rate of heat flow through a unit thickness of the material per unit cross-sectional area per unit temperature difference. Its SI unit is $\text{W/(m·K)}$.

**Fourier's Law (1.5 Marks):** Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction states that the rate of heat transfer through a homogeneous material is directly proportional to the cross-sectional area perpendicular to heat flow and the temperature gradient along the direction of flow:

$$Q = -k \cdot A \cdot \frac{dT}{dx}$$

The negative sign indicates heat flows in the direction of decreasing temperature.

**Significance (0.5 Marks):** In the composite wall experiment, Fourier's Law is applied individually to each layer and then combined using the principle of **thermal resistances in series** to determine the effective thermal conductivity of the entire assembly.

---

#### **Question 2: Short Answer [3 Marks]**
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q: What is thermal resistance? Explain why thermal resistances add in series for a composite wall.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Thermal resistance ($R$) is the opposition offered by a material or assembly to the flow of heat. It is analogous to electrical resistance in Ohm's Law. For a single slab:

$$R = \frac{L}{k \cdot A} \quad [\text{units: K/W}]$$

**Series Addition Logic (2 Marks):** Under steady-state conditions, the heat flow rate $Q$ is constant through every layer of a composite wall (no heat accumulation anywhere in the wall). Therefore, the total temperature drop across the wall is the **sum** of the individual temperature drops:

$$\Delta T_{total} = \Delta T_1 + \Delta T_2 + \Delta T_3$$

Substituting $\Delta T_i = Q \cdot R_i$:

$$Q \cdot R_{total} = Q \cdot R_1 + Q \cdot R_2 + Q \cdot R_3$$

Dividing by $Q$:

$$R_{total} = R_1 + R_2 + R_3$$

This is **mathematically identical to the addition of electrical resistances in series** in a DC circuit under Ohm's Law ($V = IR$). The analogy is so strong that thermal networks are often analyzed using the same circuit techniques (Kirchhoff's laws).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Each module carries a 14-mark question with **internal choice** (i.e., students answer either Choice A or Choice B). Each question has two sub-parts, typically (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice A**
**[KTU University Exam - Module 2, May 2024]**

**Q: A composite wall used in a furnace consists of three layers in series:**
- **Layer 1 (Fireclay Brick):** Thickness $L_1 = 0.10 \text{ m}$, thermal conductivity $k_1 = 1.05 \text{ W/(m·K)}$
- **Layer 2 (Insulating Brick):** Thickness $L_2 = 0.08 \text{ m}$, thermal conductivity $k_2 = 0.21 \text{ W/(m·K)}$
- **Layer 3 (Red Brick):** Thickness $L_3 = 0.12 \text{ m}$, thermal conductivity $k_3 = 0.72 \text{ W/(m·K)}$

The hot face temperature is $T_h = 800 \text{ °C}$ and the cold face temperature is $T_c = 60 \text{ °C}$. The cross-sectional area of the wall is $A = 4 \text{ m}^2$.

### **(a) [7 Marks] Calculate the steady-state heat flow rate through the wall per hour. Also compute the interface temperatures between Layer 1–2 and Layer 2–3.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Calculate individual thermal resistances per unit area (specific resistance):**

For Layer 1:
$$r_1 = \frac{L_1}{k_1} = \frac{0.10}{1.05} = 0.0952 \text{ m}^2 \cdot \text{K/W}$$

For Layer 2:
$$r_2 = \frac{L_2}{k_2} = \frac{0.08}{0.21} = 0.3810 \text{ m}^2 \cdot \text{K/W}$$

For Layer 3:
$$r_3 = \frac{L_3}{k_3} = \frac{0.12}{0.72} = 0.1667 \text{ m}^2 \cdot \text{K/W}$$

**Step 2: Sum the resistances (2 marks):**
$$r_{total} = r_1 + r_2 + r_3 = 0.0952 + 0.3810 + 0.1667 = 0.6429 \text{ m}^2 \cdot \text{K/W}$$

**Step 3: Compute the heat flux (heat flow per unit area):**
$$q = \frac{T_h - T_c}{r_{total}} = \frac{800 - 60}{0.6429} = \frac{740}{0.6429} = 1150.99 \text{ W/m}^2$$

**Step 4: Compute the total heat flow rate:**
$$Q = q \cdot A = 1150.99 \times 4 = 4603.95 \text{ W} \approx 4.604 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5: Convert to heat flow per hour (in kJ/hr):**
$$Q_{hr} = 4603.95 \text{ W} = 4.604 \text{ kJ/s} = 4.604 \times 3600 = 16{,}573.2 \text{ kJ/hr}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{hr} \approx 16{,}573 \text{ kJ/hr} \quad \text{or} \quad Q \approx 4.604 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step 6: Compute the interface temperature $T_{12}$ (between Layer 1 and Layer 2):**

Temperature drop across Layer 1:
$$\Delta T_1 = q \cdot r_1 = 1150.99 \times 0.0952 = 109.57 \text{ °C}$$

$$T_{12} = T_h - \Delta T_1 = 800 - 109.57 = 690.43 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 7: Compute the interface temperature $T_{23}$ (between Layer 2 and Layer 3):**

Temperature drop across Layer 2:
$$\Delta T_2 = q \cdot r_2 = 1150.99 \times 0.3810 = 438.53 \text{ °C}$$

$$T_{23} = T_{12} - \Delta T_2 = 690.43 - 438.53 = 251.90 \text{ °C}$$

**Verification (recommended):** Temperature drop across Layer 3:
$$\Delta T_3 = q \cdot r_3 = 1150.99 \times 0.1667 = 191.87 \text{ °C}$$
$$T_c = T_{23} - \Delta T_3 = 251.90 - 191.87 = 60.03 \text{ °C} \quad \checkmark \text{ (matches given } T_c)$$

---

### **(b) [7 Marks] Determine the effective thermal conductivity of the composite wall. If the Layer 2 thickness is increased to 0.16 m, find the new heat loss per hour and the percentage reduction in heat loss.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute the effective thermal conductivity (2 marks):**

Total thickness:
$$L_{total} = L_1 + L_2 + L_3 = 0.10 + 0.08 + 0.12 = 0.30 \text{ m}$$

Effective thermal conductivity:
$$k_{eff} = \frac{L_{total}}{r_{total}} = \frac{0.30}{0.6429} = 0.4666 \text{ W/(m·K)}$$

$$\boxed{k_{eff} \approx 0.467 \text{ W/(m·K)}}$$

**Step 2: Recalculate with $L_2 = 0.16 \text{ m}$ (new insulating layer thickness):**

New resistance for Layer 2:
$$r_2^{new} = \frac{0.16}{0.21} = 0.7619 \text{ m}^2 \cdot \text{K/W}$$

New total resistance:
$$r_{total}^{new} = 0.0952 + 0.7619 + 0.1667 = 1.0238 \text{ m}^2 \cdot \text{K/W}$$

**Step 3: Compute new heat flux and heat loss per hour (2 marks):**

$$q_{new} = \frac{740}{1.0238} = 722.80 \text{ W/m}^2$$

$$Q_{new} = 722.80 \times 4 = 2891.2 \text{ W} = 2.891 \text{ kW}$$

$$Q_{new, hr} = 2.891 \times 3600 = 10{,}408.3 \text{ kJ/hr}$$

**Step 4: Calculate the percentage reduction in heat loss (2 marks):**

$$\text{Reduction (\%)} = \frac{Q_{old} - Q_{new}}{Q_{old}} \times 100$$

$$\text{Reduction (\%)} = \frac{4603.95 - 2891.2}{4603.95} \times 100$$

$$\text{Reduction (\%)} = \frac{1712.75}{4603.95} \times 100 = 37.21\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{Percentage reduction} \approx 37.21\%}$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Doubling the insulating brick thickness from 0.08 m to 0.16 m reduces heat loss by approximately **37.2%**. This non-linear relationship (doubling thickness does not halve heat loss) arises because the other two layers still contribute to the total resistance, preventing a 50% reduction.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice B (Alternative)**
**[KTU University Exam - Module 2, Dec 2023]**

**Q: In a composite wall heat conduction experiment conducted in your Heat Engines Laboratory, the apparatus consists of two materials arranged in series — a brass disc and an asbestos disc — both having a diameter of $D = 100 \text{ mm}$. The thicknesses are $L_1 = 20 \text{ mm}$ (brass) and $L_2 = 25 \text{ mm}$ (asbestos). Under steady-state conditions, the following observations are recorded:**

| Parameter | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Hot face temperature | $T_h$ | $120 \text{ °C}$ |
| Brass–Asbestos interface | $T_1$ | $119.2 \text{ °C}$ |
| Cold face temperature | $T_c$ | $42 \text{ °C}$ |
| Heater voltage | $V$ | $60 \text{ V}$ |
| Heater current | $I$ | $4.0 \text{ A}$ |
| Water flow rate | $\dot{V}$ | $0.5 \text{ LPM}$ |
| Water inlet temperature | $T_{w,in}$ | $28 \text{ °C}$ |
| Water outlet temperature | $T_{w,out}$ | $32 \text{ °C}$ |

**Take $c_{p,water} = 4.186 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$ and water density $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$.**

### **(a) [7 Marks] Calculate the thermal conductivities of brass and asbestos using both the electrical input method and the cooling water method. Comment on the agreement between the two methods.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Calculate the cross-sectional area (0.5 marks):**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.1)^2}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Calculate the heat flow rate by the electrical input method (1.5 marks):**

$$Q_{elec} = V \cdot I = 60 \times 4.0 = 240 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the heat flow rate by the cooling water method (1.5 marks):**

Convert flow rate to SI:
$$\dot{V} = 0.5 \text{ LPM} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{60} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 8.333 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Mass flow rate:
$$\dot{m}_w = \rho_w \cdot \dot{V} = 1000 \times 8.333 \times 10^{-6} = 8.333 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}$$

Heat absorbed by water:
$$Q_{water} = \dot{m}_w \cdot c_{p,w} \cdot (T_{w,out} - T_{w,in})$$

$$Q_{water} = 8.333 \times 10^{-3} \times 4186 \times (32 - 28)$$

$$Q_{water} = 8.333 \times 10^{-3} \times 4186 \times 4 = 139.5 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4: Calculate the average heat flow rate (0.5 marks):**

$$\bar{Q} = \frac{Q_{elec} + Q_{water}}{2} = \frac{240 + 139.5}{2} = 189.75 \text{ W}$$

> **Comment:** The difference is $(240 - 139.5)/240 = 41.9\%$, indicating significant heat loss from the apparatus to the surroundings. A well-insulated apparatus should show a difference of less than 15%.

**Step 5: Calculate the thermal conductivity of brass using $\bar{Q}$ (1.5 marks):**

$$k_{brass} = \frac{\bar{Q} \cdot L_1}{A \cdot (T_h - T_1)} = \frac{189.75 \times 0.020}{7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times (120 - 119.2)}$$

$$k_{brass} = \frac{3.795}{7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.8} = \frac{3.795}{6.283 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\boxed{k_{brass} \approx 604 \text{ W/(m·K)}}$$

> **Note:** This is high because of the very small temperature gradient across brass ($\Delta T = 0.8 \text{ °C}$). Even tiny measurement errors in $\Delta T$ cause large errors in $k$. The standard value of brass is $\sim 109 \text{ W/(m·K)}$, and the discrepancy arises from experimental uncertainties in the temperature readings.

**Step 6: Calculate the thermal conductivity of asbestos (1.5 marks):**

$$k_{asb} = \frac{\bar{Q} \cdot L_2}{A \cdot (T_1 - T_c)} = \frac{189.75 \times 0.025}{7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times (119.2 - 42)}$$

$$k_{asb} = \frac{4.744}{7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 77.2} = \frac{4.744}{0.6063}$$

$$\boxed{k_{asbestos} \approx 7.82 \text{ W/(m·K)}}$$

> **Note:** The standard value of asbestos is $\sim 0.16 \text{ W/(m·K)}$. The huge discrepancy indicates that the calculated $Q$ (using the average) is too high, OR the temperature $T_1$ is incorrectly measured (a common issue with bare thermocouples). Students must report this honestly in the viva.

---

### **(b) [7 Marks] List the sources of error in the composite wall experiment. Explain how each error affects the calculated thermal conductivity. Suggest two design improvements to minimize these errors.**

**Model Answer:**

**Sources of Error (4 marks):**

1. **Heat Loss to Surroundings (Major):** The lateral surfaces of the apparatus are not perfectly insulated, causing heat to escape radially. This makes $Q_{heater} > Q_{water}$ and biases the calculated $k$ to higher values.
   - **Effect:** Overestimates the true thermal conductivity.

2. **Contact Resistance at Interfaces (Major):** The contact between two solid discs is never perfect. Microscopic air gaps act as additional thermal resistance.
   - **Effect:** Underestimates the true thermal conductivity of the materials, because the calculated $k$ is forced to account for the extra resistance.

3. **Thermocouple Placement Error:** If thermocouples are not exactly at the interface plane, the measured $\Delta T$ across a layer is incorrect.
   - **Effect:** Both over- and under-estimation possible, depending on whether the thermocouple is shifted toward hot or cold side.

4. **Steady-State Not Achieved:** If readings are taken before temperatures fully stabilize, the temperature profile is still transient and does not satisfy $\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = 0$.
   - **Effect:** Random errors; readings change with time, giving non-reproducible results.

5. **Cooling Water Flow Inconsistency:** Variations in the water flow rate cause fluctuations in $T_{w,out}$, making the heat balance unreliable.
   - **Effect:** $Q_{water}$ is noisy; mass flow rate measurement uncertainty directly propagates to $k$.

6. **Instrument Calibration:** Voltmeters, ammeters, and thermocouples have finite accuracy classes (e.g., $\pm 1\%$ FS).
   - **Effect:** Systematic bias; consistent over- or under-estimation across all trials.

**Design Improvements (3 marks):**

1. **Add thick lateral insulation (e.g., glass wool jacket) around the apparatus body** to minimize radial heat loss. This brings $Q_{heater}$ closer to $Q_{water}$ (difference < 10%).

2. **Apply a thin layer of thermal interface compound (e.g., thermal grease or mercury at the interface in older apparatus)** to eliminate air gaps and reduce contact resistance. This ensures the interface temperature is well-defined and the two surfaces are at the same temperature.

3. **Embed thermocouples inside the material** (in drilled holes filled with conductive paste) rather than placing them on the surface. This gives the true bulk temperature of each layer.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors (–2 marks):** Mixing mm and m, LPM and m³/s, kcal and kJ. KTU examiners are strict about unit consistency.
> 2. **Wrong Heat Balance Method (–3 marks):** Some students compute $k$ using $Q_{heater}$ only and ignore the cooling water balance. Both must be shown and compared.
> 3. **No Comment on Discrepancy (–2 marks):** If calculated $k$ differs significantly from the standard value, students often just report the number. KTU expects a **discussion of sources of error** and a calculated **percentage error**.
> 4. **Forgetting to Plot the Temperature Profile (–1 mark):** The graph of $T$ vs. $x$ is mandatory in the lab record.
> 5. **Sign Convention Error (–1 mark):** Writing $\Delta T = T_c - T_h$ (negative). Use $T_h - T_c$ to keep $Q$ positive.
> 6. **Ineffective Insulation Discussion (–2 marks):** Simply stating "heat loss" without quantifying or explaining **how it affects $k$** loses marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Master These Before the Exam**

- **Core Law:** Fourier's Law $Q = kA \frac{\Delta T}{L}$ is the foundation of the entire experiment. All other equations are derived from it.
- **Resistance Concept:** $R = \frac{L}{kA}$. The **total resistance of a composite wall is the sum** of individual layer resistances (series analogy).
- **Master Equation:** $Q = \frac{A(T_h - T_c)}{\sum \frac{L_i}{k_i}}$ — memorize this; it solves 80% of problems.
- **Effective $k$:** $k_{eff} = \frac{L_{total}}{\sum \frac{L_i}{k_i}}$. The composite is **always dominated by the layer with the highest resistance** (lowest $k$).
- **Two $Q$ Methods:** Always compute both $Q_{heater} = VI$ and $Q_{water} = \dot{m}_w c_p \Delta T_w$. A difference > 15% indicates poor insulation.
- **Steady-State Criterion:** All temperatures must remain constant for at least **5 minutes** before recording readings.
- **One-Dimensional Assumption:** Valid only if lateral surfaces are well-insulated. Heat flows in $x$-direction only.
- **Units Check:** Final $k$ must be in $\text{W/(m·K)}$. Common conversions: $1 \text{ mm} = 10^{-3} \text{ m}$, $1 \text{ LPM} = \frac{1}{60} \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$.
- **Temperature Difference vs. Absolute:** $1 \text{ K} = 1 \text{ °C}$ when used as a *difference*. Do **not** convert absolute temperatures — just use the difference.
- **Water Properties:** $c_{p,water} = 4186 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$ and $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ — remember these constants.
- **Interface Temperature:** $T_i = T_h - q \cdot \sum_{j=1}^{i} r_j$, where $q$ is the heat flux and $r_j = L_j/k_j$.
- **Percentage Error:** $\%\text{ Error} = \left| \frac{k_{exp} - k_{standard}}{k_{standard}} \right| \times 100$. Standard values: Brass $\sim 109$, Asbestos $\sim 0.16$, Mild Steel $\sim 45$, Copper $\sim 385$, Aluminum $\sim 205$ (all in $\text{W/(m·K)}$).
- **Graph Plot:** Always plot $T$ (y-axis) vs. $x$ (distance from hot face) — piecewise linear with slopes inversely proportional to local $k$.
- **Major Errors:** (1) Heat loss to surroundings, (2) Contact resistance at interfaces, (3) Unsteady-state readings, (4) Thermocouple misplacement.
- **Design Improvements:** Thick lateral insulation, thermal interface grease, embedded thermocouples, guarded hot plate design for advanced setups.
- **Industrial Significance:** Furnace walls, boiler insulation, building envelopes, cryogenic tanks, heat sinks, aerospace TPS — all rely on composite wall analysis.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat Transfer from Pin-Fin Apparatus (Natural and Forced Convection Modes)
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGL508 - HEAT ENGINES LABORATORY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Heat Transfer from Pin-Fin Apparatus (Natural and Forced Convection Modes)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pin Fin:** A *pin fin* (also called a *cylindrical fin* or *spine*) is an extended surface of circular cross-section protruding from a primary heat-exchanging surface. Its purpose is to **augment convective heat transfer** by increasing the effective surface area exposed to the surrounding fluid (typically air).

In the KTU Heat Engines Laboratory, the **Pin-Fin Apparatus** is a standard test rig used to experimentally determine:

- The **temperature distribution** along an axial fin.
- The **convective heat transfer coefficient** ($h$) under **natural convection** (buoyancy-driven flow) and **forced convection** (external airflow supplied by a blower/fan).
- The **fin efficiency ($\eta_{fin}$)** and **fin effectiveness ($\varepsilon_{fin}$)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "CPU Heat Sink" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> Think of a pin fin exactly like the small metal spikes you see on a **motorcycle engine cylinder head** or a **CPU heat sink**. The engine block gets very hot. If you leave a flat metal surface exposed, air touches only that flat area. But if you bolt on many small cylindrical rods (pin fins), the air now contacts the rod tips, sides, and gaps between rods. **Total heat-radiating area is multiplied**, and the engine cools much faster. That multiplication of surface area is the *entire* reason pin fins exist.

**Why does convection matter here?**
- In **Natural Convection**, the air surrounding the fin is *still* — it only moves because the hot fin warms the nearby air, making it lighter, so it rises and cooler air rushes in to replace it (chimney effect). This is slow.
- In **Forced Convection**, a **blower** pushes air over the fin at a known velocity. Cold air is *forced* to come into contact with the fin continuously. Heat removal becomes dramatically faster.

### 1.3 Standard Apparatus Constants (Bold-Highlighted)

The following quantities are typically stamped on the apparatus nameplate or measured during calibration:

| Physical Quantity | Typical Range / Value |
|---|---|
| **Pin Material** | **Brass / Aluminum / Mild Steel** |
| **Pin Diameter (D)** | **10 mm – 25 mm** (common: **12.5 mm**) |
| **Pin Length (L)** | **100 mm – 300 mm** |
| **Base Heater Power** | **Up to 100 W** |
| **Thermocouples** | **K-type (Chromel-Alumel)**, 4 to 7 along fin length |
| **Ambient Temperature ($T_\infty$)** | Measured by exposed thermocouple |
| **Air Velocity (forced mode)** | **0 – 10 m/s** (measured by anemometer) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Fundamental Governing Laws Used in This Experiment:**
> 1. **Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction** (heat flow *through* the solid fin).
> 2. **Newton's Law of Cooling** (heat flow *from* the fin surface to the surrounding air).
> 3. **Energy Conservation** (1st Law of Thermodynamics) applied to a control volume around the fin.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Framework

#### Step A — Why a Pin Fin? The Concept of "Extended Surface"

A bare flat surface of area $A$ loses heat at rate $Q_{bare} = hA(T_s - T_\infty)$. If we attach a cylindrical pin of length $L$ and diameter $D$, the *additional* surface area added to the system is:

$$A_{fin} = \pi D L + \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

The total heat dissipation becomes:

$$Q_{total} = Q_{bare,\,remaining} + Q_{fin}$$

The whole point of the experiment is to quantify **how much extra heat the fin dissipates**.

#### Step B — The 1-D Fin Conduction Equation

We isolate an elementary disc of the pin fin at distance $x$ from the base, of thickness $dx$. Applying Fourier's law at the two faces and Newton's law at the curved surface, and equating them via energy balance:

$$\text{Conduction in at } x - \text{Conduction out at } (x+dx) = \text{Convection loss from side}$$

This yields the celebrated **Fin Differential Equation**:

$$\frac{d^2 \theta}{dx^2} - m^2 \theta = 0$$

where:
- $\theta = T(x) - T_\infty$ is the **excess temperature** at any location $x$.
- $m = \sqrt{\dfrac{hP}{kA_c}}$ is the **fin parameter** (units: **m⁻¹**).
- $h$ = convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K).
- $P$ = perimeter of fin cross-section (m).
- $A_c$ = cross-sectional area of fin (m²).
- $k$ = thermal conductivity of fin material (W/m·K).

#### Step C — Why Two Solutions? Tip Boundary Conditions

There are two classical boundary conditions at the fin tip ($x = L$):

| B.C. Type | Mathematical Statement | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Insulated Tip** (most common in KTU labs) | $\dfrac{d\theta}{dx}\Big\vert_{x=L} = 0$ | The fin is long enough that heat loss at the tip is negligible |
| **Specified Tip Temperature** | $\theta(L) = \theta_L$ | The tip is held at a known temperature (e.g., by a small fin beyond) |
| **Convection at Tip** | $-kA_c \dfrac{d\theta}{dx}\Big\vert_{x=L} = hA_c\theta_L$ | Heat is lost by convection *from* the tip itself |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For the standard KTU Pin-Fin Apparatus, the **insulated tip** assumption is used. The solution to the ODE then becomes:

$$\theta(x) = \theta_b \cdot \frac{\cosh\left[m(L - x)\right]}{\cosh(mL)}$$

where $\theta_b = T_b - T_\infty$ is the **excess base temperature**.

#### Step D — Natural vs. Forced Convection Regimes

| Parameter | Natural Convection | Forced Convection |
|---|---|---|
| **Driving Force** | Buoyancy ($\Delta \rho g$) | External blower / fan |
| **Dominant Dimensionless Group** | **Grashof Number** ($Gr$) | **Reynolds Number** ($Re$) |
| **Velocity Field** | Self-induced; typically $V < 0.5$ m/s | Imposed; typically $1 < V < 10$ m/s |
| **$h$ Range (air)** | **5 – 25 W/m²·K** | **25 – 250 W/m²·K** |
| **Typical Correlation** | $Nu = C(Gr \cdot Pr)^n$ | $Nu = C \cdot Re^m \cdot Pr^n$ |

**Dimensionless Groups (must memorize for KTU viva):**

$$Nu = \frac{hD}{k_{air}}, \qquad Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu}, \qquad Pr = \frac{\mu C_p}{k_{air}}, \qquad Gr = \frac{g \beta \Delta T D^3}{\nu^2}$$

#### Step E — The Two Key Performance Metrics

**1. Fin Efficiency ($\eta_{fin}$):**
Ratio of *actual* heat dissipated to *maximum possible* heat dissipation (which would occur if the entire fin were at base temperature).

$$\eta_{fin} = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{max}} = \frac{\tanh(mL)}{mL}$$

**2. Fin Effectiveness ($\varepsilon_{fin}$):**
Ratio of fin heat dissipation to heat dissipation from the bare base area *without* the fin.

$$\varepsilon_{fin} = \frac{Q_{fin}}{h \cdot A_c \cdot \theta_b} = \frac{\tanh(mL) \sqrt{hPkA_c}}{hA_c}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A "good" engineering fin has $\eta_{fin} \geq 0.5$ and $\varepsilon_{fin} \geq 2.0$. Below $\varepsilon = 2$, the fin is *worse* than no fin at all!

### 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RATE THESE FORMULAS AS "MUST-KNOW" — KTU ESE 2024 will test at least 2 of them in Part B.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | SI Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $m = \sqrt{\dfrac{hP}{kA_c}}$ | Fin parameter | m⁻¹ |
| 2 | $P = \pi D$, $A_c = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | Geometry of cylindrical pin | m, m² |
| 3 | $\theta(x) = \theta_b \dfrac{\cosh[m(L-x)]}{\cosh(mL)}$ | Temperature profile (insulated tip) | K |
| 4 | $Q_{fin} = \sqrt{hPkA_c} \cdot \theta_b \cdot \tanh(mL)$ | Total heat dissipated by fin | W |
| 5 | $\eta_{fin} = \dfrac{\tanh(mL)}{mL}$ | Fin efficiency | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\varepsilon_{fin} = \tanh(mL)\sqrt{\dfrac{kP}{hA_c}}$ | Fin effectiveness | dimensionless |
| 7 | $Q_{input} = V \cdot I$ | Electrical heat supplied to base | W |
| 8 | $Q_{loss,\,air} = \dot{m}_{air} C_p (T_{out} - T_{in})$ | Heat carried by air (forced mode) | W |
| 9 | $Nu = \dfrac{hD}{k_{air}}$ | Nusselt number | dimensionless |
| 10 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number (forced) | dimensionless |
| 11 | $Gr = \dfrac{g \beta \Delta T L_c^3}{\nu^2}$ | Grashof number (natural) | dimensionless |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Pin fins are *everywhere* in mechanical and thermal engineering:

- **Air-cooled motorcycle/IC engine cylinders.**
- **Transformer tank radiators** (oil-to-air heat exchangers in substations).
- **LED heat sinks** in electronics cooling.
- **Gas turbine blade internal cooling passages.**
- **Refrigerator condenser coils** (sometimes pin-finned).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the 1-D Fin Equation (From First Principles)

Consider an elemental cylindrical slice of the pin fin at axial position $x$, of thickness $dx$, as illustrated conceptually in the diagram section.

**Step 1 — Define the control volume:**
The slice has cross-sectional area $A_c$, perimeter $P$, lateral surface area $P \cdot dx$, and volume $A_c \cdot dx$.

**Step 2 — Apply Fourier's Law at the left face (at position $x$):**
Heat conducted *into* the slice from the left:

$$Q_{x} = -k A_c \left.\frac{dT}{dx}\right|_{x}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Fourier's Law at the right face (at position $x + dx$):**
Heat conducted *out* of the slice from the right:

$$Q_{x+dx} = -k A_c \left.\frac{dT}{dx}\right|_{x+dx}$$

**Step 4 — Taylor-series expand** $Q_{x+dx}$ about $x$ (retain first two terms):

$$Q_{x+dx} = -k A_c \left[ \left.\frac{dT}{dx}\right|_{x} + \left.\frac{d^2T}{dx^2}\right|_{x} dx + \cdots \right]$$

**Step 5 — Net heat conducted into the slice** = In − Out:

$$Q_{cond,\,net} = Q_x - Q_{x+dx} = k A_c \frac{d^2 T}{dx^2} dx$$

**Step 6 — Apply Newton's Law of Cooling to the curved surface:**

$$Q_{conv} = h (P \, dx) [T(x) - T_\infty]$$

**Step 7 — Apply the Steady-State Energy Balance** (1st Law, no storage):

$$Q_{cond,\,net} = Q_{conv}$$

$$k A_c \frac{d^2 T}{dx^2} dx = h P [T(x) - T_\infty] \, dx$$

**Step 8 — Define excess temperature** $\theta(x) \equiv T(x) - T_\infty$. Since $T_\infty$ is constant, $\dfrac{d^2T}{dx^2} = \dfrac{d^2\theta}{dx^2}$. Dividing both sides by $kA_c$:

$$\frac{d^2 \theta}{dx^2} - \frac{hP}{kA_c} \theta = 0$$

**Step 9 — Substitute the fin parameter** $m^2 = \dfrac{hP}{kA_c}$:

$$\boxed{\frac{d^2 \theta}{dx^2} - m^2 \theta = 0}$$

---

### 3.2 Closed-Form Solution for Insulated Tip

The general solution of the linear homogeneous ODE $\dfrac{d^2\theta}{dx^2} = m^2\theta$ is:

$$\theta(x) = C_1 \cosh(mx) + C_2 \sinh(mx)$$

**Apply Boundary Condition 1:** At the base ($x = 0$), $\theta(0) = \theta_b$:

$$\theta_b = C_1 \cosh(0) + C_2 \sinh(0) = C_1$$

$$\Rightarrow C_1 = \theta_b$$

**Apply Boundary Condition 2:** Insulated tip at $x = L$, $\dfrac{d\theta}{dx}\Big\vert_{x=L} = 0$:

$$\frac{d\theta}{dx} = C_1 m \sinh(mx) + C_2 m \cosh(mx)$$

At $x = L$: $\theta_b m \sinh(mL) + C_2 m \cosh(mL) = 0$

$$\Rightarrow C_2 = -\theta_b \tanh(mL)$$

**Substitute back:**

$$\theta(x) = \theta_b \cosh(mx) - \theta_b \tanh(mL) \sinh(mx)$$

Using the identity $\cosh(mx)\cosh(mL) - \sinh(mx)\sinh(mL) = \cosh[m(L - x)]$, we factor out $\cosh(mL)$:

$$\theta(x) = \frac{\theta_b}{\cosh(mL)} \left[ \cosh(mx)\cosh(mL) - \sinh(mx)\sinh(mL) \right]$$

$$\boxed{\theta(x) = \theta_b \frac{\cosh[m(L - x)]}{\cosh(mL)}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Heat Transfer Rate $Q_{fin}$

Heat dissipated by the fin = heat conducted *into* the base (at $x = 0$):

$$Q_{fin} = -k A_c \left.\frac{d\theta}{dx}\right|_{x=0}$$

From the temperature profile:

$$\frac{d\theta}{dx} = -\frac{\theta_b \, m \sinh[m(L - x)]}{\cosh(mL)}$$

Evaluate at $x = 0$:

$$\left.\frac{d\theta}{dx}\right|_{x=0} = -\frac{\theta_b \, m \sinh(mL)}{\cosh(mL)} = -\theta_b \, m \tanh(mL)$$

Therefore:

$$Q_{fin} = -kA_c \left[-\theta_b \, m \tanh(mL)\right] = kA_c \, m \, \theta_b \tanh(mL)$$

Substitute $m = \sqrt{\dfrac{hP}{kA_c}}$, so $kA_c m = \sqrt{hPkA_c}$:

$$\boxed{Q_{fin} = \sqrt{hPkA_c} \; \theta_b \; \tanh(mL)}$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Given:** A brass pin fin has $D = 12.5$ mm, $L = 130$ mm, $k_{brass} = 109$ W/m·K, $h = 30$ W/m²·K, base temperature $T_b = 90 °C$, ambient $T_\infty = 30 °C$. Find (i) fin parameter $m$, (ii) fin efficiency, (iii) fin effectiveness, (iv) heat dissipated.

**Step 1 — Geometry:**
$P = \pi D = \pi \times 0.0125 = 0.03927$ m
$A_c = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times (0.0125)^2}{4} = 1.227 \times 10^{-4}$ m²

**Step 2 — Compute $m$:**
$m^2 = \dfrac{hP}{kA_c} = \dfrac{30 \times 0.03927}{109 \times 1.227 \times 10^{-4}}$

$$m^2 = \frac{1.178}{0.01337} = 88.11 \text{ m}^{-2}$$

$$m = \sqrt{88.11} = 9.386 \text{ m}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $mL$:**
$mL = 9.386 \times 0.130 = 1.220$

**Step 4 — Fin efficiency:**
$\tanh(1.220) = \dfrac{e^{1.220} - e^{-1.220}}{e^{1.220} + e^{-1.220}} = \dfrac{3.387 - 0.295}{3.387 + 0.295} = \dfrac{3.092}{3.682} = 0.8398$

$$\eta_{fin} = \frac{0.8398}{1.220} = 0.6885 \;\;(\text{i.e., } 68.85\%)$$

**Step 5 — Fin effectiveness:**
$\varepsilon_{fin} = \tanh(mL) \sqrt{\dfrac{kP}{hA_c}} = 0.8398 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{109 \times 0.03927}{30 \times 1.227 \times 10^{-4}}}$

$\sqrt{\dfrac{4.281}{0.003681}} = \sqrt{1162.9} = 34.10$

$$\varepsilon_{fin} = 0.8398 \times 34.10 = 28.64$$

> Excellent — effectiveness >> 2, so the fin is **highly justified**.

**Step 6 — Heat dissipated:**
$\theta_b = 90 - 30 = 60$ K
$\sqrt{hPkA_c} = \sqrt{30 \times 0.03927 \times 109 \times 1.227 \times 10^{-4}} = \sqrt{0.01575} = 0.1255$ W/K

$$Q_{fin} = 0.1255 \times 60 \times 0.8398 = 6.323 \text{ W}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Pin-Fin Apparatus (Natural Convection Mode)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[AC Variac Power Supply 0-230V] --> A2[Voltmeter V]
    A2 --> A3[Ammeter A]
    A3 --> A4[Heater Coil inside Brass Base Block]
    A4 --> A5[Pin Fin Mounted Vertically]
    A5 --> A6[Base Thermocouple T1 at x = 0]
    A5 --> A7[Mid Thermocouple T2 at x = L/2]
    A5 --> A8[Tip Thermocouple T3 at x = L]
    A9[Ambient Thermocouple T_inf] --> A10[Digital Temperature Indicator 8 Channel]
    A6 --> A10
    A7 --> A10
    A8 --> A10
    A10 --> A11[Data Logging and Calculation]
    S1[Still Air Surrounding Fin] -.Natural Convection.-> A5
    Q1[Heat Input Q_in = V x I] --> A4
    Q2[Heat Lost by Convection Q_conv = h A T - T_inf] --> S1
```

### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Forced Convection Mode Addition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Centrifugal Blower with Damper] --> B2[Air Flow Straightener Honeycomb]
    B2 --> B3[Test Section Containing Pin Fin]
    B3 --> B4[Anemometer Probe Pitot Static Tube]
    B4 --> B5[Digital Manometer Pressure Readout]
    B3 --> B6[Outlet Air Temperature T_out]
    B6 --> B7[Thermocouple Array]
    B7 --> B8[Data Acquisition System]
    B8 --> B9[Calculate h from Q = m_dot C_p T_out - T_in]
    B8 --> B10[Calculate Nusselt Number Nu = hD / k_air]
    B10 --> B11[Compare with Standard Correlation Nu = C Re^m Pr^n]
```

### 4.3 Schematic of Temperature Profile Along the Pin Fin

```mermaid
graph LR
    N1[Base at x = 0: T = T_b Highest] --> N2[Mid at x = L/2: T decreases exponentially] --> N3[Tip at x = L: T = T_t Lowest but above T_inf]
    N4[T_inf Ambient Air Temperature Constant] -.Reference Line.-> N2
    N5[Steep Drop near Base] -.Conduction Dominant.-> N2
    N6[Gradual Drop near Tip] -.Convection Dominant.-> N3
```

### 4.4 Energy Balance Schematic (1-D Fin Element)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q1[Heat In at x by Conduction Q_x = -k A_c dT/dx] --> ELEMENT[Control Volume Slice dx]
    ELEMENT --> Q2[Heat Out at x + dx by Conduction Q_x_plus_dx]
    ELEMENT --> Q3[Heat Lost by Convection to Air Q_conv = h P dx T - T_inf]
    Q1 --> Q4[Net Conduction In = k A_c d2T by dx2 times dx]
    Q4 --> EQ[Steady State: Net Conduction In = Convection Out]
    EQ --> FIN[Fin ODE: d2 theta by dx2 - m squared theta = 0]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** Define *fin efficiency* and *fin effectiveness*. State the minimum effectiveness criterion for a fin to be considered worthwhile.

**Model Answer:**
*Fin efficiency* ($\eta_{fin}$) is the ratio of the actual heat transferred by the fin to the heat that would be transferred if the entire fin surface were at the base temperature:

$$\eta_{fin} = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{max}} = \frac{\tanh(mL)}{mL}$$

*Fin effectiveness* ($\varepsilon_{fin}$) is the ratio of heat dissipated by the fin to the heat that would be dissipated by the bare base (without the fin) at the same base temperature:

$$\varepsilon_{fin} = \frac{Q_{fin}}{h A_c \theta_b} = \tanh(mL)\sqrt{\frac{kP}{hA_c}}$$

A fin is considered worthwhile only if **$\varepsilon_{fin} \geq 2$**, meaning the fin dissipates at least twice the heat of the bare surface it replaces. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Differentiate between *natural convection* and *forced convection* with reference to the Pin-Fin apparatus.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Natural Convection | Forced Convection |
|---|---|---|
| Driving mechanism | Buoyancy due to density difference (cold air sinks, hot air rises) | External device such as a blower or fan |
| Governing dimensionless number | **Grashof number** ($Gr$) | **Reynolds number** ($Re$) |
| Typical $h$ value (air) | 5–25 W/m²·K | 25–250 W/m²·K |
| Air velocity | Self-induced, slow | Imposed, controlled |
| Apparatus requirement | No blower needed | Blower with speed control + anemometer |

In the Pin-Fin apparatus, natural convection is observed when the blower is OFF; switching the blower ON transitions the experiment to forced convection, and $h$ increases significantly. **(3 marks)**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Style]*

> **Q.A (a)** Derive the general one-dimensional fin equation starting from Fourier's law of conduction and Newton's law of cooling. State the assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q.A (b)** For a cylindrical brass pin fin of diameter $D = 15$ mm, length $L = 150$ mm, $k = 110$ W/m·K, $h = 35$ W/m²·K, $T_b = 95 °C$, $T_\infty = 32 °C$, and an insulated tip, calculate: **(i)** fin parameter $m$, **(ii)** temperature at the tip, **(iii)** fin efficiency, **(iv)** fin effectiveness, **(v)** heat dissipated. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.A(a) — Derivation of the Fin Equation (7 marks):**

Refer to **Section 3.1** of these notes for the full step-by-step derivation.

**Key Steps to be Written in Exam:**

1. **Assumptions** (1 mark):
   - Steady-state, 1-D conduction along fin length.
   - Constant thermal conductivity $k$.
   - Constant convective coefficient $h$ along the surface.
   - No internal heat generation.
   - Surrounding fluid at uniform temperature $T_\infty$.
   - Negligible radiation.

2. **Element Slice** (1 mark): Take a slice $dx$ at position $x$. Net heat conducted into slice = $kA_c \dfrac{d^2T}{dx^2} dx$.

3. **Convection Loss** (1 mark): Lateral area $P \cdot dx$. Heat lost = $hP[T - T_\infty] dx$.

4. **Energy Balance** (2 marks): Equate and divide by $kA_c dx$:

   $$\frac{d^2T}{dx^2} - \frac{hP}{kA_c}(T - T_\infty) = 0$$

5. **Substitution** (1 mark): $\theta = T - T_\infty$, $m^2 = \dfrac{hP}{kA_c}$, giving:

   $$\boxed{\frac{d^2\theta}{dx^2} - m^2 \theta = 0}$$

6. **Statement** (1 mark): This is the 1-D fin differential equation.

---

**Solution to Q.A(b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:** $D = 0.015$ m, $L = 0.150$ m, $k = 110$ W/m·K, $h = 35$ W/m²·K, $T_b = 95 °C$, $T_\infty = 32 °C$.

**Step 1 — Geometry (1 mark):**
$P = \pi D = 3.1416 \times 0.015 = 0.04712$ m
$A_c = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times (0.015)^2}{4} = 1.767 \times 10^{-4}$ m²
$\theta_b = 95 - 32 = 63$ K

**Step 2 — Fin parameter (1 mark):**
$m^2 = \dfrac{hP}{kA_c} = \dfrac{35 \times 0.04712}{110 \times 1.767 \times 10^{-4}} = \dfrac{1.649}{1.944 \times 10^{-2}} = 84.84$ m⁻²

$$m = \sqrt{84.84} = 9.211 \text{ m}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Temperature at tip, $x = L$ (1 mark):**
$\theta(L) = \theta_b \dfrac{\cosh(0)}{\cosh(mL)} = \dfrac{\theta_b}{\cosh(mL)}$
$mL = 9.211 \times 0.150 = 1.382$
$\cosh(1.382) = \dfrac{e^{1.382} + e^{-1.382}}{2} = \dfrac{3.983 + 0.2511}{2} = 2.117$
$T_{tip} = T_\infty + \dfrac{63}{2.117} = 32 + 29.76 = 61.76 °C$

**Step 4 — Fin efficiency (1 mark):**
$\tanh(1.382) = \dfrac{3.983 - 0.2511}{3.983 + 0.2511} = \dfrac{3.732}{4.234} = 0.8814$
$\eta_{fin} = \dfrac{0.8814}{1.382} = 0.6378 = 63.78\%$

**Step 5 — Fin effectiveness (1 mark):**
$\varepsilon_{fin} = 0.8814 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{110 \times 0.04712}{35 \times 1.767 \times 10^{-4}}} = 0.8814 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{5.183}{6.184 \times 10^{-3}}}$
$\sqrt{838.1} = 28.95$
$\varepsilon_{fin} = 0.8814 \times 28.95 = 25.52$

**Step 6 — Heat dissipated (2 marks):**
$\sqrt{hPkA_c} = \sqrt{35 \times 0.04712 \times 110 \times 1.767 \times 10^{-4}}$
$= \sqrt{3.206 \times 10^{-2}} = 0.1791$ W/K
$Q_{fin} = 0.1791 \times 63 \times 0.8814 = 9.945$ W

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE** *[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]*

> **Q.B (a)** Explain with a neat sketch the experimental setup to determine the convective heat transfer coefficient under **(i)** natural convection and **(ii)** forced convection using a Pin-Fin apparatus. List the observations to be tabulated. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q.B (b)** During a Pin-Fin experiment in forced convection, the following data were recorded: voltage across heater = 60 V, current = 0.9 A, ambient temperature = 30 °C, base temperature = 90 °C, tip temperature = 55 °C, mid temperature = 72 °C, air velocity at test section = 4 m/s, fin material = aluminum ($k = 205$ W/m·K), $D = 12$ mm, $L = 120$ mm. Determine the convective coefficient $h$ and the Reynolds number $Re$. Given: $\mu_{air} = 1.85 \times 10^{-5}$ kg/m·s, $\rho_{air} = 1.15$ kg/m³, $k_{air} = 0.026$ W/m·K. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.B(a) — Experimental Setup (7 marks):**

**(i) Natural Convection Mode (3 marks):**

Components:
- Heater (electrical resistance coil) embedded in a **brass base block**.
- Cylindrical **pin fin** (Al/Brass) mounted vertically on the base.
- Multiple **K-type thermocouples** fixed along the fin length (at $x = 0$, $L/4$, $L/2$, $3L/4$, $L$).
- **Ambient thermocouple** placed away from the fin.
- **Voltmeter** and **ammeter** to measure heater input power.
- **Digital temperature indicator** for all thermocouples.
- **Variac** (variable autotransformer) to regulate heater voltage.

Procedure: With blower OFF, set a heater voltage, wait for steady state (~30–45 min), record all temperatures and V, I.

**(ii) Forced Convection Mode (2 marks):**

Additional components:
- **Centrifugal blower** with a **gate valve / damper** to control flow rate.
- **Honeycomb flow straightener** at the inlet of the test section to ensure uniform flow.
- **Pitot-static tube** + **U-tube manometer** or **digital manometer** to measure air velocity.
- **Outlet thermocouple** at the exit of the test section to measure exit air temperature.

Procedure: Same as natural convection, but the blower is switched ON at a chosen damper setting.

**Tabulated Observations (2 marks):**

| Column | Quantity |
|---|---|
| 1 | Heater voltage $V$ (V) |
| 2 | Heater current $I$ (A) |
| 3 | Air velocity $V_{air}$ (m/s) |
| 4 | $T_1$ at $x = 0$ (°C) |
| 5 | $T_2$ at $x = L/4$ (°C) |
| 6 | $T_3$ at $x = L/2$ (°C) |
| 7 | $T_4$ at $x = 3L/4$ (°C) |
| 8 | $T_5$ at $x = L$ (°C) |
| 9 | $T_\infty$ ambient (°C) |
| 10 | $T_{out}$ exit air (°C) — forced mode only |

---

**Solution to Q.B(b) — Forced Convection Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Heat supplied by heater (1 mark):**
$Q_{in} = V \cdot I = 60 \times 0.9 = 54$ W

**Step 2 — Excess temperatures (1 mark):**
$\theta_b = 90 - 30 = 60$ K
$\theta_{tip} = 55 - 30 = 25$ K

**Step 3 — Temperature profile check (1 mark):**
Theoretical: $\dfrac{\theta_{tip}}{\theta_b} = \dfrac{1}{\cosh(mL)}$
Observed: $\dfrac{25}{60} = 0.4167$
Therefore $\cosh(mL) = \dfrac{1}{0.4167} = 2.400$
$mL = \cosh^{-1}(2.400) = 1.5225$

**Step 4 — Fin parameter and $h$ (2 marks):**
$mL = 1.5225$, $L = 0.120$ m, so $m = 12.69$ m⁻¹
$P = \pi D = \pi \times 0.012 = 0.0377$ m
$A_c = \dfrac{\pi (0.012)^2}{4} = 1.131 \times 10^{-4}$ m²
$k_{Al} = 205$ W/m·K
$h = \dfrac{m^2 k A_c}{P} = \dfrac{(12.69)^2 \times 205 \times 1.131 \times 10^{-4}}{0.0377}$
$h = \dfrac{161.0 \times 205 \times 1.131 \times 10^{-4}}{0.0377} = \dfrac{3.733}{0.0377} = 99.0$ W/m²·K

**Step 5 — Reynolds number (2 marks):**
$Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \dfrac{1.15 \times 4 \times 0.012}{1.85 \times 10^{-5}} = \dfrac{0.0552}{1.85 \times 10^{-5}} = 2984$

**Step 6 — Nusselt number (bonus verification):**
$Nu = \dfrac{hD}{k_{air}} = \dfrac{99.0 \times 0.012}{0.026} = 45.69$

For $Re \approx 3000$ in cross-flow over a cylinder, standard Hilpert correlation gives $Nu \approx 80-100$. Our value is in the **same order of magnitude**, validating the experiment.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert mm → m.** A diameter of 12 mm must be entered as 0.012 m, *not* 12. This single mistake makes every subsequent $m$, $h$, $Re$ value wrong. **−2 to −3 marks.**
> 2. **Mixing $k_{fin}$ and $k_{air}$.** The fin's $k$ (e.g., aluminum = 205) is used in $m$ and $\eta_{fin}$. The air's $k$ (≈ 0.026) is used *only* in $Nu$. **−1 mark.**
> 3. **Using the wrong fin geometry formula.** Pin fin is circular: $P = \pi D$, $A_c = \pi D^2/4$. Do *not* use rectangular formulas. **−2 marks.**
> 4. **Energy Balance Violation.** In an ideal experiment, $Q_{in} = Q_{loss}$. If students don't check this, they lose the **energy balance verification** mark. **−1 mark.**
> 5. **Forgetting to record $T_\infty$.** Without $T_\infty$, you cannot compute $\theta_b$, and the entire problem collapses. **−3 marks minimum.**

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST — Pin-Fin Experiment**

- ✅ **Pin Fin** = cylindrical extended surface; purpose = increase convective surface area.
- ✅ Two conduction regimes: **insulated tip** (most common KTU assumption) and **convection tip**.
- ✅ **1-D Fin ODE:** $\dfrac{d^2\theta}{dx^2} - m^2 \theta = 0$, where $m^2 = \dfrac{hP}{kA_c}$.
- ✅ **Temperature profile (insulated tip):** $\theta(x) = \theta_b \dfrac{\cosh[m(L-x)]}{\cosh(mL)}$.
- ✅ **Heat dissipated:** $Q_{fin} = \sqrt{hPkA_c}\;\theta_b\;\tanh(mL)$.
- ✅ **Efficiency:** $\eta_{fin} = \dfrac{\tanh(mL)}{mL}$ (always < 1).
- ✅ **Effectiveness:** $\varepsilon_{fin} = \tanh(mL)\sqrt{\dfrac{kP}{hA_c}}$; **must be ≥ 2** for a worthwhile fin.
- ✅ **Natural convection** dominated by $Gr$; **Forced convection** dominated by $Re$.
- ✅ Always **convert mm → m** before any calculation.
- ✅ Always **distinguish** $k_{fin}$ (solid material) and $k_{air}$ (fluid).
- ✅ **Energy balance check:** $Q_{in} = VI \approx Q_{fin} + Q_{bare}$ at steady state.
- ✅ Common experimental correlations to memorize:
  - Natural (vertical cylinder, Churchill-Chu): $Nu = \left\{0.60 + \dfrac{0.387 (GrPr)^{1/6}}{\left[1 + (0.559/Pr)^{9/16}\right]^{8/27}}\right\}^2$
  - Forced (Hilpert for cylinder): $Nu = C Re^m Pr^{0.37}$
- ✅ KTU Viva favourites:
  1. "Why does temperature drop along the fin?" → Because heat is progressively lost to air by convection.
  2. "Why is $\varepsilon_{fin} \geq 2$ the design rule?" → Because the fin material cost must be justified by at least doubling heat transfer.
  3. "What happens when you increase air velocity?" → $Re$ ↑, $h$ ↑, $mL$ ↑, $\eta_{fin}$ ↓, but $Q_{fin}$ ↑↑.
- ✅ **Pitfall to avoid**: Do not equate $h$ of natural and forced convection; they differ by an order of magnitude.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Stefan-Boltzmann Constant
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAGL508 - HEAT ENGINES LABORATORY LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Determination of Stefan-Boltzmann Constant # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Stefan-Boltzmann Law (KTU Standard Definition):** *The total radiant heat energy emitted per unit time per unit area of a blackbody is directly proportional to the fourth power of its absolute thermodynamic temperature.* Mathematically expressed as: $$\frac{E}{A} = \sigma T^{4}$$ Where: - **E** = Total energy radiated per unit time (Watts, W) - **A** = Surface area of the emitting body (m²) - **σ** = **Stefan-Boltzmann Constant = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴** - **T** = Absolute temperature of the blackbody (Kelvin, K) For a **real (grey) body**, which is not a perfect blackbody, the law is modified using **emissivity (ε)**: $$P = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, A \, T^{4}$$ Where **ε** (dimensionless, $0 \le \varepsilon \le 1$) is the emissivity of the surface material. For a **tungsten filament** at temperatures between 1500 K – 2500 K, ε ≈ **0.3**. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition > [!NOTE] > **"Why T to the power 4?" — The Campfire Analogy** Imagine standing near a campfire. When the fire is mildly warm (say 500 K / 227 °C), you barely feel heat. But when it blazes red-hot (say 1000 K / 727 °C), the heat is **not twice as intense** — it is **16 times more intense**! That is the magic of the **fourth power**. | Temperature | Relative Heat Radiated | |:-----------:|:----------------------:| | T = 500 K | 1× | | T = 1000 K | **16× (2⁴)** | | T = 1500 K | **81× (3⁴)** | | T = 2000 K | **256× (4⁴)** | **Everyday Engineering Examples:** - 🔥 **Incandescent bulb filament:** A tungsten filament at ~2800 K glows white-hot because of Stefan-Boltzmann radiation. - 🌡️ **Infrared thermometers & thermal cameras:** They detect emitted radiation and back-calculate temperature using σT⁴. - 🛰️ **Satellite thermal design:** Engineers size radiators on spacecraft using σT⁴ to reject waste heat into deep space (3 K). - ☀️ **Solar constant calculation:** The Sun's surface temperature (~5778 K) was historically estimated using σT⁴. --- ## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants (Board-Exam Essential) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorize These Constants for KTU Lab Exam:** > - **Stefan-Boltzmann Constant (σ) = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴** > - **Emissivity of Tungsten (ε) ≈ 0.30** (between 1500–2500 K) > - **Reference resistance temperature for Tungsten: T₀ = 293 K (20 °C)** > - **Tungsten resistance–temperature exponent: n ≈ 1.204** --- ## 1.4 Aim of the Experiment > [!NOTE] > **Aim:** To determine the Stefan-Boltzmann constant experimentally by measuring the radiation emitted from a tungsten filament lamp at different temperatures, and to verify the **fourth-power dependence** of radiated power on absolute temperature. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Underlying Physical Principle A tungsten filament lamp is an excellent laboratory source that approximates a **grey body**. When electric current is passed through the filament, electrical energy is dissipated as heat. In steady state, this electrical power input equals the thermal power radiated by the filament (conduction and convection losses are negligible in vacuum): $$P_{\text{electrical}} = P_{\text{radiated}}$$ $$VI = I^{2}R = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, A \, T^{4}$$ By varying the input voltage, we vary the filament temperature, and hence the radiated power. Measuring V, I, and knowing ε and A allows determination of **σ**. --- ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Experiment 1. **Pass current** through a tungsten filament lamp mounted in a shielded enclosure. 2. **Measure voltage V** across the filament using a voltmeter (in parallel). 3. **Measure current I** through the filament using an ammeter (in series). 4. **Compute resistance** at each operating point: $R = V/I$. 5. **Determine filament temperature T** from the known resistance–temperature relation of tungsten. 6. **Compute radiated power** at each point: $P = VI$. 7. **Plot log P vs. log T** → expect a **straight line of slope ≈ 4**. 8. **Determine σ** from the intercept of the plot. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas required for KTU Lab Exam — Memorize This Table:** | # | Formula | Meaning | Units | |:-:|:--------|:-------|:------| | 1 | $P = VI$ | Electrical power supplied | W | | 2 | $R = V / I$ | Filament resistance at operating temperature | Ω | | 3 | $R_{T} / R_{293} = (T / 293)^{1.204}$ | Tungsten resistance–temperature law | dimensionless | | 4 | $T = 293 \left( R_{T} / R_{293} \right)^{1/1.204}$ | Inverse: temperature from resistance | K | | 5 | $P = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, A \, T^{4}$ | Stefan-Boltzmann law for grey body | W | | 6 | $\log P = \log(\varepsilon \sigma A) + 4 \log T$ | Linear form for graph plotting | — | | 7 | $\sigma = P / (\varepsilon A T^{4})$ | Direct calculation of σ | W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴ | | 8 | $R_{\text{cold}} = V_{\text{min}} / I_{\text{min}}$ | Cold resistance (≈ 293 K reference) | Ω | | 9 | $\% \text{ Error} = \frac{\vert \sigma_{\text{exp}} - \sigma_{\text{std}} \vert}{\sigma_{\text{std}}} \times 100$ | Percentage error formula | % | --- ## 2.4 The Resistance–Temperature Relation for Tungsten Tungsten does **not** obey a simple linear R–T law. Over the working range of an incandescent filament (500 K – 3000 K), the relation is approximately a **power law**: $$R_{T} = k \, T^{n} \quad \text{where} \quad n \approx 1.204$$ Rearranging in a normalized form using the room-temperature reference ($T_0 = 293$ K, $R_0 = R_{293}$): $$\frac{R_{T}}{R_{293}} = \left(\frac{T}{293}\right)^{1.204}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why is this relation important?** Because in the experiment we cannot insert a thermocouple into a glowing filament. The only quantity we can easily measure is **resistance**, and we use this empirical R–T relation to back-calculate the **filament temperature T**. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Lamp Design:** Predicting filament life and luminous efficacy. - **Furnace Design:** Sizing heating elements and refractory losses. - **Re-entry Vehicles:** Calculating thermal load on spacecraft heat shields. - **Cryogenic Storage:** Estimating boil-off rate of LNG tanks using σT⁴. - **Power Plant Boilers:** Radiation losses from superheater tubes. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Linear Form of Stefan-Boltzmann Law (for Graph Plotting) **Starting Point:** $$P = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, A \, T^{4}$$ **Step 1:** Take natural logarithm (log₁₀) on both sides: $$\log P = \log(\varepsilon \sigma A) + \log(T^{4})$$ **Step 2:** Apply the power rule of logarithms: $\log(T^{4}) = 4 \log T$ $$\log P = 4 \log T + \log(\varepsilon \sigma A)$$ **Step 3:** Compare with the equation of a straight line $y = mx + c$: $$\underbrace{\log P}_{y} = \underbrace{4}_{m} \cdot \underbrace{\log T}_{x} + \underbrace{\log(\varepsilon \sigma A)}_{c}$$ **Conclusion:** A plot of **log P (y-axis) vs. log T (x-axis)** must yield a **straight line with slope = 4** and y-intercept = $\log(\varepsilon \sigma A)$. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Determination of σ from Graph Intercept Once the intercept $c = \log(\varepsilon \sigma A)$ is obtained from the graph: **Step 1:** Take antilog: $$10^{c} = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, A$$ **Step 2:** Solve for σ: $$\sigma = \frac{10^{c}}{\varepsilon \, A}$$ **Step 3:** Compare with standard value and compute percentage error: $$\% \text{ Error} = \frac{\vert \sigma_{\text{experimental}} - \sigma_{\text{standard}} \vert}{\sigma_{\text{standard}}} \times 100$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Temperature from Resistance (Inverse Relation) **Starting with:** $$\frac{R_{T}}{R_{0}} = \left(\frac{T}{T_{0}}\right)^{n} \quad \text{where } n = 1.204, \; T_{0} = 293 \, \text{K}$$ **Step 1:** Raise both sides to the power $1/n$: $$\left(\frac{R_{T}}{R_{0}}\right)^{1/n} = \frac{T}{T_{0}}$$ **Step 2:** Solve for T: $$T = T_{0} \left(\frac{R_{T}}{R_{0}}\right)^{1/n}$$ **Substituting numerical values** $T_0 = 293$ K and $1/n = 1/1.204 \approx 0.8306$: $$T = 293 \times \left(\frac{R_{T}}{R_{0}}\right)^{0.8306} \quad \text{(in Kelvin)}$$ --- ## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Pattern) > [!NOTE] > **Sample Question:** A tungsten filament lamp has cold resistance $R_{293} = 50 \, \Omega$. When operated at 100 V it draws 0.5 A. Given filament area $A = 3 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^{2}$ and emissivity $\varepsilon = 0.3$, calculate (a) filament temperature, (b) value of σ, (c) percentage error. ### Part (a): Filament Temperature **Step 1:** Hot resistance: $$R_{T} = \frac{V}{I} = \frac{100}{0.5} = 200 \, \Omega$$ **Step 2:** Resistance ratio: $$\frac{R_{T}}{R_{0}} = \frac{200}{50} = 4.0$$ **Step 3:** Apply the temperature relation: $$T = 293 \times (4.0)^{0.8306}$$ **Step 4:** Evaluate the power: $$4.0^{0.8306} = e^{0.8306 \times \ln 4} = e^{0.8306 \times 1.3863} = e^{1.1514} \approx 3.163$$ **Step 5:** Final temperature: $$T = 293 \times 3.163 \approx 927 \, \text{K} \quad (\approx 654 \, ^{\circ}\text{C})$$ ### Part (b): Stefan-Boltzmann Constant **Step 1:** Radiated power: $$P = VI = 100 \times 0.5 = 50 \, \text{W}$$ **Step 2:** Apply Stefan-Boltzmann law: $$\sigma = \frac{P}{\varepsilon \, A \, T^{4}}$$ **Step 3:** Calculate $T^4$: $$T^{4} = 927^{4} = 7.39 \times 10^{11} \, \text{K}^{4}$$ **Step 4:** Substitute all values: $$\sigma = \frac{50}{0.3 \times (3 \times 10^{-5}) \times (7.39 \times 10^{11})}$$ $$\sigma = \frac{50}{0.3 \times 2.217 \times 10^{7}} = \frac{50}{6.65 \times 10^{6}}$$ $$\boxed{\sigma = 7.52 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{W·m}^{-2}\text{·K}^{-4}}$$ ### Part (c): Percentage Error $$\% \text{ Error} = \frac{\vert 7.52 \times 10^{-6} - 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \vert}{5.67 \times 10^{-8}} \times 100$$ **Observation:** The experimental value is far off. This indicates an assumption error — likely **ε = 0.3 was wrong** for this temperature regime, OR the filament area was approximated incorrectly. In practice, KTU expects students to **compute σ assuming the formula holds with given ε**, and present the result with a reasonable error margin (5–20%). > [!WARNING] > **Real KTU Lab Note:** Values within 10–20% of $5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ are considered **acceptable**. Higher deviations are discussed in the viva as arising from: (i) ε varies with temperature, (ii) filament area changes due to thermal expansion, (iii) neglect of end-loss radiation. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Reading the Graph | Graph Feature | Expected Value | What It Confirms | |:--------------|:---------------|:-----------------| | Slope of log P vs log T | ≈ 4.0 | **Fourth-power law verified** | | Intercept $c = \log(\varepsilon \sigma A)$ | Used to find σ | Stefan-Boltzmann law validated | | Linearity (R²) | > 0.98 | Filament behaves as grey body | | y-value at log T = 3 | $\log(\varepsilon \sigma A) + 12$ | Numerical consistency check | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram of the Experimental Setup ```mermaid flowchart LR A[AC Variac
0-250V] --> B[Ammeter A
0-1A] B --> C[Tungsten Filament Lamp
Enclosed in Shielded Box] C --> D[Voltmeter V
0-300V] D --> A E[Observer reads
V and I] --> F[Calculate
R = V/I] F --> G[Look up
Temperature T from
R-T Calibration] G --> H[Compute
P = V*I] H --> I[Plot log P
vs log T] I --> J[Find slope and
intercept] J --> K[Calculate
Stefan-Boltzmann
Constant sigma] ``` ## 4.2 Functional Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Step 1: Setup] --> P2[Step 2: Cold Resistance R0 at 293 K] P2 --> P3[Step 3: Apply Voltage via Variac] P3 --> P4[Step 4: Record V and I] P4 --> P5[Step 5: Compute R = V/I] P5 --> P6[Step 6: Find T using R-T relation] P6 --> P7[Step 7: Compute P = V*I] P7 --> P8[Step 8: Tabulate log P and log T] P8 --> P9[Step 9: Plot log P vs log T] P9 --> P10[Step 10: Slope should be ~4] P10 --> P11[Step 11: Compute sigma from intercept] P11 --> P12[Step 12: Calculate Percentage Error] ``` ## 4.3 Circuit Schematic Representation ```mermaid graph LR SRC[Variac
AC Source 0-250V] --- AMM[Ammeter A] AMM --- LAMP[Tungsten Filament
Lamp] LAMP --- VOLT[Voltmeter V] VOLT --- SRC VOLT2[Voltmeter parallel
to Lamp] -.Measures V.-> LAMP ``` ## 4.4 Block-Level Data Processing Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR R0[Cold Resistance
R0 at 293K] --> TBL[(Resistance
Temperature
Calibration Table)] RT[Hot Resistance
R = V/I] --> TBL TBL --> TEMP[Filament
Temperature T in K] PWR[P = V*I] --> LG1[log P] TEMP --> LG2[log T] LG2 --> PLOT[Plot
log P vs log T] LG1 --> PLOT PLOT --> SLOPE[Slope m approx 4] PLOT --> INTC[Intercept c = log epsilon sigma A] INTC --> SIGMA[sigma = 10 to power c / epsilon A] SLOPE --> VERIFY[Verify Fourth
Power Law] SIGMA --> ERR[Percentage
Error] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **State Stefan-Boltzmann Law and write its mathematical form. Mention the significance of the constant.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > *Stefan-Boltzmann Law states that the total radiant energy emitted per unit time per unit area of a blackbody is directly proportional to the fourth power of its absolute temperature.* The mathematical form is: > > $$E/A = \sigma T^{4}$$ > > The constant σ is called the **Stefan-Boltzmann constant** with value **5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴**. It is fundamental in calculating heat transfer by radiation in furnaces, lamps, and solar systems. --- ### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Why is tungsten used as the filament material in this experiment? Mention two reasons.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > Tungsten is used because: *(i)* it has an **extremely high melting point (3695 K)**, allowing it to be heated to high temperatures without melting, and *(ii)* its **resistance varies systematically with temperature** following the empirical law $R_T / R_{293} = (T/293)^{1.204}$, which enables accurate determination of filament temperature from resistance measurements. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Derive the linear form of Stefan-Boltzmann law suitable for graphical verification. Explain how the slope and intercept are used to determine σ. **(7 marks)** **(b)** In an experiment, a tungsten filament of cold resistance 40 Ω and surface area $4 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^2$ draws 0.6 A at 90 V. Given ε = 0.3, calculate (i) the filament temperature, (ii) the experimental value of σ, and (iii) the percentage error. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Derivation (7 marks):** Starting from $P = \varepsilon \sigma A T^{4}$, taking log on both sides: $$\log P = \log(\varepsilon \sigma A) + 4 \log T$$ Comparing with $y = mx + c$: slope $m = 4$ and intercept $c = \log(\varepsilon \sigma A)$. From intercept: $$\sigma = \frac{10^{c}}{\varepsilon A}$$ The slope verifies the fourth-power dependence, while σ is calculated from the intercept using known ε and A. **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Step 1:** Hot resistance: $$R = V/I = 90 / 0.6 = 150 \, \Omega$$ **Step 2:** Temperature: $$T = 293 \times \left(\frac{150}{40}\right)^{1/1.204} = 293 \times (3.75)^{0.8306}$$ $$3.75^{0.8306} = e^{0.8306 \times \ln 3.75} = e^{0.8306 \times 1.3218} = e^{1.0978} \approx 2.998$$ $$T = 293 \times 2.998 \approx 878.4 \, \text{K}$$ **Step 3:** Power: $$P = VI = 90 \times 0.6 = 54 \, \text{W}$$ **Step 4:** σ: $$\sigma = \frac{P}{\varepsilon A T^{4}} = \frac{54}{0.3 \times 4 \times 10^{-5} \times (878.4)^{4}}$$ $$T^4 = (878.4)^4 \approx 5.95 \times 10^{11}$$ $$\sigma = \frac{54}{0.3 \times 4 \times 10^{-5} \times 5.95 \times 10^{11}} = \frac{54}{7.14 \times 10^{6}}$$ $$\boxed{\sigma_{\text{exp}} = 7.56 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{W·m}^{-2}\text{·K}^{-4}}$$ **Step 5:** Percentage error: $$\% \text{ Error} = \frac{\vert 7.56 \times 10^{-6} - 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \vert}{5.67 \times 10^{-8}} \times 100 \approx \text{large}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students commonly lose marks by **(1)** forgetting to convert cold resistance temperature (293 K) explicitly, **(2)** using the wrong exponent (1.2 instead of 1/1.204), and **(3)** computing $T^4$ without intermediate steps. **Always show the calculation of $T^4$ as a separate step** to earn full method marks. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Describe the apparatus used for the determination of Stefan-Boltzmann constant with a neat block diagram. List the observations recorded and the formulae used. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The following readings were obtained in a Stefan-Boltzmann experiment. Plot log P vs log T and find σ. | V (V) | I (A) | |:-----:|:-----:| | 20 | 0.20 | | 40 | 0.30 | | 60 | 0.38 | | 80 | 0.46 | | 100 | 0.52 | Given: cold resistance $R_0 = 30 \, \Omega$, $A = 2 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^2$, $\varepsilon = 0.3$. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Apparatus and Procedure (7 marks):** **Apparatus:** Variac (0–250 V AC), ammeter (0–1 A), voltmeter (0–300 V), tungsten filament lamp housed in a shielded enclosure, connecting wires. **Procedure:** 1. Connect the circuit as shown in the block diagram. 2. Set variac to minimum, record cold resistance $R_0 = V/I$. 3. Gradually increase voltage in steps; record V and I at each step. 4. Calculate $R = V/I$ and find T from R-T relation. 5. Calculate $P = VI$ and tabulate. 6. Plot **log P vs log T** and find slope (should be ≈ 4) and intercept. 7. Compute σ from intercept. **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Step 1:** Tabulate R, T, P, log P, log T: | V (V) | I (A) | R = V/I (Ω) | T = 293(R/30)^0.8306 (K) | P = VI (W) | log T | log P | |:-----:|:-----:|:-----------:|:------------------------:|:----------:|:-----:|:-----:| | 20 | 0.20 | 100 | 293 × (3.33)^0.8306 ≈ 884 | 4.0 | 2.946 | 0.602 | | 40 | 0.30 | 133.3 | 293 × (4.44)^0.8306 ≈ 1075| 12.0 | 3.031 | 1.079 | | 60 | 0.38 | 157.9 | 293 × (5.26)^0.8306 ≈ 1208| 22.8 | 3.082 | 1.358 | | 80 | 0.46 | 173.9 | 293 × (5.80)^0.8306 ≈ 1289| 36.8 | 3.110 | 1.566 | | 100 | 0.52 | 192.3 | 293 × (6.41)^0.8306 ≈ 1379| 52.0 | 3.139 | 1.716 | **Step 2:** Best-fit slope: $$\text{slope} = \frac{\Delta \log P}{\Delta \log T} = \frac{1.716 - 0.602}{3.139 - 2.946} = \frac{1.114}{0.193} \approx 5.77$$ (Acceptable slopes are typically between 3.5 and 4.5 in real experiments.) **Step 3:** Intercept (extrapolating to log T = 0): $$c = \log P - 4 \log T = 0.602 - 4 \times 2.946 = 0.602 - 11.784 = -11.182$$ $$10^{c} = 10^{-11.182} = 6.58 \times 10^{-12}$$ **Step 4:** Calculate σ: $$\sigma = \frac{10^{c}}{\varepsilon A} = \frac{6.58 \times 10^{-12}}{0.3 \times 2 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{6.58 \times 10^{-12}}{6 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$\boxed{\sigma_{\text{exp}} \approx 1.10 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{W·m}^{-2}\text{·K}^{-4}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** The most common mistake in this question is **forgetting to use absolute temperature (Kelvin)** when computing $T^4$. Students frequently plug in °C values and get absurdly wrong σ. **Always convert to Kelvin first.** Second most common error: using **log₁₀ vs ln** inconsistently — KTU standard is **log₁₀ (base 10)** for graphical plots. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Fire Revision — Stefan-Boltzmann Experiment** - ✅ **Stefan-Boltzmann Law:** $P = \varepsilon \sigma A T^{4}$ — power radiated is proportional to **T⁴**. - ✅ **σ standard value:** **5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴**. - ✅ **Emissivity of tungsten (ε):** ≈ **0.30** (assume constant in KTU problems). - ✅ **Filament temperature reference:** $T_0 = 293$ K (room temperature). - ✅ **Tungsten R-T law:** $R_T / R_{293} = (T / 293)^{1.204}$, exponent $n = 1.204$. - ✅ **Inverse formula:** $T = 293 \times (R_T / R_{293})^{1/1.204}$. - ✅ **Graph:** log P (y-axis) vs log T (x-axis) → slope **= 4**, intercept = log(εσA). - ✅ **Procedure mantra:** Measure V → Compute R = V/I → Find T from R-T law → Compute P = VI → Plot log P vs log T → Verify slope ≈ 4 → Find σ. - ✅ **Always convert °C to K** before computing $T^4$. - ✅ **Acceptable experimental error:** 5–20% deviation from standard σ is normal; discuss causes in viva (ε variation with T, filament expansion, end losses). - ✅ **Apparatus to draw in viva:** Variac + Ammeter (series) + Filament Lamp + Voltmeter (parallel) — closed loop. - ✅ **Common viva question:** *"Why use a tungsten filament and not a nichrome wire?"* → Because tungsten's melting point (3695 K) is much higher, allowing access to high T where radiation dominates.

topics.title: Determination of Emissivity of a Grey Surface
Effectiveness of Parallel/Counter Flow Heat Exchanger
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGL508 - HEAT ENGINES LABORATORY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Effectiveness of Parallel/Counter Flow Heat Exchanger

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **heat exchanger** is a process equipment designed to transfer thermal energy between two or more fluids at different temperatures, with the streams separated by a solid wall (recuperative type) or by direct contact (regenerative type). The *effectiveness* of a heat exchanger ($\varepsilon$) is defined as the ratio of the **actual heat transfer rate ($Q_{actual}$)** to the **maximum possible heat transfer rate ($Q_{max}$)** that could be achieved under the operating conditions.

$$ \varepsilon = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{max}} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (as per RBT Levels L1–L3):** Effectiveness is the dimensionless performance index of a heat exchanger indicating how closely it approaches thermodynamic perfection for a given fluid inlet temperatures and mass flow rates.

In the context of **Module Experiment 2** of *PCAGL508 – Heat Engines Laboratory*, the student is expected to experimentally determine the effectiveness of:
- **(a) Parallel Flow (Co-current) Heat Exchanger** – both fluids enter at the same end and travel in the same direction.
- **(b) Counter Flow (Counter-current) Heat Exchanger** – fluids enter at opposite ends and travel in opposite directions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy: The plumbing in your home geyser.**
> Imagine two pipes of water touching each other. One is boiling hot from the heating coil, the other is cold tap water. If both pipes have water flowing in the same direction (**parallel**), the hot water cools as it gives up heat, but the cold water that meets the "cooler" hot water at the outlet never gets the chance to reach a high temperature. The system hits a *thermal equilibrium* far from what we want.
> 
> If we instead reverse the cold water to enter from the opposite end (**counter flow**), the coldest cold water meets the nearly-cooled hot water at the exit, while the hottest hot water meets the warming cold water at the inlet. The temperature "driving force" remains high throughout the length. This is why **counter-flow heat exchangers are universally used in power plants, AC systems, and oil refineries** – they deliver higher effectiveness for the *same* surface area.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following quantities are foundational to the experiment:

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|
| Specific heat capacity of water | $c_p$ | kJ/(kg·K) |
| Density of water | $\rho$ | kg/m³ |
| Mass flow rate | $\dot{m}$ | kg/s |
| Heat transfer rate | $Q$ | W or kW |
| Log Mean Temperature Difference | $\Delta T_{LM}$ | K or °C |
| Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient | $U$ | W/(m²·K) |
| Effectiveness | $\varepsilon$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| Heat Capacity Rate | $C = \dot{m} c_p$ | W/K |

> [!TIP]
> **Standard value to remember:** $c_p$ of water = **4.186 kJ/(kg·K)** = 4186 J/(kg·K). Density of water $\approx$ **1000 kg/m³**. These will be used in almost every KTU numerical.

---

## 1.4 Types of Heat Exchangers Studied in the Lab

The PCAGL508 apparatus in the KTU-affiliated engineering colleges (Government / Aided / Self-financing) typically contains a **double-pipe concentric tube heat exchanger** that can be reconfigured:

1. **Parallel Flow Configuration** – Hot fluid inlet and Cold fluid inlet on the same side.
2. **Counter Flow Configuration** – Hot fluid inlet and Cold fluid inlet on opposite sides.

The hot fluid is usually **hot water from a heater**, and the cold fluid is **chilled water from a reservoir**. Both fluids have thermocouples / RTD sensors at the inlets and outlets, and rotameters for flow-rate measurement.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operating Principle & Heat Transfer Mechanism

The heat transfer from the hot fluid to the cold fluid across the tube wall follows the three-mode conduction–convection–conduction sequence:

1. **Convective heat transfer** from hot bulk fluid to inner tube wall.
2. **Conductive heat transfer** through the tube wall (steel / copper).
3. **Convective heat transfer** from outer tube wall to cold bulk fluid.

The overall process is governed by **Newton's Law of Cooling** integrated over the exchanger surface:

$$ Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LM} $$

Where:
- $Q$ = heat transfer rate (W)
- $U$ = overall heat transfer coefficient (W/m²K)
- $A$ = effective heat transfer area (m²)
- $\Delta T_{LM}$ = Log Mean Temperature Difference (K)

## 2.2 Why Log Mean Temperature Difference?

Because the temperature difference $\Delta T$ between the two fluids **changes continuously** along the length of the heat exchanger, a simple arithmetic mean is **inaccurate** and would over-predict $Q$ for parallel flow and under-predict for counter flow. The rigorous integration of a differential element gives the **LMTD** form.

### 2.2.1 LMTD Formula

$$ \Delta T_{LM} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln\left( \dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2} \right)} $$

Where:
- $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,i}$ (temperature difference at the inlet)
- $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,o}$ (temperature difference at the outlet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For Counter-Flow Exchanger**, the formula is **algebraically different** because $\Delta T_2$ is defined at the *opposite* end:
> - $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o}$
> - $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,i}$

## 2.3 Concept of Effectiveness – The "Why"

Effectiveness answers the engineering question: **"Out of the maximum heat that *could possibly* be transferred, how much did our exchanger actually transfer?"**

The maximum possible heat transfer is limited by the **smaller heat capacity rate** ($C_{min}$) of the two fluids, because the fluid with the smaller thermal capacity rate will reach the *other fluid's inlet temperature* first (in an infinitely long counter-flow exchanger).

$$ Q_{max} = C_{min} \cdot (T_{h,i} - T_{c,i}) $$

Where:
- $C_{min} = \min(\dot{m}_h c_{p,h},\, \dot{m}_c c_{p,c})$
- $C_{max} = \max(\dot{m}_h c_{p,h},\, \dot{m}_c c_{p,c})$
- Heat capacity ratio $C_r = C_{min} / C_{max}$

## 2.4 NTU–Effectiveness Relationship

The **Number of Transfer Units (NTU)** is a dimensionless parameter:

$$ NTU = \frac{U \cdot A}{C_{min}} $$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Equation | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Heat transfer rate (hot fluid side) | $Q_h = \dot{m}_h c_{p,h}(T_{h,i} - T_{h,o})$ | From measured temperatures and flow |
| 2 | Heat transfer rate (cold fluid side) | $Q_c = \dot{m}_c c_{p,c}(T_{c,o} - T_{c,i})$ | Cross-verification with $Q_h$ |
| 3 | Mean experimental $Q$ | $Q_{avg} = (Q_h + Q_c)/2$ | Reduces measurement error |
| 4 | Log Mean Temperature Difference | $\Delta T_{LM} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Both parallel & counter flow |
| 5 | Overall heat transfer coefficient | $U = \dfrac{Q}{A \cdot \Delta T_{LM}}$ | Performance metric |
| 6 | Heat capacity rates | $C_h = \dot{m}_h c_{p,h}$, $C_c = \dot{m}_c c_{p,c}$ | Identify $C_{min}$, $C_{max}$ |
| 7 | Maximum possible heat transfer | $Q_{max} = C_{min}(T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})$ | Theoretical upper limit |
| 8 | **Effectiveness** | $\varepsilon = Q_{actual} / Q_{max}$ | Primary result of experiment |
| 9 | Number of Transfer Units | $NTU = U A / C_{min}$ | For cross-verification |
| 10 | Heat capacity ratio | $C_r = C_{min} / C_{max}$ | Used in $\varepsilon$–NTU charts |
| 11 | Effectiveness (Counter-flow, $C_r \ne 1$) | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp[-NTU(1 - C_r)]}{1 - C_r \exp[-NTU(1 - C_r)]}$ | Theoretical comparison |
| 12 | Effectiveness (Counter-flow, $C_r = 1$) | $\varepsilon = NTU / (1 + NTU)$ | Special balanced case |
| 13 | Effectiveness (Parallel flow) | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp[-NTU(1 + C_r)]}{1 + C_r}$ | Co-current configuration |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's hint:** For a *balanced* heat exchanger where both fluids have equal $C$ (water on both sides, equal flow rates), the counter-flow $\varepsilon$ simplifies to equation 12, which is the most commonly tested variant in KTU 2024 scheme lab viva questions.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| Power generation | Condensers and feedwater heaters in Rankine cycle |
| HVAC | Chilled-water coils in air-handling units (AHUs) |
| Petroleum | Crude preheaters before distillation column |
| Chemical | Reactor effluent coolers |
| Automotive | Engine oil coolers, EGR coolers |
| Cryogenics | LNG vaporizers using seawater |

> [!NOTE]
> The decision between parallel and counter flow is governed by trade-offs: **counter flow** has higher effectiveness and uniform wall temperature; **parallel flow** has a built-in temperature *crossover* protection (the outlet of the cold fluid can never exceed the outlet of the hot fluid) — useful in **exothermic reactor cooling** where runaway reactions must be avoided.
